questions.question: A startup needs to deploy a stateless HTTP API that handles variable traffic with spikes up to 10x normal load. The team wants minimal operational overhead and automatic scaling to zero during off-hours. Which compute option best fits these requirements?
options: Cloud Run
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Autopilot
App Engine Flexible
Managed Instance Group with autoscaling
questions.explanation: Cloud Run is ideal for stateless HTTP workloads requiring scale-to-zero and minimal ops overhead. It scales automatically from zero to thousands of instances within seconds. GKE Autopilot still requires cluster management and does not scale to zero. App Engine Flexible does not scale to zero and has slower cold starts. Managed Instance Groups require more configuration and do not scale to zero without additional orchestration.

questions.question: A company runs a globally distributed e-commerce application that requires single-digit millisecond latency for database reads and writes across multiple regions, with strong consistency. Which database should they choose?
options: Cloud Spanner
Cloud SQL with read replicas
Firestore in Native mode
Bigtable with replication
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner provides globally distributed, strongly consistent, horizontally scalable relational database with single-digit millisecond latencies. Cloud SQL with read replicas offers eventual consistency for reads and does not scale globally. Firestore provides strong consistency within a region but is document-based, not relational, and has higher latencies globally. Bigtable is NoSQL and provides eventual consistency with replication.

questions.question: Your organization stores 500 TB of compliance data that must be retained for 7 years and accessed at most once per year for audits. Cost minimization is the top priority. Which Cloud Storage class should you use?
options: Archive
Coldline
Nearline
Standard
questions.explanation: Archive storage is designed for data accessed less than once per year, with the lowest storage cost ($0.0012/GB/month) and a 365-day minimum storage duration. Coldline is for data accessed at most once per quarter ($0.004/GB/month, 90-day minimum). Nearline is for data accessed at most once per month. Standard is for frequently accessed data. Since audit access is at most annual, Archive is most cost-effective.

questions.question: An enterprise needs a disaster recovery solution for their on-premises ERP system with an RPO of 4 hours and an RTO of 1 hour. Which DR strategy best meets these requirements while minimizing cost?
options: Warm standby with periodic data replication every 4 hours
Active-active multi-region deployment
Backup and restore with daily snapshots to Cloud Storage
Pilot light with minimal infrastructure in GCP
questions.explanation: Warm standby with 4-hour replication meets the RPO of 4 hours, and because a scaled-down but running environment exists, failover within 1 hour is achievable for the RTO. Active-active exceeds requirements and costs more. Backup and restore with daily snapshots would violate the 4-hour RPO. Pilot light requires more time to scale up resources, potentially exceeding the 1-hour RTO.

questions.question: A company wants to connect their on-premises data center to Google Cloud with consistent 10 Gbps throughput, low latency, and SLA-backed reliability. The data center is co-located with a Google-approved colocation facility. Which connectivity option should they use?
options: Dedicated Interconnect
Partner Interconnect
Cloud VPN with HA configuration
Direct Peering
questions.explanation: Dedicated Interconnect provides a direct physical connection to Google's network at speeds of 10 Gbps or 100 Gbps per link, with an SLA and low, consistent latency. It requires co-location at a Google-approved facility. Partner Interconnect is used when direct co-location is not available. Cloud VPN is encrypted over the public internet and cannot guarantee consistent 10 Gbps throughput. Direct Peering is for Google Workspace/YouTube traffic, not general GCP services.

questions.question: Your application serves users in North America, Europe, and Asia. You need a load balancer that routes HTTPS traffic to the nearest healthy backend, supports SSL termination, and provides a single global anycast IP. Which load balancer should you use?
options: Global External Application Load Balancer
Regional External Application Load Balancer
External Network Load Balancer
Internal Application Load Balancer
questions.explanation: The Global External Application Load Balancer (formerly Global HTTP(S) LB) uses Google's global network with a single anycast IP, routes users to the nearest healthy backend, and handles SSL termination. The Regional External Application Load Balancer is confined to a single region. The External Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and does not do SSL termination or HTTP routing. The Internal Application Load Balancer is for private traffic within a VPC.

questions.question: A data engineering team needs to process streaming sensor data from 50,000 IoT devices in real time, apply transformations, and write results to BigQuery. Which pipeline architecture is most appropriate?
options: Pub/Sub ingestion with Dataflow streaming pipeline writing to BigQuery
Pub/Sub ingestion with Dataproc Spark Streaming writing to BigQuery
IoT Core to Cloud Storage, then scheduled Dataflow batch jobs to BigQuery
Direct BigQuery streaming inserts from IoT devices using the Storage Write API
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub provides durable, scalable message ingestion for high-throughput IoT data. Dataflow (Apache Beam) natively supports streaming pipelines with exactly-once semantics and direct BigQuery write connectors. Dataproc Spark Streaming requires managing a cluster and has higher operational overhead. Batching to Cloud Storage then processing adds latency and doesn't qualify as real-time. Direct BigQuery streaming from 50,000 devices would create unmanageable connection overhead and quota issues.

questions.question: An application team uses Cloud Memorystore for Redis as a session cache. After a Redis instance restart, all sessions are lost and users must re-authenticate. Which configuration change would prevent session loss?
options: Enable Redis persistence (RDB snapshots or AOF) on the Memorystore instance
Increase the Memorystore instance tier to Standard
Enable read replicas on the Memorystore instance
Use Memorystore for Memcached instead of Redis
questions.explanation: Memorystore for Redis supports persistence via RDB snapshots and Append-Only File (AOF) modes. Enabling persistence ensures data survives instance restarts. Upgrading to Standard tier provides high availability with a replica but does not persist data across restarts. Read replicas improve read performance but not durability. Memcached does not support persistence at all.

questions.question: A financial services company needs to migrate a legacy monolith to microservices on GCP. The monolith uses a single Oracle database. During migration, both the monolith and new microservices must read/write the same data. Which migration pattern should the architect recommend?
options: Strangler Fig pattern with a shared database during transition
Big Bang rewrite with full cutover in a single weekend
Lift-and-shift the monolith to Compute Engine first, then refactor
Database-per-service pattern implemented immediately across all services
questions.explanation: The Strangler Fig pattern incrementally replaces monolith functionality with microservices while both share the database during transition, minimizing risk. Big Bang rewrites carry high risk of failure and downtime. Lift-and-shift delays modernization without enabling microservices decomposition. Database-per-service is the target state but cannot be implemented immediately when services depend on the same data — it requires careful data partitioning that must happen incrementally.

questions.question: A retail company runs analytics on 5 years of transaction data. Queries scan up to 10 TB per run and are executed by 20 analysts simultaneously. They need sub-second response for dashboard queries but cost optimization for ad-hoc analysis. Which BigQuery feature combination should you recommend? Select TWO.
options: BigQuery BI Engine for accelerating dashboard queries
Partitioned and clustered tables to reduce bytes scanned
BigQuery omni for cross-cloud queries
Flat-rate pricing for all queries
questions.explanation: BigQuery BI Engine provides in-memory analysis acceleration for dashboard queries, delivering sub-second response times. Partitioned and clustered tables reduce bytes scanned by pruning irrelevant partitions and clustering blocks, significantly reducing query cost for ad-hoc analysis. BigQuery Omni is for querying data in other clouds, not relevant here. Flat-rate pricing would cost more than on-demand for intermittent ad-hoc usage by 20 analysts.

questions.question: A healthcare company needs to store patient medical images (DICOM files) averaging 200 MB each, with frequent access for the first 30 days after creation, then rare access for up to 10 years. Which Cloud Storage configuration minimizes cost while meeting access patterns?
options: Standard class with a lifecycle rule to transition to Coldline after 30 days
Standard class with a lifecycle rule to transition to Archive after 30 days
Nearline class for all objects with no lifecycle rules
Standard class with a lifecycle rule to transition to Nearline after 30 days, then Coldline after 90 days
questions.explanation: The optimal approach transitions from Standard (frequent access, first 30 days) to Nearline (occasional access, days 30-90) to Coldline (rare access, after 90 days). Going directly to Archive after 30 days would incur high retrieval costs if accessed occasionally in months 1-3. Nearline for all objects is more expensive during the first 30 days of frequent access. The two-step transition to Nearline then Coldline balances retrieval costs with storage costs across the data lifecycle.

questions.question: Your company is designing a microservices platform where services need to communicate asynchronously. During peak load, a downstream service becomes slow but upstream services must not block. Which pattern and GCP service combination best addresses this?
options: Pub/Sub with push subscriptions and dead-letter topics for failed messages
Cloud Tasks with HTTP target tasks and retry configuration
Synchronous REST calls with Cloud Load Balancing for distribution
gRPC streaming between services over internal VPC
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub with push subscriptions decouples producers from consumers. When the downstream service is slow, messages queue in Pub/Sub without blocking producers. Dead-letter topics capture repeatedly failed messages for investigation. Cloud Tasks is designed for task queues with specific scheduling needs, less suited for event-driven fan-out. Synchronous REST calls would block upstream services when downstream is slow. gRPC streaming is synchronous and would also cause backpressure issues.

questions.question: A company needs a relational database for a new SaaS application expecting 10,000 to 1,000,000 users over 3 years in a single region. The application uses complex SQL joins and transactions. They want managed service with automatic storage scaling. Which database is the best fit?
options: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with high availability
Cloud Spanner single-region instance
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL
Firestore in Datastore mode
questions.explanation: AlloyDB for PostgreSQL is a fully managed, PostgreSQL-compatible database optimized for demanding transactional and analytical workloads, offering up to 4x faster transactional throughput than standard Cloud SQL and 100x faster analytical queries. It automatically scales storage and supports complex SQL. Cloud SQL is suitable but AlloyDB is the better fit for rapid growth to millions of users. Cloud Spanner is designed for globally distributed workloads and is more expensive for single-region use. Firestore does not support complex SQL joins.

questions.question: An organization wants to implement a VPC network design where development, staging, and production environments share networking resources (like Cloud NAT and VPN gateways) but have isolated workloads. Which VPC design pattern should you recommend?
options: Shared VPC with a host project and separate service projects per environment
Three separate VPCs with VPC peering between them
A single VPC with separate subnets per environment and firewall rules
Three separate VPCs with Cloud VPN tunnels between them
questions.explanation: Shared VPC allows a host project to own the VPC network (and shared resources like Cloud NAT, VPN gateways) while service projects (dev, staging, prod) host their workloads in that network with IAM-controlled subnet access. This provides centralized network management with workload isolation. VPC Peering doesn't allow sharing of Cloud NAT or VPN gateways. A single VPC with subnets provides weak isolation — firewall rules can be bypassed. VPN tunnels between separate VPCs add latency, cost, and operational overhead.

questions.question: A company processes 1 million transactions per day using a batch ETL pipeline on Dataproc. The pipeline runs for 2 hours each night. They want to reduce cost. Which approach would provide the greatest cost savings?
options: Use preemptible (Spot) VMs for Dataproc worker nodes
Switch from Dataproc to Dataflow
Increase the number of worker nodes to reduce runtime
Use a persistent Dataproc cluster instead of ephemeral clusters
questions.explanation: Preemptible (Spot) VMs for Dataproc workers cost ~80% less than regular VMs. Since batch ETL jobs can tolerate node preemption (Dataproc restarts failed tasks), this provides the greatest cost reduction. Switching to Dataflow may not reduce cost and adds migration effort. Adding more worker nodes reduces runtime but increases per-hour cost proportionally or worse. Using a persistent cluster means paying for idle time between the 2-hour nightly runs (22 hours per day wasted).

questions.question: A gaming company needs a database to store player profiles with flexible schema, support for nested data structures, real-time sync to mobile clients, and horizontal scaling. Which database should they choose?
options: Firestore in Native mode
Cloud Bigtable
Cloud SQL for MySQL
Cloud Spanner
questions.explanation: Firestore Native mode supports flexible schemas, nested documents/arrays, real-time listeners for client sync (Firebase SDKs), and horizontally scales automatically. Bigtable is designed for time-series and wide-column data, lacks real-time sync, and requires schema design expertise. Cloud SQL is relational with a fixed schema and doesn't support real-time client sync. Cloud Spanner is relational, globally consistent, but has no real-time sync feature and is costlier for this use case.

questions.question: Your organization needs to implement a data lakehouse architecture. Raw data lands in Cloud Storage, is processed by Dataflow, and must be queryable by both data scientists using Python notebooks and business analysts using SQL. Which architecture pattern should you recommend?
options: Write processed data to BigQuery; data scientists use BigQuery notebooks and analysts use BigQuery SQL
Write processed data to Cloud Storage as Parquet; use Dataproc for Python and BigQuery external tables for SQL
Write processed data to Bigtable; use Dataproc for Python notebooks
Write processed data to Cloud SQL; use Vertex AI Workbench for Python and Cloud SQL Studio for SQL
questions.explanation: BigQuery serves as the unified analytics engine: it natively supports SQL for analysts and integrates with Vertex AI Workbench / BigQuery notebooks for Python-based data science. Processed data stored in BigQuery is immediately available to both personas without duplicate storage. Cloud Storage + external tables adds latency and complexity. Bigtable is not suited for SQL analytics or ad-hoc queries. Cloud SQL doesn't scale for large analytical workloads and lacks native notebook integration.

questions.question: A company needs to serve static website content globally with low latency and wants to reduce egress costs from Cloud Storage. Which combination of GCP services should you recommend?
options: Cloud Storage bucket with Cloud CDN enabled via a Global External Application Load Balancer
Cloud Storage bucket with Signed URLs and no CDN
Cloud Storage multi-region bucket with uniform bucket-level access
Cloud Run serving static files with Cloud Armor in front
questions.explanation: Cloud CDN caches content at Google's 100+ edge locations globally, reducing latency and Cloud Storage egress costs (cached responses don't incur Storage egress fees). It requires a Global External Application Load Balancer as the origin. Signed URLs control access but don't reduce latency or cost. Multi-region Storage improves availability and geo-redundancy but doesn't cache at the edge. Cloud Run adds unnecessary compute overhead for purely static content.

questions.question: An organization is migrating a 3-tier web application to GCP. The web and application tiers must scale horizontally, but the database tier must remain on-premises during migration. Which architecture supports this with secure connectivity?
options: Deploy web and app tiers on GKE in GCP; use Cloud VPN or Dedicated Interconnect to connect GCP VPC to on-premises database
Deploy all tiers on GCP using Cloud SQL as a proxy to the on-premises database
Deploy web tier on Cloud Run, app tier on App Engine; expose on-premises database via a public IP with TLS
Use Cloud SQL with external data sync from on-premises via Database Migration Service
questions.explanation: Deploying the scalable tiers on GKE in GCP while maintaining secure private connectivity to the on-premises database via Cloud VPN (or Dedicated Interconnect for production SLAs) is the standard hybrid migration pattern. Cloud SQL cannot proxy to an on-premises database. Exposing the on-premises database via a public IP is a security risk. Database Migration Service is for migrating data to Cloud SQL, not for maintaining a live on-premises database connection.

questions.question: A startup wants to build a mobile backend with user authentication, serverless functions, and a NoSQL database — all managed services with minimal configuration. Which set of GCP services forms the most integrated solution?
options: Firebase Authentication, Cloud Functions for Firebase, and Firestore
Identity Platform, Cloud Run, and Cloud SQL
IAP, App Engine, and Datastore
Identity Platform, Cloud Functions, and Bigtable
questions.explanation: Firebase Authentication + Cloud Functions for Firebase + Firestore is the canonical Firebase/GCP mobile backend stack. They share the same Firebase project, have native SDKs for iOS/Android/Web, and integrate seamlessly. Identity Platform + Cloud Run + Cloud SQL requires more configuration. IAP + App Engine + Datastore lacks native mobile SDK integration. Identity Platform + Bigtable requires custom schema design and lacks mobile real-time sync.

questions.question: A media company ingests 50,000 video uploads per day. Each video must be transcoded into 5 formats. The transcoding jobs take between 5 and 30 minutes. They want automatic scaling based on queue depth. Which architecture is most appropriate?
options: Pub/Sub topic receiving upload events, Cloud Tasks queue for transcoding jobs, GCE workers with autoscaling based on queue depth
Cloud Run receiving upload webhooks and spawning transcoding jobs synchronously
Cloud Scheduler triggering Dataflow batch jobs every hour
App Engine Standard processing upload events with a pull queue
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub captures upload events; Cloud Tasks manages the transcoding work queue with configurable retries, rate limiting, and scheduling; GCE MIG workers scale based on Pub/Sub/Cloud Tasks queue depth via custom metrics in Cloud Monitoring. Cloud Run is not suitable for 5-30 minute CPU-intensive jobs (max timeout 60 minutes, but not designed for heavy compute). Cloud Scheduler + Dataflow adds up to 1 hour of latency. App Engine Standard has strict runtime limits unsuitable for 30-minute jobs.

questions.question: Your company has a strict data residency requirement: all EU customer data must remain in the EU. You use BigQuery for analytics. Which configuration enforces this requirement?
options: Create BigQuery datasets in an EU multi-region or specific EU region location
Use BigQuery column-level security with EU-only access policies
Enable VPC Service Controls around BigQuery with EU-specific access levels
Use Cloud DLP to tag EU data and restrict queries using row-level security
questions.explanation: BigQuery dataset location (EU multi-region or eu-west1, etc.) determines where data is stored and processed. Data in EU-located datasets never leaves the EU. Column-level security controls who can see data but not where it's stored. VPC Service Controls restrict access paths but don't control data residency. Cloud DLP tags and masks sensitive data but doesn't control physical data location.

questions.question: A company runs a critical application on GKE. They need to ensure that if an entire GCP region becomes unavailable, the application continues serving traffic with RPO = 0 and RTO < 5 minutes. Which architecture achieves this?
options: Active-active multi-region GKE clusters with Global External Application Load Balancer and synchronous data replication
Active-passive multi-region GKE clusters with DNS failover and asynchronous replication
Single-region GKE cluster with multi-zone node pools
Active-active clusters with Cloud VPN between regions and regional load balancers
questions.explanation: Active-active multi-region deployment ensures both regions serve live traffic, so regional failure causes immediate traffic rerouting (RTO < 5 min) via the Global LB health checks. Synchronous replication achieves RPO = 0. Active-passive with async replication risks data loss (RPO > 0) and DNS failover typically takes 5+ minutes (RTO violated). A single multi-zone cluster fails when the entire region is unavailable. Regional load balancers with VPN doesn't meet RTO < 5 min due to failover complexity.

questions.question: An analytics team runs ad-hoc queries in BigQuery. Costs have escalated because analysts scan entire tables. Which TWO features should you implement to reduce query costs without restricting analyst productivity? Select TWO.
options: Partition tables by date and advise analysts to include partition filters in WHERE clauses
Cluster tables on frequently filtered columns
Enable BigQuery flat-rate pricing to cap costs
Move data to Cloud Storage and use external tables
questions.explanation: Partitioning by date allows BigQuery to scan only relevant partitions when analysts include date filters, reducing bytes billed. Clustering physically sorts data by clustered columns, reducing bytes scanned for queries filtering on those columns. Together they can reduce scanned bytes by 80-95%. Flat-rate pricing caps slot usage, not necessarily bytes scanned costs for on-demand billing. External tables on Cloud Storage are slower and don't reduce costs — BigQuery still scans the underlying files.

questions.question: A company is designing an IoT platform for 1 million connected devices sending telemetry every second. The platform must ingest, process, and store data for real-time dashboards and historical analysis. Which architecture handles this scale?
options: IoT devices → Pub/Sub → Dataflow (streaming) → BigQuery for analytics and Bigtable for real-time lookups
IoT devices → Cloud IoT Core → Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions → Cloud SQL
IoT devices → Cloud Storage (streaming uploads) → Dataproc batch processing → BigQuery
IoT devices → Cloud Tasks → Cloud Run → Firestore
questions.explanation: This architecture handles 1M msg/sec: Pub/Sub scales to millions of messages per second; Dataflow provides auto-scaling streaming processing; BigQuery handles petabyte analytical queries for historical dashboards; Bigtable handles millions of reads/writes per second for real-time lookups with millisecond latency. Cloud Functions + Cloud SQL cannot scale to 1M msg/sec. Cloud Storage streaming uploads add latency and complexity. Cloud Tasks + Cloud Run + Firestore is better for transactional workloads, not high-throughput telemetry.

questions.question: Your application currently uses Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL. A new feature requires full-text search with relevance ranking across millions of documents. Modifying the Cloud SQL schema is not permitted. Which solution adds search capability with minimal architectural change?
options: Sync Cloud SQL changes to Elasticsearch via Datastream and query Elasticsearch for search
Use Cloud SQL's built-in pg_trgm extension for full-text search
Migrate to Firestore and use its native array-contains queries
Export data to BigQuery daily and use BigQuery SEARCH function
questions.explanation: Datastream (GCP's change data capture service) streams changes from Cloud SQL PostgreSQL to downstream systems. Syncing to Elasticsearch (self-managed on GCE or via a managed service) provides enterprise-grade full-text search with relevance ranking without modifying Cloud SQL schema. pg_trgm provides basic trigram-based similarity search but not production-scale relevance ranking. Firestore migration is a major architectural change. BigQuery daily export adds up to 24 hours of staleness.

questions.question: A company is evaluating compute options for a containerized application that requires GPU acceleration for ML inference, must scale to zero when idle, and should integrate with Cloud Run's managed infrastructure. Which option should they choose?
options: Cloud Run with GPU support
GKE Autopilot with GPU node pools
GKE Standard with preemptible GPU nodes
Vertex AI Prediction endpoints
questions.explanation: Cloud Run now supports GPU-attached instances (NVIDIA L4 GPUs), provides scale-to-zero behavior, and uses the same managed Cloud Run infrastructure. GKE Autopilot with GPU nodes does not scale to zero (minimum 1 node per pool). GKE Standard with preemptible GPU nodes requires cluster management and doesn't scale to zero. Vertex AI Prediction endpoints are designed for serving ML models with a specific SDK interface, not arbitrary containerized applications.

questions.question: A bank needs to store financial transaction records with ACID compliance, support for complex SQL queries, and a requirement to query data from 5 years ago in under 2 seconds. The dataset is 50 TB. Which solution meets all requirements?
options: BigQuery with date-partitioned tables
Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with read replicas
Cloud Spanner with a single region instance
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL
questions.explanation: BigQuery handles 50 TB datasets with sub-2-second query times when queries are well-structured with partition pruning. It supports SQL and is ACID-compliant for DML operations. Cloud SQL and AlloyDB struggle to query 50 TB efficiently within 2 seconds — they're designed for OLTP, not OLAP-scale queries. Cloud Spanner handles globally distributed OLTP well but is not optimized for full-scan OLAP analytics on 50 TB within 2 seconds.

questions.question: An organization needs to ensure that their Cloud Storage bucket hosting a data lake is never accidentally deleted, and that objects cannot be deleted before a 1-year retention period. Which TWO features should you configure? Select TWO.
options: Object retention locks with a 1-year retention period
Bucket Lock with a 1-year retention policy
Versioning enabled on the bucket
IAM deny policy preventing storage.buckets.delete on the bucket
questions.explanation: Bucket Lock with a retention policy prevents deletion of objects before the retention period expires and, once locked, the policy itself cannot be removed or shortened. An IAM deny policy explicitly denying storage.buckets.delete prevents accidental bucket deletion. Object retention locks (introduced in 2023) work at the object level but Bucket Lock is the established feature for compliance retention. Versioning protects against overwrites but not retention enforcement.

questions.question: A SaaS company wants to offer their product in multiple GCP regions. Each customer's data must be isolated and stored only in the region the customer selected. The application uses a shared GKE cluster per region. How should the architect handle data isolation?
options: Separate Cloud SQL instances per customer in the customer's selected region, accessed via Private Service Connect
A single global Cloud Spanner instance with row-level security filtering by customer ID
A single Cloud SQL instance per region with schema-per-customer isolation
Firestore databases per region with IAM rules binding each customer to their namespace
questions.explanation: Separate Cloud SQL instances per customer in their selected region provides the strongest data isolation and enforces data residency. Private Service Connect enables secure, private connectivity from the GKE cluster to each customer's database. A global Spanner instance stores data across regions and cannot enforce single-region residency per customer. Schema-per-customer on a shared instance risks noisy-neighbor issues and complicates backup/restore isolation. Firestore doesn't support per-region database isolation with the required data residency guarantees.

questions.question: A logistics company runs a global tracking system. Package location events are generated worldwide and must be processed in the origin continent to comply with data sovereignty rules, then aggregated globally for analytics. Which Pub/Sub feature enables regional processing?
options: Pub/Sub message storage policy to restrict message storage to specific regions
Pub/Sub Lite with zonal topics in each compliance region
Separate Pub/Sub topics per region with Cloud Functions processing locally
Pub/Sub with Cloud Dataflow regional endpoints
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub message storage policies allow you to constrain where messages are stored and processed to specific GCP regions, satisfying data sovereignty requirements. This is the built-in compliance feature for regional data residency in Pub/Sub. Pub/Sub Lite is a cost-optimized variant but lacks global redundancy needed for a logistics platform. Separate topics with Cloud Functions requires significant custom routing logic. Dataflow regional endpoints control where Dataflow workers run but not where Pub/Sub stores messages.

questions.question: A development team wants to implement a circuit breaker pattern for their microservices running on Cloud Run. The circuit breaker should open when error rate exceeds 50% over a 10-second window. Which approach is recommended on GCP?
options: Deploy an Envoy-based service mesh via Traffic Director or Anthos Service Mesh with circuit breaker outlier detection
Implement circuit breaker logic in each service's application code using Resilience4j
Use Cloud Load Balancing circuit breaker policies on backend services
Configure Cloud Endpoints with circuit breaker policies in the API gateway
questions.explanation: Traffic Director and Anthos Service Mesh (Istio-based) provide infrastructure-level circuit breakers via Envoy's outlier detection, without requiring application code changes. This is the GCP-recommended approach for service mesh circuit breaking. Application-level Resilience4j works but is language-specific and requires code changes in every service. Cloud Load Balancing does not have circuit breaker policies. Cloud Endpoints doesn't support circuit breaker policies natively.

questions.question: A company migrates a .NET Framework 4.5 application to GCP. The application cannot be rewritten and requires Windows Server 2019. Which compute option is most appropriate?
options: Compute Engine VM with a Windows Server 2019 image
App Engine Standard with .NET runtime
Cloud Run with a Windows container
GKE with Windows node pools
questions.explanation: Compute Engine supports Windows Server 2019 images and allows running .NET Framework 4.5 applications with a lift-and-shift approach. App Engine Standard supports .NET Core/5+ but not .NET Framework 4.5. Cloud Run does not support Windows containers. GKE Windows node pools support Windows containers but .NET Framework 4.5 apps typically require full VMs, not containers, for a lift-and-shift migration.

questions.question: Your organization needs to implement event-driven autoscaling for a GKE workload based on the number of unacknowledged messages in a Pub/Sub subscription. Which solution should you use?
options: KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) with Pub/Sub scaler on GKE
GKE Horizontal Pod Autoscaler with custom metrics from Cloud Monitoring
GKE Vertical Pod Autoscaler triggered by Pub/Sub message count
Cloud Run instead of GKE, as it natively scales on Pub/Sub queue depth
questions.explanation: KEDA integrates with GKE and provides a Pub/Sub scaler that directly reads subscription backlog metrics to drive HPA scaling. It is the recommended and officially supported approach for event-driven autoscaling on GKE. HPA with custom metrics from Cloud Monitoring can work but requires more configuration and a custom metrics adapter. VPA scales pod resources (CPU/memory), not pod count. Cloud Run is an alternative but changes the infrastructure platform, which may not be acceptable.

questions.question: A financial company needs a hybrid connectivity solution with 99.99% uptime SLA for their GCP connection. They cannot use a colocation facility. Which configuration meets this requirement?
options: Partner Interconnect with two VLAN attachments in two different metros connected to two different Cloud Routers
Dedicated Interconnect with a single 10 Gbps link
HA VPN with two tunnels over the internet
Partner Interconnect with a single VLAN attachment
questions.explanation: Partner Interconnect achieves 99.99% SLA when configured with redundant VLAN attachments in two different metros (different service providers preferred) connected to separate Cloud Routers in the same region. Dedicated Interconnect requires colocation access. HA VPN achieves 99.99% SLA but goes over the public internet — not suitable for financial applications requiring dedicated connectivity. A single VLAN attachment in Partner Interconnect achieves only 99.9% SLA.

questions.question: A company's Cloud Storage bucket triggers a Cloud Function to process each uploaded file. Processing takes up to 8 minutes. Occasionally, the same file is processed twice due to event delivery retries. Which design pattern prevents duplicate processing?
options: Implement idempotency by recording processed file IDs in Firestore and checking before processing
Disable Cloud Storage event retries to prevent duplicate triggers
Use Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery instead of direct Cloud Storage triggers
Increase Cloud Function timeout to prevent retries
questions.explanation: Idempotent processing — checking a Firestore record of processed file IDs before starting work — is the correct pattern. If a file ID exists, skip processing. This handles both retry-induced duplicates and any other delivery-at-least-once scenarios. Disabling retries risks missing legitimate processing failures. Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery reduces duplicates but does not eliminate them entirely at the Dataflow/function consumer level. Increasing timeout reduces retries due to timeout but doesn't prevent duplicates from other causes.

questions.question: A company's Dataflow streaming pipeline is experiencing high latency. The pipeline reads from Pub/Sub, joins a stream with a lookup table from BigQuery, and writes to Cloud Storage. Which optimization reduces latency most effectively?
options: Cache the BigQuery lookup table as a side input loaded once at pipeline startup instead of querying per element
Increase the number of Dataflow workers
Switch from Pub/Sub pull subscription to push subscription
Replace Cloud Storage output with Bigtable for faster writes
questions.explanation: Querying BigQuery per element is the most common source of high latency in streaming Dataflow pipelines. Loading the lookup table as a side input at startup (or refreshing periodically) eliminates per-element BigQuery calls, dramatically reducing latency. Adding workers helps with throughput but not per-element latency if the bottleneck is the BigQuery call. Pull vs. push subscription doesn't significantly affect processing latency. Replacing the output changes the architecture unnecessarily.

questions.question: A multinational company needs to centrally manage networking for 50 GCP projects across three regions while allowing each project team to manage their own VM instances. Which network architecture achieves centralized control with delegated instance management?
options: Shared VPC: networking team owns the host project, each product team gets a service project with subnet-level IAM bindings
VPC peering between all 50 projects
Each project has its own VPC; Cloud Interconnect connects all projects centrally
Organization-level VPC with all 50 projects sharing a single project VPC
questions.explanation: Shared VPC centralizes network control in the host project (managed by the networking team) while granting service project teams IAM roles to use specific subnets for their instances. This is the canonical GCP pattern for central networking + delegated compute. VPC peering between 50 projects creates a complex mesh (non-transitive) and doesn't centralize networking. Cloud Interconnect connecting individual project VPCs doesn't centralize intra-GCP networking. There is no 'organization-level VPC' concept in GCP.

questions.question: A team is designing a Cloud Run service that needs to access a Cloud SQL instance. The Cloud SQL instance is in the same project and region. What is the recommended connection method?
options: Use the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy via the Cloud Run built-in Cloud SQL connection (unix socket via Cloud SQL instance connection name)
Connect via Cloud SQL public IP with SSL certificate authentication
Use Private Service Connect to connect Cloud Run to Cloud SQL
Connect via Cloud SQL private IP using a Serverless VPC Access connector
questions.explanation: Cloud Run natively supports Cloud SQL connections by specifying the instance connection name, which automatically handles the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy as a sidecar, providing IAM-authenticated, encrypted connections via unix sockets. This is the Google-recommended approach. Connecting via public IP exposes the database to the internet. Private Service Connect is for connecting to services in other VPCs/projects. Serverless VPC Access + private IP works but adds network hops and is less elegant than the native built-in connection.

questions.question: A company wants to implement a blue/green deployment for their App Engine Standard application to test a new version with 5% of traffic before full rollout. Which App Engine feature enables this?
options: Traffic splitting by percentage across multiple App Engine versions
App Engine flexible environments with Cloud Load Balancing weights
Cloud Run revisions with traffic splitting
GKE canary deployments with Istio traffic management
questions.explanation: App Engine Standard natively supports traffic splitting across versions by percentage, IP address, or cookie. You can send 5% of traffic to the new version and 95% to the current version directly in the App Engine console or via gcloud. This is built into App Engine and requires no additional services. Cloud Run revisions support traffic splitting but the question specifies App Engine. GKE with Istio is valid but changes the platform.

questions.question: An organization stores sensitive PII in BigQuery. Analysts need to run queries but must not see individual customer names or email addresses. Admins need full access. Which BigQuery feature should you implement?
options: Column-level security with policy tags and data masking rules in Sensitive Data Protection
Row-level security policies filtering by analyst identity
Authorized views exposing only non-PII columns
BigQuery column encryption with AEAD functions
questions.explanation: BigQuery column-level security uses policy tags in Data Catalog to classify sensitive columns (name, email). Sensitive Data Protection data masking rules define how masked values appear to users without the Fine-Grained Reader role (e.g., NULL, hashed, or partial mask). Admins with the Fine-Grained Reader role see real data. Row-level security restricts rows, not columns. Authorized views require creating and maintaining separate views. AEAD encryption requires analysts to possess decryption keys — not suitable for transparent access control.

questions.question: A company needs their GCP workloads to access Google APIs (e.g., Cloud Storage, BigQuery) without sending traffic over the public internet. The VMs have no external IP addresses. Which feature enables this?
options: Private Google Access enabled on the subnet
Cloud NAT configured on the VPC
VPC Service Controls peering to Google's API network
Private Service Connect endpoints for Google APIs
questions.explanation: Private Google Access allows VMs without external IPs to reach Google APIs and services using the VM's internal IP address, routing traffic via Google's internal network. Cloud NAT allows VMs without external IPs to reach the internet (not internal Google APIs). VPC Service Controls restrict which identities and resources can access services, but don't route traffic. Private Service Connect endpoints also work for this but Private Google Access is the simpler, standard approach for all Google APIs.

questions.question: A manufacturing company collects sensor data that must be stored indefinitely but queried with aggregations by time window. The dataset grows by 1 TB per month. Queries filter by sensor ID and time range. Which storage and query solution is most cost-effective?
options: BigQuery with a partitioned table by DAY on the timestamp column, clustered by sensor_id
Bigtable with row key designed as sensor_id#reverse_timestamp
Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with time-series partitioning
Firestore with composite indexes on sensor_id and timestamp
questions.explanation: BigQuery handles multi-TB analytical workloads natively. Partitioning by DAY on timestamp allows queries filtering by time range to scan only relevant partitions. Clustering by sensor_id further reduces bytes scanned when filtering by sensor. BigQuery's on-demand pricing means you pay only for bytes scanned. Bigtable is excellent for high-throughput row-level reads/writes but aggregation queries are expensive. Cloud SQL struggles with TB-scale time-series analytics. Firestore is not designed for aggregation queries on TB-scale data.

questions.question: A company runs a mission-critical application on GKE. A recent deployment caused an outage. The team wants to implement a deployment strategy that automatically rolls back if a new version causes increased error rates. Which GKE feature supports this?
options: GKE rolling updates with liveness and readiness probes triggering automatic rollback
GKE Autopilot automated rollback based on Cloud Monitoring error rate alerts
Anthos Service Mesh with canary deployment and automated rollback policies
Cloud Deploy with target approval gates and rollback commands
questions.explanation: Anthos Service Mesh (Istio) supports canary deployments where traffic is gradually shifted to a new version. Integrated with Cloud Monitoring, you can configure automated rollback policies that revert traffic if error rates exceed thresholds. GKE rolling updates with probes roll back if pods fail health checks (application crashes), but not based on application-level error rates. GKE Autopilot doesn't have automated error-rate-based rollback. Cloud Deploy provides pipeline orchestration but rollback is manual or approval-gated.

questions.question: A team needs to run a long-running data migration job that will take 6-8 hours on GCP. The job is CPU and memory intensive, uses a custom Docker container, and must complete without interruption. Which compute option is best?
options: Cloud Batch job with a single non-preemptible VM task
Cloud Run job with a custom container
A preemptible Compute Engine VM
Cloud Functions with maximum timeout
questions.explanation: Cloud Batch is designed for long-running batch jobs on VMs, supporting custom containers, configurable machine types, and non-preemptible VMs to prevent interruption. Cloud Run jobs have a maximum timeout of 24 hours but are designed for shorter tasks; for 6-8 hour CPU-intensive work needing full VM resources, Cloud Batch is more appropriate. Preemptible VMs can be interrupted at any time, violating the no-interruption requirement. Cloud Functions has a maximum timeout of 60 minutes, far less than 6-8 hours.

questions.question: A retail company needs to implement a recommendation engine that serves personalized product recommendations with latency under 100ms. The model is retrained nightly using Vertex AI. Which serving architecture meets the latency requirement?
options: Pre-compute recommendations nightly after retraining and store in Firestore; serve from Firestore at runtime
Serve the Vertex AI model via online prediction endpoint at runtime
Cache model outputs in Memorystore for Redis with a 24-hour TTL
Deploy the model to Cloud Run with a custom inference server
questions.explanation: Pre-computing recommendations nightly and storing in Firestore allows sub-millisecond retrieval by user ID, far below the 100ms requirement. Since the model is retrained nightly anyway, pre-computed results are as fresh as possible. Vertex AI online prediction endpoints typically have p99 latencies of 200-500ms, which may exceed the 100ms requirement under load. Memorystore caching is effective but requires a cache miss path. Cloud Run inference adds model loading overhead per cold start.

questions.question: An organization is choosing between Cloud VPN and Dedicated Interconnect for connecting their on-premises data center to GCP. Their requirements are: 500 Mbps average throughput, 1.5 Gbps peak throughput, and SLA of 99.9%. Which option is most cost-effective?
options: HA VPN with two tunnels (each supporting up to 3 Gbps) achieves 99.99% SLA and handles the throughput
Dedicated Interconnect with a 10 Gbps link for SLA and headroom
Partner Interconnect at 1 Gbps capacity for cost efficiency
Standard VPN (single tunnel) for simplicity
questions.explanation: HA VPN with two tunnels achieves 99.99% SLA (exceeding the 99.9% requirement) and each tunnel supports up to 3 Gbps, covering both average and peak throughput needs. It is significantly cheaper than Dedicated Interconnect (which requires co-location and higher commitment). Partner Interconnect at 1 Gbps barely covers the 1.5 Gbps peak. Standard VPN with a single tunnel achieves only 99.9% SLA and has a single point of failure.

questions.question: A company uses Cloud Storage to store user-uploaded files. They need to generate temporary URLs that grant time-limited read access to specific files without requiring the user to have a Google account. Which feature should they use?
options: Signed URLs generated by the application backend using a service account key
Uniform bucket-level access with allUsers read permission
IAP-protected Cloud Run endpoint that streams files from Cloud Storage
Firebase Storage download URLs
questions.explanation: Signed URLs provide time-limited, cryptographically signed access to specific Cloud Storage objects. They can be accessed by anyone with the URL, without requiring a Google account. The backend generates them using a service account's credentials. Uniform bucket-level access with allUsers makes files permanently public, not time-limited. IAP requires Google account authentication. Firebase Storage download URLs are permanent unless revoked, not time-limited.

questions.question: A company needs to migrate their on-premises Hadoop cluster to GCP. The cluster runs both batch processing (nightly) and interactive SQL queries (during business hours). Which GCP architecture separates these workloads cost-effectively?
options: Use ephemeral Dataproc clusters for nightly batch jobs and BigQuery for interactive SQL queries
Migrate the Hadoop cluster to a persistent Dataproc cluster for both workloads
Use Dataflow for batch processing and Cloud SQL for interactive SQL queries
Use a persistent Dataproc cluster for batch and a separate persistent Dataproc cluster for interactive SQL
questions.explanation: Ephemeral Dataproc clusters for batch jobs spin up only during the nightly run (paying only for runtime), while BigQuery handles interactive SQL on data stored in Cloud Storage (no cluster to maintain, pay per query). This is the recommended GCP modernization path for Hadoop migrations. A persistent Dataproc cluster runs 24/7, paying for idle time. Dataflow for batch + Cloud SQL for interactive SQL doesn't leverage existing Hive/Spark workloads. Two persistent clusters doubles idle costs.

questions.question: A startup needs a multi-tier architecture for a web application: React frontend, Node.js API, and PostgreSQL database. They want maximum managed service usage, auto-scaling, and pay-per-use pricing. Which combination is most appropriate? Select TWO.
options: Cloud Run for the Node.js API backend with auto-scaling and scale-to-zero
Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with serverless auto-scaling
Firebase Hosting for the React frontend with global CDN
GKE Autopilot for the Node.js API backend
questions.explanation: Cloud Run provides auto-scaling to zero for the Node.js API with pay-per-request pricing. Firebase Hosting provides global CDN delivery for the static React frontend with a generous free tier and pay-per-use pricing. Cloud SQL does not have true serverless auto-scaling (it scales storage but not compute automatically). GKE Autopilot has minimum resource costs and doesn't scale to zero.

questions.question: A team runs a GKE cluster for production workloads. Node upgrades have been causing brief disruptions. They want nodes to upgrade automatically during a predictable maintenance window without disrupting running pods. Which GKE features should they configure? Select TWO.
options: Node auto-upgrade enabled on the node pool
Maintenance windows and exclusions configured on the cluster
GKE Autopilot mode for automatic node management
Preemptible nodes with automatic replacement
questions.explanation: Node auto-upgrade keeps node pools updated automatically to the latest GKE patch version. Maintenance windows allow you to specify when GKE can perform disruptive operations (like node upgrades), ensuring they happen during low-traffic periods. Together they provide automatic, predictable upgrades. GKE Autopilot manages nodes fully but changes the cluster mode entirely. Preemptible nodes are for cost savings, not controlled upgrade scheduling.

questions.question: A GKE cluster's node pool needs to scale based on CPU utilization of pods. Nodes should be added when average CPU across all pods exceeds 70% and removed when it drops below 30%. Which configuration achieves this?
options: Enable GKE Cluster Autoscaler on the node pool and configure HorizontalPodAutoscaler on the deployment with CPU target 70%
Configure GKE Vertical Pod Autoscaler with target CPU of 70%
Use Managed Instance Group autoscaling with CPU target 70% as the underlying node group
Configure Cloud Monitoring alert policies to trigger node additions via Cloud Functions
questions.explanation: HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) scales pod count based on CPU utilization. When pods need more resources than available nodes can provide, GKE Cluster Autoscaler adds nodes. When pods scale down and nodes are underutilized, Cluster Autoscaler removes them. VPA adjusts pod resource requests (CPU/memory limits), not pod count. MIG autoscaling is the underlying mechanism but shouldn't be configured directly when using GKE. Cloud Monitoring + Cloud Functions is error-prone and not the recommended approach.

questions.question: Your organization wants to deploy GKE workloads where Google manages node provisioning, patching, and scaling, and you pay only for pod resource requests. Which GKE mode should you use?
options: GKE Autopilot
GKE Standard with Cluster Autoscaler
GKE Standard with node auto-provisioning
Cloud Run on GKE (Knative)
questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot is a fully managed GKE mode where Google handles node provisioning, scaling, patching, and security hardening. You are billed per pod resource requests (CPU, memory, ephemeral storage), not per node. GKE Standard with Cluster Autoscaler still requires you to manage node pools and pay for nodes. Node auto-provisioning creates new node pools automatically but you still manage and pay for nodes. Cloud Run on GKE (now Knative on GKE) is a different deployment model.

questions.question: A Managed Instance Group (MIG) runs a stateful application where each VM has a unique persistent disk. During a rolling update, the new VM template must be applied without losing the existing persistent disks. Which MIG feature supports this?
options: Stateful MIG with stateful disks configured in the instance configuration
Regional MIG with automatic failover to preserve disk data
MIG with per-instance configurations and snapshot-based disk reattachment
Unmanaged instance group with manual disk management
questions.explanation: Stateful MIGs support preserving per-instance stateful configuration including persistent disks, IPs, and metadata across updates and auto-healing events. When a stateful disk is configured, the MIG reattaches the existing disk to the replacement VM instead of creating a new one. Regional MIG failover moves instances between zones but doesn't specifically handle stateful disk preservation. Per-instance configs combined with snapshots is a workaround but not the primary feature. Unmanaged groups lose all autoscaling and update management benefits.

questions.question: A company deploys App Engine Standard applications. They want to validate a new version handles production traffic before full rollout and need to split traffic by cookie to ensure returning users consistently get the same version. Which App Engine traffic splitting method should they use?
options: Cookie-based traffic splitting using the GOOGAPPUID cookie
IP-based traffic splitting
Random traffic splitting by percentage
Custom HTTP header-based routing via Cloud Load Balancing
questions.explanation: App Engine supports cookie-based traffic splitting using the GOOGAPPUID cookie. Once a user is assigned to a version via the cookie, they consistently receive that version on subsequent requests (session affinity). IP-based splitting provides some consistency but users behind NAT may be split. Random splitting by percentage has no session affinity — users may get different versions on each request. App Engine traffic splitting is built-in and doesn't use Cloud Load Balancing custom headers.

questions.question: A Cloud Run service handles payment processing. The service must not scale beyond 10 instances to prevent overwhelming a downstream payment gateway that has a rate limit. The service also needs at least 2 instances always running for low latency. Which Cloud Run settings achieve this?
options: Set minimum instances to 2 and maximum instances to 10
Set concurrency to 10 and minimum instances to 2
Set maximum instances to 10 and request timeout to limit throughput
Use Cloud Tasks to queue requests and limit Cloud Run to 2 instances
questions.explanation: Cloud Run's minimum instances setting keeps 2 instances warm (eliminating cold starts for consistent low latency), and maximum instances caps scaling at 10 instances (protecting the downstream payment gateway rate limit). Concurrency controls how many requests a single instance handles simultaneously, not the total instance count. Limiting timeout doesn't prevent scaling. Cloud Tasks + 2 instances would add latency and queue complexity unnecessarily.

questions.question: An organization has three GCP projects: a host project with a Shared VPC and two service projects. A developer in a service project needs to create VM instances that use subnets from the Shared VPC. Which IAM role must be granted to the developer in the host project?
options: compute.networkUser on the specific subnet in the host project
compute.networkAdmin on the host project
compute.admin on the host project
resourcemanager.projectIamAdmin on the host project
questions.explanation: The compute.networkUser role on the specific subnet in the host project allows the developer to use that subnet when creating VMs in their service project. It grants permission to use the network resource without giving broader network management access. compute.networkAdmin grants full network management rights (too permissive). compute.admin on the host project grants VM and network admin (too permissive). resourcemanager.projectIamAdmin manages IAM policies, not subnet access.

questions.question: A company's GKE cluster nodes need to download container images from Artifact Registry. The nodes have no external IP addresses. Which configuration allows the nodes to pull images without public internet access?
options: Enable Private Google Access on the node subnet and use Artifact Registry in the same region
Configure Cloud NAT on the VPC to provide outbound internet access for image pulls
Use a GKE node pool with external IP addresses for image pulling only
Mirror all images to a Cloud Storage bucket and configure containerd to pull from there
questions.explanation: Private Google Access enables VMs without external IPs to access Google APIs and services, including Artifact Registry, through Google's internal network. Artifact Registry is a Google service reachable via Private Google Access. Cloud NAT routes traffic to the public internet, not Google's internal network — it works but is less secure and not the recommended path. Adding external IPs to nodes increases the attack surface. Mirroring to Cloud Storage is complex and not how container runtimes work.

questions.question: Your organization needs VMs in a private subnet to access the internet for software package updates, without assigning external IP addresses to the VMs. Which GCP service provides this capability?
options: Cloud NAT
Cloud VPN
Private Google Access
Cloud Interconnect
questions.explanation: Cloud NAT (Network Address Translation) allows VMs without external IP addresses to make outbound connections to the internet (e.g., for package updates) by translating their private IPs to a NAT gateway IP. Cloud VPN connects private networks, not VMs to the internet. Private Google Access allows VMs to reach Google APIs only, not the general internet. Cloud Interconnect connects on-premises networks to GCP, not VMs to the internet.

questions.question: A security team wants to ensure that all VM instances in a GCP project use only approved machine types and that no VM can have an external IP address. Which GCP feature enforces these requirements at scale?
options: Organization Policy constraints: constraints/compute.restrictCloudSQLInstances is incorrect; use constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess set to deny all, and constraints/compute.allowedMachineTypes
IAM deny policies preventing compute.instances.create with disallowed machine types
Terraform Sentinel policies enforcing machine type and IP constraints
Organization Policy constraints: constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess set to deny all and constraints/compute.allowedMachineTypes listing approved types
questions.explanation: Organization Policy constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess set to deny all prevents any VM in the scope (organization, folder, or project) from having an external IP. constraints/compute.allowedMachineTypes restricts VMs to an approved list of machine types. These policies are enforced server-side — even users with compute.admin cannot bypass them. IAM deny policies operate on API methods, not resource parameters like machine type. Terraform Sentinel only works for Terraform-managed infrastructure.

questions.question: A company uses Terraform to manage GCP infrastructure. The team wants to store Terraform state securely with locking to prevent concurrent modifications. Which backend should they configure?
options: Google Cloud Storage backend with a state locking bucket
Local filesystem backend with Git version control
Terraform Cloud remote backend
Cloud SQL backend for state storage
questions.explanation: The GCS backend for Terraform stores state in Cloud Storage and uses Cloud Storage object locking for state locking to prevent concurrent modifications. It is the recommended GCP-native backend for teams. Local filesystem backend has no locking and is unsuitable for teams. Terraform Cloud is a valid option but introduces an external dependency outside GCP. There is no Cloud SQL backend for Terraform.

questions.question: An organization's GKE application pods need to call a Cloud Storage API using a service account. The security team prohibits downloading service account key files. Which authentication method should be used?
options: Workload Identity Federation for GKE, binding a Kubernetes service account to a GCP service account
Mount the GCP service account key as a Kubernetes secret and reference it in the pod spec
Use the GKE node's default service account, which has Editor permissions
Annotate pods with the service account email and use Application Default Credentials
questions.explanation: Workload Identity for GKE is the recommended approach for GKE pods to authenticate to Google Cloud APIs without service account key files. It binds a Kubernetes ServiceAccount to a GCP Service Account via IAM, and pods automatically receive short-lived credentials. Mounting key files as secrets is exactly what the security team prohibits. The node's default service account with Editor permissions violates least privilege. Annotating pods with a service account email alone doesn't provide credentials — Workload Identity must be configured.

questions.question: A company wants to implement rolling updates on a Managed Instance Group with zero downtime. New VMs must pass health checks before old VMs are removed. Which MIG configuration parameters control this behavior? Select TWO.
options: maxSurge: number of additional VMs created during update before old ones are deleted
maxUnavailable: set to 0 to ensure all existing VMs remain available during update
minReadySec: minimum time a new VM must be healthy before proceeding
replacementMethod: set to SUBSTITUTE to replace all VMs simultaneously
questions.explanation: maxSurge defines how many new VMs can be created above the target size during an update — new VMs are health-checked before old ones are removed. maxUnavailable set to 0 ensures no existing VMs go offline until replacement VMs are healthy, achieving zero downtime. minReadySec adds a stability buffer after health check passes but is optional. replacementMethod SUBSTITUTE replaces all VMs at once, causing downtime — the opposite of zero downtime.

questions.question: A company wants to provision GCP infrastructure using Infrastructure as Code and needs to use GCP-native tooling (no Terraform). They want to define resources in YAML and use GCP's deployment APIs directly. Which service should they use?
options: Cloud Deployment Manager
Config Connector on GKE
Cloud Build with gcloud commands
Pulumi with GCP provider
questions.explanation: Cloud Deployment Manager is GCP's native IaC service that uses YAML/Jinja2/Python templates and interacts directly with GCP's deployment API. Config Connector is Kubernetes-based and requires a GKE cluster to manage GCP resources. Cloud Build with gcloud commands is scripting, not declarative IaC. Pulumi is a third-party tool that uses programming languages, not YAML declarative templates.

questions.question: A company needs to set up a billing alert that notifies the finance team when monthly GCP spend exceeds $50,000, and automatically pauses non-critical Cloud Run services if spend reaches $75,000. How should this be implemented?
options: Create a budget with a $50,000 alert threshold notifying via email, and a $75,000 threshold with a Pub/Sub notification triggering a Cloud Function to disable Cloud Run services
Create a Cloud Monitoring alert on billing metrics with email notification at $50,000
Use Cloud Scheduler to check daily spend via Billing API and send alerts
Configure automatic budget enforcement to stop all services when spending exceeds $75,000
questions.explanation: GCP Budgets support multiple alert thresholds with different notification channels. At $50,000, email alerts notify the finance team. At $75,000, Pub/Sub notification triggers a Cloud Function that programmatically disables Cloud Run services. Cloud Monitoring alerts on billing metrics exist but are less direct. Cloud Scheduler + Billing API polling is manual and delayed. GCP does not natively 'stop all services' on budget — this must be implemented via Pub/Sub + Cloud Functions.

questions.question: An organization has a GKE cluster with multiple node pools. One node pool runs GPU workloads and should only host GPU pods. Another runs general workloads. How should you ensure GPU pods only run on GPU nodes and general pods don't consume GPU resources?
options: Add a taint (nvidia.com/gpu=present:NoSchedule) to GPU nodes and add a corresponding toleration and node selector to GPU pods
Use Pod Affinity rules to prefer GPU nodes for GPU pods
Use Resource Quotas to limit GPU resource usage to the GPU namespace only
Configure node labels and use nodeSelector in pod specs for GPU pods
questions.explanation: Taints on GPU nodes with NoSchedule effect prevent general pods (without the toleration) from being scheduled on GPU nodes, preserving expensive GPU resources. GPU pods add the matching toleration and a node selector or node affinity to ensure they land on GPU nodes. Pod Affinity prefers but doesn't guarantee GPU node placement for GPU pods or prevent general pod placement on GPU nodes. Resource Quotas limit quantities, not placement. nodeSelector alone prevents GPU pods from landing elsewhere but doesn't prevent general pods from landing on GPU nodes.

questions.question: A company uses Dedicated Interconnect with two 10 Gbps links for redundancy. They want to ensure that if one link fails, traffic automatically fails over to the other. Which routing configuration achieves this?
options: Configure two Cloud Routers with BGP sessions on each VLAN attachment and use equal-cost multipath (ECMP) routing
Configure static routes with different priorities for each link
Use a single Cloud Router with both VLAN attachments and manual route failover
Configure Cloud DNS to failover traffic between Interconnect links
questions.explanation: Cloud Router with BGP sessions on each VLAN attachment enables dynamic routing. With two equal-cost paths, ECMP distributes traffic across both links. When one link fails, BGP automatically removes the failed path and all traffic routes via the healthy link. Static routes with different priorities require manual intervention or don't automatically detect link failures. A single Cloud Router with manual failover requires manual intervention. Cloud DNS operates at the application layer, not network link failover.

questions.question: Your organization wants to implement Private DNS resolution for internal services. Employees on-premises should be able to resolve *.internal.company.com to GCP internal IPs when connected via Cloud VPN. Which Cloud DNS configuration achieves this?
options: Create a Cloud DNS private zone for internal.company.com and configure DNS peering or DNS forwarding from on-premises DNS servers to Cloud DNS inbound forwarding IP
Create a Cloud DNS public zone for internal.company.com with A records pointing to internal IPs
Configure split-horizon DNS by creating the same zone in both Cloud DNS and on-premises DNS
Use Cloud Endpoints to resolve internal service names over VPN
questions.explanation: Cloud DNS private zones resolve names only for resources within the associated VPCs. Cloud DNS inbound forwarding creates a forwarding IP within the VPC that on-premises DNS servers can forward queries to over VPN. This allows on-premises clients to resolve *.internal.company.com to GCP internal IPs. A public zone would expose internal DNS to the internet. Split-horizon requires manual synchronization between two DNS systems. Cloud Endpoints is an API gateway, not a DNS service.

questions.question: A team manages 200 GCP projects across multiple business units. They need to enforce that all projects tag resources with a cost-center label. Which approach enforces this consistently without manual per-project configuration?
options: Organization Policy with constraints/compute.requireOsLogin is incorrect; use a custom organization policy constraint enforcing label requirements at the organization or folder level
Cloud Asset Inventory scheduled exports with label compliance reporting and manual remediation
Terraform modules enforcing labels in all resource definitions across all 200 projects
A custom organization policy constraint requiring the cost-center label on supported resource types, applied at the organization level
questions.explanation: Custom organization policy constraints (managed constraints or custom constraints via CEL expressions) can enforce that specific labels exist on resource creation across all 200 projects without per-project configuration. Applied at the organization level, they cascade to all folders and projects. Cloud Asset Inventory reporting is detective, not preventive. Terraform modules only work for Terraform-managed resources and require all teams to use Terraform. The first option incorrectly references a different constraint.

questions.question: A Cloud Load Balancer health check is failing for a backend VM that appears healthy. The VM is running the application on port 8080, but the health check is configured for port 80. Additionally, a firewall rule is needed. Which TWO changes are needed to fix the health check? Select TWO.
options: Update the health check port from 80 to 8080
Create a firewall rule allowing ingress from 130.211.0.0/22 and 35.191.0.0/16 on port 8080
Create a firewall rule allowing ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 on port 8080
Restart the VM to reset the health check state
questions.explanation: The health check must be updated to use port 8080 to match the application's listening port. GCP health check probes originate from the IP ranges 130.211.0.0/22 and 35.191.0.0/16 — a firewall rule must allow ingress from these ranges on port 8080 to allow probes to reach the VM. Allowing 0.0.0.0/0 is overly permissive. Restarting the VM doesn't fix a misconfigured health check port.

questions.question: A company uses Cloud CDN in front of their Cloud Storage static assets. After deploying a new version of their website, some users still receive old cached content. Which is the fastest way to serve the updated content to all users?
options: Issue a cache invalidation request for the updated paths via the Cloud CDN API or console
Set Cache-Control: no-cache headers on new objects in Cloud Storage
Wait for the CDN TTL to expire naturally
Change the CDN cache mode from CACHE_ALL_STATIC to USE_ORIGIN_HEADERS
questions.explanation: Cloud CDN cache invalidation immediately purges cached objects from all CDN edge locations for the specified URL paths. This is the fastest way to force new content to be served without waiting for TTL expiry. Setting no-cache headers on new objects only affects future caching, not already-cached content at edges. Waiting for TTL expiry could take hours or days. Changing cache mode affects future caching behavior but doesn't invalidate existing cached content.

questions.question: An organization runs GKE workloads across three zones in a region. They want to ensure that if one zone fails, the remaining two zones have sufficient capacity to handle 100% of the load. Each zone normally runs 10 replicas. How many replicas should be configured?
options: 30 replicas with pod anti-affinity to spread across all three zones
20 replicas with pod affinity for two specific zones
15 replicas with zone-aware load balancing
10 replicas with cluster autoscaler to add capacity on zone failure
questions.explanation: With 30 replicas spread across 3 zones (10 per zone) using pod anti-affinity, if one zone fails, 20 replicas remain across 2 zones — exactly 100% of the normal load (since 20 = 2x the per-zone normal). Pod anti-affinity with topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone ensures even distribution. 20 replicas (10 per zone in 2 zones) leaves no capacity if one fails. 15 replicas with zone balancing (5 per zone) drops to 10 on zone failure — only 67% capacity. Autoscaling adds capacity but takes time and may not be fast enough during sudden zone failure.

questions.question: A company's Cloud Run service needs to connect to a Cloud SQL instance in the same VPC using private IP. The Cloud Run service is deployed in the same region. Which configuration is required?
options: Configure Serverless VPC Access connector in the same region and configure Cloud Run to use it; connect to Cloud SQL private IP
Configure Cloud Run with a VPC egress setting of ALL_TRAFFIC and use Cloud SQL Auth Proxy
Enable Private Google Access on the Cloud SQL subnet
Use VPC peering between Cloud Run's managed network and the VPC containing Cloud SQL
questions.explanation: Cloud Run requires a Serverless VPC Access connector to connect to resources inside a VPC using private IP addresses. The connector bridges Cloud Run's serverless environment to the VPC. Once connected, Cloud Run can reach Cloud SQL's private IP directly. ALL_TRAFFIC egress sends all outbound traffic through the VPC (needed if Cloud SQL private IP is used), but the VPC connector itself is the required component. Private Google Access is for Google APIs, not VPC resources. VPC Peering doesn't connect Cloud Run's managed environment.

questions.question: Your organization uses Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) for Compute Engine. You have a committed 100 vCPUs in us-central1 but consistently use 120 vCPUs. How are the 20 additional vCPUs billed?
options: At on-demand pricing for the 20 vCPUs above the commitment
At sustained use discount pricing for all 120 vCPUs
At on-demand pricing for all 120 vCPUs since the commitment is exceeded
The 20 extra vCPUs are blocked until the commitment is increased
questions.explanation: CUDs apply to committed resources at the discounted rate. Resources used above the commitment (the 20 extra vCPUs) are billed at on-demand pricing. GCP does not block usage above commitments or apply CUDs retroactively to the entire usage when commitments are exceeded. Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs) are separate from CUDs and cannot be combined with CUDs on the same resources.

questions.question: A company wants to implement a multi-project GCP environment where resources in Project A can communicate with resources in Project B using private IP addresses, without routing traffic through the internet. Which approach should they use?
options: VPC Network Peering between the VPC in Project A and the VPC in Project B
Cloud VPN between the two projects' VPCs
Shared VPC with Project A as host and Project B as service project
Cloud Interconnect between the two projects
questions.explanation: VPC Network Peering enables private IP connectivity between VPCs in different projects (or the same project) within GCP without traffic traversing the public internet. It's low-latency and uses Google's internal network. Cloud VPN encrypts traffic but routes it over the internet (or Google's network with a premium tier) and adds cost and latency. Shared VPC requires one project to be the host — not suitable if both projects are peers. Cloud Interconnect connects on-premises to GCP, not GCP projects to each other.

questions.question: A team is configuring an App Engine Flexible application. The application processes images and requires specific system-level libraries. The deployment is failing with a library not found error. Which App Engine Flexible feature should they use?
options: Use a custom Dockerfile to install the required system libraries in the container image
Switch to App Engine Standard and install libraries via requirements.txt
Configure app.yaml with a libraries section to specify system dependencies
Use Cloud Build to pre-install libraries before deployment
questions.explanation: App Engine Flexible supports custom Docker containers. A custom Dockerfile lets you install any system-level library (e.g., libavcodec, ImageMagick) as part of the container build. App Engine Standard uses a sandboxed environment where system-level library installation is not possible. The app.yaml libraries section only supports a limited set of pre-approved libraries in App Engine Standard. Cloud Build runs the build but the Dockerfile is what installs the libraries.

questions.question: A GKE cluster hosts a multi-tenant application. Each tenant's workload must not consume more than 4 vCPUs and 8 GB RAM in their namespace. Which Kubernetes resource enforces these limits at the namespace level?
options: ResourceQuota on each tenant namespace
LimitRange on each tenant namespace
PodDisruptionBudget per tenant namespace
NetworkPolicy restricting resource-consuming pods
questions.explanation: ResourceQuota enforces aggregate resource limits across all pods in a namespace — e.g., total CPU requests cannot exceed 4 vCPUs and total memory requests cannot exceed 8 GB across all pods in the namespace. LimitRange sets default and maximum limits per pod/container, not aggregate namespace limits. PodDisruptionBudget controls the minimum available pods during disruptions, not resource consumption. NetworkPolicy controls network traffic, not compute resource consumption.

questions.question: A company's load balancer is receiving HTTPS traffic. The security team requires TLS 1.3 only and must disable older TLS versions. Where should this SSL policy be configured?
options: Create an SSL policy with profile RESTRICTED or MODERN and minimum TLS version TLS_1_3, attach it to the target HTTPS proxy
Configure the SSL policy directly on the backend service
Set TLS version in the firewall rules for port 443
Configure the application servers to reject TLS 1.2 connections
questions.explanation: SSL policies in GCP control the TLS versions and cipher suites used by the load balancer's SSL/TLS negotiation. You attach the SSL policy to the target HTTPS proxy (or target SSL proxy). Setting minimum TLS version to TLS_1_3 disables older versions at the load balancer level. SSL policies are configured on target proxies, not backend services. Firewall rules operate at IP/port level, not TLS version. Configuring application servers is more complex and doesn't centralize enforcement.

questions.question: An organization wants to use Cloud DNS to serve different DNS records for the same domain name depending on whether the query originates from within a GCP VPC or from the internet. Which Cloud DNS feature enables this?
options: Split-horizon DNS using a public zone for internet queries and a private zone for VPC queries
Cloud DNS forwarding zones pointing to different authoritative servers
