DNS peering between the VPC and the public DNS namespace
Cloud DNS routing policies with geolocation rules
questions.explanation: Cloud DNS split-horizon DNS uses a public zone to serve external DNS queries (internet) and a private zone with the same domain name to serve internal queries from associated VPCs. Queries from within the VPC are answered by the private zone; internet queries get the public zone responses. Forwarding zones delegate resolution to other DNS servers, not serve different records. DNS peering shares private zones between VPCs, not between public and private. Routing policies do geographic load balancing within a single zone.

questions.question: A company wants to control costs on their GCP organization. They want to ensure that developers cannot create expensive resources like high-memory VMs (n1-ultramem series) or TPU nodes across all projects. Which mechanism prevents this across the entire organization?
options: Organization Policy constraint constraints/compute.restrictCloudSQLInstances is wrong; use constraints/compute.allowedMachineTypes and constraints/compute.restrictTPUInstances applied at the organization node
IAM conditions on compute.instances.create that restrict machine types for the developer role
Budget alerts configured to notify when expensive resources are created
Organization Policy constraints for allowed machine types and TPU restrictions applied at the organization level
questions.explanation: Organization Policy constraints/compute.allowedMachineTypes restricts which machine types can be created, and appropriate TPU-related constraints prevent TPU node creation. Applied at the organization level, they cascade to all folders and projects, preventing developers from creating restricted resource types regardless of their IAM permissions. IAM conditions are complex to maintain and can't easily restrict specific machine type series. Budget alerts are detective, not preventive. The first option incorrectly references an unrelated constraint.

questions.question: A company is migrating a stateful application to GKE. The application uses a database that writes data to local disk. Each pod needs its own dedicated storage volume that persists through pod restarts and rescheduling. Which Kubernetes resource type should you use?
options: StatefulSet with a volumeClaimTemplate using a StorageClass backed by persistent disks
Deployment with a PersistentVolumeClaim mounted as a shared volume
DaemonSet with host path volumes
Deployment with an emptyDir volume
questions.explanation: StatefulSets with volumeClaimTemplates create a unique PersistentVolumeClaim for each pod replica. The PVC (backed by a GCP Persistent Disk) persists through pod restarts and rescheduling — the same disk reattaches to the replacement pod. Deployments with a shared PVC would have all pods competing for a single disk (and ReadWriteMany is not supported by standard persistent disks). DaemonSet with hostPath tightly couples storage to specific nodes. emptyDir is ephemeral and lost when the pod restarts.

questions.question: A GKE cluster needs to isolate network traffic between namespaces. Pods in the frontend namespace should only communicate with pods in the backend namespace on port 8080. No other cross-namespace traffic is allowed. Which Kubernetes resource enforces this?
options: NetworkPolicy resources in the backend namespace allowing ingress from the frontend namespace on port 8080
RBAC ClusterRole restricting pod-to-pod communication between namespaces
GKE firewall rules blocking cross-namespace traffic at the VPC level
Anthos Service Mesh AuthorizationPolicy allowing only frontend-to-backend traffic
questions.explanation: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy in the backend namespace with an ingress rule selecting pods from the frontend namespace on port 8080, combined with a default-deny ingress NetworkPolicy, allows only the specified traffic. RBAC controls API server access, not pod-to-pod network traffic. VPC firewall rules operate on node IPs, not Kubernetes pod namespaces. Anthos Service Mesh AuthorizationPolicy also works but requires Anthos Service Mesh (Istio) to be installed, adding operational overhead.

questions.question: An engineering team uses Cloud Build for CI/CD. They need build steps to access a private Artifact Registry in a different project. The Cloud Build service account is in the source project. Which configuration grants the required access?
options: Grant the Cloud Build service account the roles/artifactregistry.reader role in the Artifact Registry project
Grant the Cloud Build service account the roles/viewer role on the Artifact Registry project
Create a service account key and store it in Cloud Build substitution variables
Enable Artifact Registry API sharing between the two projects
questions.explanation: Granting Cloud Build's service account the roles/artifactregistry.reader role in the target Artifact Registry project allows it to pull images and artifacts from that registry. This follows least privilege. roles/viewer is a primitive role that grants broader read access than needed. Service account keys are insecure and the security team should avoid them. There is no 'API sharing' between projects for Artifact Registry — IAM is the access control mechanism.

questions.question: A company wants to automate infrastructure provisioning when a developer merges a pull request to the main branch. The infrastructure code is in Terraform. Which GCP-native CI/CD setup achieves this?
options: Cloud Build trigger on the GitHub main branch merge event, running terraform plan and terraform apply steps
Cloud Scheduler triggering Cloud Functions to run Terraform on a schedule
Cloud Deployment Manager running on each pull request merge via webhooks
Vertex AI Pipelines orchestrating Terraform execution steps
questions.explanation: Cloud Build integrates with GitHub (and Cloud Source Repositories) with triggers that fire on branch push or PR merge events. A Cloud Build pipeline can run terraform plan for validation and terraform apply for provisioning. This is the standard GCP-native GitOps approach for Terraform. Cloud Scheduler is for time-based triggers, not event-based. Cloud Deployment Manager uses its own template format, not Terraform. Vertex AI Pipelines is for ML workflows.

questions.question: A company runs a production GKE cluster. They discover that some nodes are underutilized (running at 15% CPU and 20% memory). Which GKE feature consolidates workloads onto fewer nodes to reduce cost?
options: GKE Cluster Autoscaler with scale-down enabled and appropriate utilization thresholds
GKE Vertical Pod Autoscaler in RECREATE mode
Node auto-provisioning with minimum node pool size
GKE Workload Identity to reduce per-pod resource overhead
questions.explanation: GKE Cluster Autoscaler automatically removes underutilized nodes when their pods can be rescheduled to other nodes. The scale-down threshold (default 50% utilization) triggers node removal when nodes are below the threshold and their pods can fit on remaining nodes. This consolidates workloads and reduces node count. VPA adjusts resource requests per pod but doesn't remove nodes. Node auto-provisioning creates nodes automatically but doesn't remove underutilized ones independently. Workload Identity is for authentication, not resource optimization.

questions.question: A company provisions Cloud Run services using Terraform. They want to ensure that the Cloud Run service URL is output after provisioning and stored in Secret Manager for use by other services. Which Terraform workflow achieves this without hardcoding values?
options: Use a terraform output for the Cloud Run service URL and a google_secret_manager_secret_version resource to store it in Secret Manager
Manually copy the Cloud Run URL from the console and store it in Secret Manager
Use a Cloud Build step after Terraform apply to retrieve and store the URL
Configure a Cloud Run custom domain and use that hardcoded domain name
questions.explanation: Terraform's google_cloud_run_service resource exposes the service URL as an output attribute. A google_secret_manager_secret_version resource can reference this output to store the URL in Secret Manager within the same Terraform configuration, eliminating manual steps or hardcoding. Manual copy defeats IaC automation. Cloud Build post-processing works but adds complexity. Custom domains don't help with service discovery between services.

questions.question: Your organization wants to implement cost allocation for GCP spending across 10 business units. Each business unit has multiple projects. Which labeling strategy and billing export configuration should you use?
options: Apply business-unit labels to all GCP resources; export billing data to BigQuery and query by label for cost allocation reports
Create separate billing accounts per business unit
Use Cloud Billing subaccounts for each business unit with separate invoices
Use Committed Use Discounts per business unit for cost tracking
questions.explanation: Resource labels (e.g., business-unit: finance) propagate to BigQuery billing exports, enabling cost allocation queries by label. This is the standard GCP cost allocation approach without needing multiple billing accounts. Separate billing accounts per business unit is operationally complex and breaks centralized negotiated discounts. Subaccounts with separate invoices add billing complexity and are typically used for resellers. CUDs are discount mechanisms, not cost allocation tools.

questions.question: A company configures a Partner Interconnect connection. After provisioning VLAN attachments, BGP sessions are not establishing. The Cloud Router is in us-central1. Which steps should you verify first? Select TWO.
options: Verify that the BGP peer IP addresses in Cloud Router match the partner-provided BGP peer IPs
Verify that the Cloud Router ASN matches the partner's ASN exactly
Verify that the VLAN attachment is in the same region as the Cloud Router
Verify that firewall rules allow BGP traffic (TCP port 179) from the partner IP
questions.explanation: BGP session failures are most commonly due to mismatched peer IP addresses (the Cloud Router BGP peer IP must match what the partner provides) or regional mismatches (VLAN attachments must be in the same region as the Cloud Router). Cloud Router and partner ASNs do not need to match — BGP uses different ASNs for eBGP peering. BGP for Interconnect runs within Google's private network — firewall rules don't apply to BGP for Interconnect (unlike VPN).

questions.question: A company runs 500 Cloud SQL instances across 50 projects. They want centralized visibility into all instances' CPU, memory, and storage utilization without building custom tooling. Which GCP capability provides this?
options: Cloud Monitoring with a custom dashboard aggregating Cloud SQL metrics across all projects using a monitoring-scoped project
Cloud Asset Inventory export to BigQuery with SQL queries on resource metadata
Cloud SQL Admin API polling each instance for metrics
Cloud Logging log-based metrics aggregating Cloud SQL performance logs
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring supports monitoring-scoped projects (formerly known as workspace host projects) that aggregate metrics from multiple GCP projects. A single Monitoring scope can include all 50 projects, and a custom dashboard can display Cloud SQL CPU, memory, and storage metrics across all 500 instances. Cloud Asset Inventory provides resource metadata (configuration), not runtime performance metrics. Polling the Admin API is custom tooling the question says to avoid. Log-based metrics require Cloud SQL to log performance data, which is not the standard metric collection method.

questions.question: A team needs to implement a blue/green deployment for a Cloud Run service. They want to deploy a new revision, test it with 10% of traffic, then promote it to 100% if healthy. Which Cloud Run feature enables this workflow?
options: Cloud Run traffic splitting between revisions by percentage
Cloud Run tags to create named revision URLs for testing
Cloud Deploy pipeline with Cloud Run as a deployment target
Cloud Load Balancing weighted backend services pointing to different Cloud Run revisions
questions.explanation: Cloud Run natively supports traffic splitting between revisions by percentage. You can deploy a new revision and send 10% of traffic to it while keeping 90% on the current revision. After validation, update the split to 100% on the new revision. Cloud Run tags create named URLs for direct testing without affecting traffic split (useful for testing but not for gradual traffic shifting). Cloud Deploy orchestrates deployments but the traffic splitting is done via Cloud Run's native feature. Cloud Load Balancing doesn't route to individual Cloud Run revisions.

questions.question: A company's GKE cluster must comply with a security policy requiring that all container images come only from their private Artifact Registry. Images from Docker Hub or other public registries must be blocked. Which control enforces this?
options: Binary Authorization policy requiring attestation, combined with an Artifact Registry attestor that signs images from approved registries
Kubernetes admission webhook that validates image registry in pod specs
OPA Gatekeeper constraint template restricting image registry prefixes
GKE node pool with a custom node image that blocks external registry access at the network level
questions.explanation: OPA Gatekeeper with a ConstraintTemplate and Constraint can enforce that all container images in pod specs must start with an allowed registry prefix (e.g., us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/company-project/). This is the standard Kubernetes-native policy enforcement approach. Binary Authorization with attestors validates that images have been signed (attested) — it can enforce approved registries but requires attestation infrastructure. A custom admission webhook works but OPA Gatekeeper is the GKE-recommended policy engine. Network-level blocking is complex and doesn't check image spec before pod scheduling.

questions.question: A startup wants to use GCP's resource hierarchy to organize resources. They have three environments (dev, staging, prod) and two product lines (payments, marketplace). What is the recommended resource hierarchy?
options: Organization > two product-line folders > three environment folders per product line > projects per environment/product combination
Organization > three environment folders > projects per product line
Organization > projects directly, using labels to identify product lines and environments
Single project with separate VPCs and subnets per environment and product line
questions.explanation: The recommended hierarchy mirrors organizational structure: Organization → product-line folders (payments, marketplace) → environment sub-folders (dev, staging, prod) → projects. This enables IAM policies and Organization Policies to be applied at each level with inheritance, giving product teams control over their folders while platform teams manage org-level policies. Environments-first hierarchy makes cross-product policy management harder. Labels on projects in a flat hierarchy lose IAM inheritance benefits. A single project with VPCs is not scalable and lacks isolation.

questions.question: A company runs a GKE workload that processes 1,000 concurrent HTTP requests per second during business hours, and drops to near zero at night. They want to minimize cost during off-hours while maintaining fast scale-up. Which combination of GKE features achieves this?
options: HorizontalPodAutoscaler with KEDA scaling to zero on Pub/Sub message count, and Cluster Autoscaler to remove nodes when all pods scale down
Vertical Pod Autoscaler in AUTO mode to reduce resource requests during off-hours
GKE Autopilot with scale-to-zero node pools during off-hours
Scheduled scaling using CronJobs to adjust replica counts based on business hours
questions.explanation: KEDA can scale deployments to zero replicas when the Pub/Sub/HTTP queue is empty, eliminating pod costs. Cluster Autoscaler then removes empty nodes, eliminating node costs. On incoming traffic, KEDA scales pods up and Cluster Autoscaler adds nodes. This provides true scale-to-zero. VPA adjusts resource sizes, not replica counts to zero. GKE Autopilot doesn't have scale-to-zero for HTTP workloads. CronJobs with scheduled scaling are rigid and don't respond to actual traffic patterns.

questions.question: A company wants to test a change to their global load balancer URL map configuration before applying it to production. Which GCP feature allows safe testing of routing changes?
options: URL map validation and traffic director test with gcloud compute url-maps validate
Create a parallel load balancer with the new URL map and use DNS-based testing
Apply the URL map change to staging load balancer first and promote manually
Use Cloud Armor simulation mode to test URL routing rules
questions.explanation: GCP provides URL map validation (gcloud compute url-maps validate) that checks URL map configuration for errors without applying it. Additionally, URL map traffic simulation allows you to test how specific requests would be routed by a URL map before deploying. This is the safe, built-in testing mechanism. Creating a parallel load balancer is expensive and operationally complex. Staging promotion works but doesn't specifically test the URL map logic. Cloud Armor is for security policies, not URL routing.

questions.question: A company's Cloud Run service experiences high cold start latency (3-5 seconds). The service handles user-facing requests where latency is critical. Which Cloud Run configuration reduces cold start impact?
options: Set minimum instances to a non-zero value to keep instances warm
Increase maximum instances to handle load faster
Use CPU always allocated setting to keep CPUs running between requests
Increase the memory allocation to speed up container initialization
questions.explanation: Setting minimum instances (formerly min-instances) to at least 1 keeps one or more instances warm and ready, eliminating cold starts for those instances. New instances beyond the minimum will still cold start, but user-facing requests are served by warm instances first. Increasing maximum instances helps with scaling at high load but doesn't reduce cold starts. CPU always allocated keeps CPU running between requests for computation but doesn't prevent container cold starts. Memory allocation affects runtime performance, not cold start duration significantly.

questions.question: A team manages GCP resources using Cloud Deployment Manager. They notice that Deployment Manager deployments fail intermittently when API quotas are exceeded during large deployments. Which strategy reduces this issue?
options: Use Deployment Manager's preview mode to validate before deploying, and break large deployments into smaller sequential deployments
Increase all API quotas to maximum before deployment
Switch to Terraform which handles quota errors better
Deploy during off-peak hours to avoid quota contention
questions.explanation: Preview mode validates the deployment without executing API calls (catching syntax errors). Breaking large deployments into smaller sequential pieces reduces the burst of API calls, staying within quota limits. Increasing all quotas to maximum requires a Google approval process and may not be granted. Switching to Terraform is a larger migration effort and doesn't inherently handle quota errors better. Off-peak deployment reduces contention but doesn't solve the root cause.

questions.question: A company uses Shared VPC. A network administrator in the host project accidentally deleted a firewall rule that all service projects depend on. The deletion went unnoticed for 2 hours. Which GCP feature would have detected this change immediately?
options: Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity logs) capturing the firewall rule deletion event, with a log-based alert routing to PagerDuty
VPC Flow Logs showing traffic drops after the firewall rule was deleted
Cloud Monitoring uptime checks detecting connectivity loss
Security Command Center detecting misconfiguration via asset change monitoring
questions.explanation: Admin Activity audit logs capture all write operations on GCP resources, including firewall rule deletion, in near-real time. A log-based metric and alert on compute.firewalls.delete events can trigger immediate notification to on-call teams. VPC Flow Logs show traffic patterns but would show drops only if traffic actually failed, not the deletion event itself. Uptime checks detect service impact but lag behind the configuration change. Security Command Center is good for detecting misconfigurations but Admin Activity logs are the most direct and immediate.

questions.question: An organization wants to migrate workloads from AWS to GCP with minimal code changes. The application uses AWS SQS for message queuing and AWS S3 for object storage. Which GCP services are the direct equivalents?
options: Pub/Sub for message queuing and Cloud Storage for object storage
Cloud Tasks for message queuing and Cloud Storage for object storage
Pub/Sub for message queuing and Filestore for object storage
Cloud Spanner for message queuing and Cloud Storage for object storage
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub is the GCP equivalent of AWS SQS for managed, scalable message queuing. Cloud Storage is the GCP equivalent of AWS S3 for object storage with similar concepts (buckets, objects, access controls). Cloud Tasks is a managed task queue (closer to SQS FIFO or delayed delivery), but Pub/Sub is the primary equivalent. Filestore is a managed NFS file system, not object storage. Cloud Spanner is a relational database.

questions.question: A company wants to ensure that their GKE pods can only be scheduled on nodes with specific hardware (e.g., SSD local storage) and that the scheduler considers pod resource requests accurately. Which two Kubernetes features work together to achieve this? Select TWO.
options: Node labels and nodeSelector or nodeAffinity in pod specs to target SSD nodes
Pod resource requests and limits set accurately in pod specs for scheduler decisions
DaemonSet to deploy pods on all SSD nodes regardless of other workloads
PodAntiAffinity to keep pods away from non-SSD nodes
questions.explanation: Node labels (e.g., cloud.google.com/gke-local-ssd=true) combined with nodeSelector or nodeAffinity in pod specs ensure pods only schedule on SSD-equipped nodes. Accurate resource requests (CPU, memory) ensure the scheduler makes correct bin-packing decisions and doesn't over-commit nodes. DaemonSets deploy on all matching nodes regardless of need — not appropriate for selective scheduling. PodAntiAffinity repels pods from each other, not from non-SSD nodes.

questions.question: A company runs a GKE cluster in a private network. Cluster nodes have no external IPs. The cluster needs to pull container images from Docker Hub. The company does not want to mirror all Docker Hub images to Artifact Registry. Which network configuration enables Docker Hub access from private nodes?
options: Configure Cloud NAT on the VPC network to provide outbound internet access for the node subnet
Enable Private Google Access on the node subnet
Configure a Cloud VPN tunnel to Docker Hub's IP ranges
Use a GKE node pool with external IP addresses for image pulling only
questions.explanation: Cloud NAT allows private GKE nodes (without external IPs) to make outbound internet connections, including pulling images from Docker Hub. Cloud NAT translates the internal IPs to a NAT gateway IP for outbound connections. Private Google Access only provides access to Google APIs and services, not Docker Hub. A VPN tunnel to Docker Hub's IP ranges is not feasible (Docker Hub doesn't provide VPN endpoints). Adding external IPs to nodes increases the attack surface and is against security best practices.

questions.question: A developer needs to allow a Cloud Run service to read from a Cloud Storage bucket. Following the principle of least privilege, which IAM configuration is correct?
options: Create a dedicated service account for the Cloud Run service; grant roles/storage.objectViewer on the specific bucket to that service account
Grant roles/storage.objectViewer at the project level to the Cloud Run service account
Grant roles/editor at the project level to the Cloud Run default service account
Grant roles/storage.admin on the bucket to the Cloud Run service account for full access
questions.explanation: Least privilege requires a dedicated service account (not the default Compute Engine SA) with only the roles/storage.objectViewer role applied to the specific bucket (resource-level binding). Project-level bindings grant access to all buckets in the project — too broad. roles/editor is a primitive role granting write access to many GCP services — far too permissive. roles/storage.admin grants bucket management and deletion rights, which are unnecessary for read-only access.

questions.question: A company needs to grant a third-party vendor temporary access to specific GCP resources for 48 hours without creating a GCP account for the vendor. Which feature enables this?
options: Workforce Identity Federation with time-bound access using IAM conditions
Create a service account key and share it with the vendor for 48 hours
Add the vendor's personal Google account with a time-limited IAM binding
Create a temporary GCP project and transfer ownership to the vendor
questions.explanation: Workforce Identity Federation allows external identities (from corporate IdPs like Okta, Azure AD, or any OIDC/SAML provider) to access GCP resources without creating a Google account. IAM conditions with expiry timestamps enforce time-bound access (48-hour window). Service account keys are long-lived credentials that are difficult to revoke and are a security anti-pattern. The vendor may not have a Google account. Transferring project ownership is irreversible and excessive.

questions.question: A financial institution must ensure that cryptographic keys used to encrypt Cloud Storage data are stored in HSM-backed hardware and never leave the hardware module. They also require full key lifecycle control. Which Cloud KMS option meets these requirements?
options: Cloud KMS with Cloud HSM (Hardware Security Module) protection level for CMEK
Cloud KMS with default SOFTWARE protection level for CMEK
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) with keys stored on-premises
Google default encryption with AES-256 keys managed by Google
questions.explanation: Cloud HSM provides FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware security modules. Keys created with the HSM protection level are generated and used only within the HSM — they never leave the hardware module. Cloud KMS with CMEK gives full key lifecycle control (create, rotate, destroy, disable). SOFTWARE protection level stores keys in software, not HSM. CSEK keeps keys on-premises but requires sending them to Google per request. Google-managed encryption offers no key lifecycle control.

questions.question: An organization's security policy requires that Google support staff cannot access customer data in Cloud Storage even when investigating support tickets, unless the customer explicitly approves each access request. Which GCP feature enforces this?
options: Access Approval, which requires customer approval before Google support can access customer data
Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) with key access revocation
VPC Service Controls perimeter around Cloud Storage
Data Access audit logs monitoring all access to Cloud Storage objects
questions.explanation: Access Approval requires explicit customer approval for each Google support access request before support staff can access customer content. Without approval, even Google support cannot access the data. CMEK can prevent access by revoking keys but doesn't require per-request approval workflow. VPC Service Controls restricts access paths but doesn't control Google support access. Audit logs detect access after the fact — they're detective, not preventive.

questions.question: A company stores customer PII in BigQuery. They need to de-identify the data for use by the analytics team, replacing SSNs with format-preserving tokens that are consistent (same SSN always maps to same token) and reversible. Which Sensitive Data Protection technique should they use?
options: Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE) with FFX mode using Cloud KMS-wrapped key
Data masking with nullification (replacing SSNs with NULL)
Pseudonymization using SHA-256 hashing of the SSN
Bucketing to replace SSN ranges with category labels
questions.explanation: Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE) with FFX maintains the original format (SSNs remain SSN-formatted tokens), produces consistent tokens (deterministic — same SSN always maps to same token), and is reversible using the KMS-wrapped key. Nullification is irreversible and loses the data entirely. SHA-256 hashing is deterministic but not reversible and doesn't preserve format. Bucketing loses individual record-level tokens — analytics can't join on bucketed SSNs.

questions.question: A company's security team wants to prevent any data from leaving a set of GCP services (BigQuery, Cloud Storage) into the public internet while still allowing internal GCP-to-GCP data flows. Which GCP feature implements this data exfiltration prevention?
options: VPC Service Controls service perimeter around the projects containing BigQuery and Cloud Storage
Cloud Armor security policies on all load balancers in the projects
Private Google Access with firewall rules blocking egress to 0.0.0.0/0
Organization Policy constraints/compute.restrictCloudSQLInstances on egress
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls creates a security perimeter around GCP services. Data in BigQuery and Cloud Storage within the perimeter cannot be copied to resources outside the perimeter — preventing exfiltration to personal GCP projects or the internet. Access from outside requires explicit access levels (e.g., corporate network IP). Cloud Armor is a WAF protecting HTTP workloads, not data APIs. Private Google Access + firewall rules control VM-to-internet traffic, not API-to-internet data exfiltration. Organization Policy doesn't have data exfiltration controls for BigQuery/Storage.

questions.question: A company uses IAP to protect a web application running on GKE. Remote employees access the application over the internet. The security team wants to add an additional control ensuring that only managed corporate devices can access the application. Which feature extends IAP with device posture checks?
options: BeyondCorp Enterprise / Context-Aware Access with device certificate validation as an access level condition
Cloud Armor IP allowlist restricting access to corporate office IP ranges
IAP with SAML authentication using the corporate IdP
mTLS between the client device and the IAP-protected application
questions.explanation: BeyondCorp Enterprise (Context-Aware Access) extends IAP with access levels that include device posture checks such as corporate device certificate validation, OS version, screen lock status, and more. Only devices meeting the device policy can access the application. Cloud Armor IP allowlists restrict by network location, not device identity — remote employees may not always be on corporate networks. SAML with corporate IdP validates user identity, not device posture. mTLS is a transport security mechanism, not a device management policy.

questions.question: A company's application uses several GCP service accounts. A quarterly audit reveals that many service accounts have not been used in over 90 days. What is the recommended approach to reduce risk from these unused service accounts?
options: Disable unused service accounts and delete them after confirming no active dependencies
Rotate service account keys for all unused accounts
Remove all roles from unused service accounts but keep them active
Set IAM conditions on all service account roles to expire in 30 days
questions.explanation: Disabling unused service accounts immediately prevents credential misuse while allowing recovery if a dependency is discovered. After confirming no active use (checking Cloud Audit Logs for last-used timestamps), delete the service accounts. This removes the risk surface entirely. Rotating keys for unused accounts doesn't reduce access — the accounts still have permissions and can be compromised. Removing all roles is good but leaves orphaned service accounts that could be re-granted permissions. Setting expiry conditions is appropriate for human access, but service accounts used by systems may break.

questions.question: A company needs to store database credentials used by a GKE application. The credentials must be automatically rotated every 30 days. Access must be auditable and revocable per service. Which GCP service should they use?
options: Secret Manager with automatic rotation via Pub/Sub notifications triggering a Cloud Function to update credentials
Cloud KMS to encrypt credentials stored in Kubernetes secrets
Environment variables injected at deployment time from Cloud Build
Cloud Storage bucket with IAM-controlled access storing credentials as objects
questions.explanation: Secret Manager provides versioned secret storage with automatic rotation support (triggered via Pub/Sub notifications to Cloud Functions that generate new credentials and create new secret versions). Access is controlled by IAM per secret, is audited in Cloud Audit Logs, and can be revoked per service. Cloud KMS encrypts secrets but doesn't manage rotation or provide a secrets management API. Environment variables at deployment are static and require redeployment to rotate. Cloud Storage lacks secret versioning and rotation mechanisms.

questions.question: Your organization needs to ensure that all GCP API calls from production services originate only from within your corporate VPC and not from personal developer machines or CI/CD systems. Which GCP feature enforces this restriction?
options: VPC Service Controls access levels requiring VPC network origin for production service perimeter
Organization Policy constraints restricting which IP ranges can call GCP APIs
IAM deny policies preventing API calls from non-corporate IPs
Private Google Access restricting API calls to VPC-internal sources only
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls access levels can require that API calls to protected services originate from a specific VPC network (using the VPC Network condition). This ensures production BigQuery, Storage, and other perimeter-protected services can only be accessed from the corporate VPC. Organization Policy has no IP-based API call restrictions. IAM deny policies deny specific principals, not IP origins. Private Google Access routes VPC traffic to Google APIs but doesn't restrict which origins can call APIs.

questions.question: A company must comply with HIPAA requirements. They store Protected Health Information (PHI) in Cloud Storage and BigQuery. Which TWO GCP controls are required to support HIPAA compliance? Select TWO.
options: Enable Data Access audit logs for Cloud Storage and BigQuery to log all PHI access
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Google Cloud
Encrypt PHI with CSEK (Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys) in all services
Store PHI only in multi-region Cloud Storage buckets
questions.explanation: A signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Google Cloud is a legal HIPAA requirement — Google must be a covered business associate. Data Access audit logs for all PHI access provide the audit trail required by HIPAA's access monitoring requirements. CSEK is not required — Google's default AES-256 encryption satisfies HIPAA encryption requirements. Multi-region storage is a resilience decision, not a HIPAA requirement.

questions.question: A company's Cloud KMS key used for CMEK on a BigQuery dataset needs to be rotated. What happens to data encrypted with the old key version after rotation?
options: Existing data remains encrypted with the old key version; new writes use the new key version. Old key version remains enabled to decrypt existing data
All existing data is automatically re-encrypted with the new key version immediately
Existing data becomes inaccessible until manually re-encrypted with the new key
BigQuery automatically re-encrypts data in the background over 24 hours
questions.explanation: Cloud KMS key rotation creates a new primary key version but does not automatically re-encrypt existing data. Data encrypted with old key versions remains readable as long as those key versions remain enabled. New data is encrypted with the new primary version. Old key versions should remain enabled (not destroyed) until you are confident all data has been re-encrypted or is no longer needed. BigQuery does not automatically re-encrypt data on key rotation.

questions.question: A company wants to audit which users accessed specific BigQuery tables containing financial data. They need to capture individual query-level access, not just administrative operations. Which Cloud Audit Log type must be enabled?
options: Data Access audit logs with DATA_READ log type enabled for BigQuery
Admin Activity audit logs for BigQuery
System Event audit logs for BigQuery query execution
VPC Flow Logs to capture BigQuery API traffic
questions.explanation: Data Access audit logs with the DATA_READ type capture data read operations, including BigQuery table queries (jobservice.getQueryResults, tabledata.list). Admin Activity logs capture administrative operations (create/delete datasets, set IAM policies) but not queries. System Event logs are for GCP-initiated events, not user queries. VPC Flow Logs capture network traffic, not BigQuery API operations.

questions.question: A security team needs to forward all Cloud Audit Logs from 50 GCP projects to a centralized Cloud Storage bucket in a dedicated security project for long-term retention. Which approach achieves centralized log aggregation?
options: Create an aggregated log sink at the organization level routing Admin Activity and Data Access logs to the Cloud Storage bucket in the security project
Create individual log sinks in each of the 50 projects routing to the Cloud Storage bucket
Enable Cloud Logging's log export feature per project and aggregate via BigQuery
Use Cloud Monitoring alert policies to forward critical log events to the security project
questions.explanation: Organization-level aggregated log sinks capture logs from all projects, folders, and resources within the organization hierarchy in a single sink configuration. This is far more efficient than creating 50 individual sinks and automatically captures new projects added in the future. Individual per-project sinks require creating and managing 50 sinks and adding new ones as projects are created. BigQuery aggregation requires a separate ETL step. Cloud Monitoring alerts are for monitoring metrics, not log forwarding.

questions.question: A company uses Binary Authorization on their GKE cluster. They want to ensure that only container images that have passed a security vulnerability scan and been attested by their security team can be deployed. Which Binary Authorization policy setting achieves this?
options: Require attestation from a custom attestor linked to a Cloud KMS signing key; the security team signs images after vulnerability scanning
Enable the built-in Google vulnerability scanning attestor in Binary Authorization
Set Binary Authorization to dry-run mode to log but not block unattested images
Use the default Binary Authorization policy requiring images from Artifact Registry only
questions.explanation: Custom attestors linked to Cloud KMS signing keys allow the security team to cryptographically sign (attest) images after passing vulnerability scans. Binary Authorization verifies the attestation signature before allowing pod deployment. GKE does not have a built-in Google vulnerability scanning attestor in Binary Authorization — you must integrate Artifact Analysis and create custom attestors. Dry-run mode logs violations but doesn't block deployments. The default policy restricts registry source but not vulnerability scanning.

questions.question: A company's on-premises workloads need to call GCP APIs. Currently they use a service account key file stored on their servers. They want to eliminate static key files. Which solution removes the need for service account keys?
options: Workload Identity Federation with the on-premises workload's identity provider (e.g., AWS IAM, Azure AD, or corporate OIDC IdP) mapped to a GCP service account
Short-lived service account key rotation every 24 hours via a Cloud Function
Store the service account key in Secret Manager and retrieve it via the on-premises server at runtime
Use OAuth 2.0 3-legged flow with the service account for on-premises workloads
questions.explanation: Workload Identity Federation allows external identities (AWS roles, Azure managed identities, corporate OIDC tokens, SPIFFE/SPIRE) to exchange their native credentials for short-lived GCP access tokens without any service account key files. This is the Google-recommended replacement for service account keys in external environments. Short-lived key rotation still uses static keys (just refreshed). Secret Manager retrieval still requires service account keys. OAuth 2.0 3-legged flow requires user interaction, not suitable for workloads.

questions.question: A company needs to protect their public-facing web application from OWASP Top 10 attacks including SQL injection and cross-site scripting. The application is behind a Global External Application Load Balancer. Which GCP service provides this protection?
options: Cloud Armor with preconfigured WAF rules for OWASP Top 10
IAP (Identity-Aware Proxy) with security policies
Cloud CDN with request filtering rules
GKE Network Policy with L7 inspection
questions.explanation: Cloud Armor is GCP's WAF and DDoS protection service. It integrates with Global External Application Load Balancers and provides preconfigured WAF rules based on the ModSecurity Core Rule Set (CRS) for OWASP Top 10 attack patterns including SQLi and XSS. IAP controls authentication and authorization, not attack pattern detection. Cloud CDN caches content and has no WAF capabilities. GKE Network Policy operates at L3/L4, not L7 application layer.

questions.question: A company needs to prevent specific high-risk API calls (e.g., disabling audit logging) in all projects across their organization, even for users with Owner role. Which IAM feature enforces organization-wide prohibitions that override all other permissions?
options: IAM Deny policies at the organization level that deny the specific API methods to all principals including org admins
Organization Policy constraints restricting the specific API methods
Custom IAM roles that exclude high-risk permissions, assigned to all users
Privileged Access Management (PAM) requiring justification before high-risk API calls
questions.explanation: IAM Deny policies (introduced in 2022) can deny specific permissions to specific principals — including overriding allow policies. A deny policy at the organization level applies to all principals including those with Owner role. Organization Policy constraints restrict resource configuration, not API method calls at the IAM level. Custom roles don't override predefined role grants (Owner role). PAM adds approval workflows but doesn't block calls that happen without going through PAM.

questions.question: A company's security team needs to scan all files uploaded to Cloud Storage for malware before they are made available to users. Which architecture implements this?
options: Cloud Storage Pub/Sub notification triggers a Cloud Run job running a malware scanning container; move clean files to a published bucket and quarantine infected files
Enable Cloud Armor malware scanning on the Cloud Storage bucket
Use Sensitive Data Protection (DLP) to scan files for malware signatures
Configure Cloud Security Scanner to scan Cloud Storage buckets on a schedule
questions.explanation: The standard pattern for Cloud Storage malware scanning uses Pub/Sub event notifications when files are uploaded (storage.objects.finalize), triggering a Cloud Run job that runs a malware scanning tool (e.g., ClamAV or commercial AV). Clean files are moved to a production bucket; infected files go to quarantine. Cloud Armor is a WAF for HTTP traffic, not file scanning. Sensitive Data Protection scans for sensitive data (PII, credentials), not malware. Cloud Security Scanner tests web apps for vulnerabilities, not Cloud Storage files.

questions.question: A company uses Cloud Armor to protect their web application. They want to block traffic from a specific country and rate-limit requests from all other sources to 1,000 requests per minute per IP. Which Cloud Armor features implement this? Select TWO.
options: Geographic-based deny rule matching on the source country
Rate-based ban rule with a rate limit of 1,000 requests per minute per IP
Cloud Armor adaptive protection for automatic traffic analysis
IP allowlist rule permitting only known good IP ranges
questions.explanation: Cloud Armor security policies support geographic-based rules that match on origin country using geo() conditions, enabling country-level blocking. Rate-based ban rules enforce per-IP rate limits, blocking IPs that exceed the threshold. These two rules in a Cloud Armor security policy achieve both requirements. Adaptive protection provides automated DDoS detection and mitigation suggestions but doesn't implement the specific country block and rate limit rules. IP allowlists would block all traffic except known IPs, which is more restrictive than required.

questions.question: A company's security policy requires that no GCP service account should have the roles/owner or roles/editor primitive roles. Which tool would most efficiently detect and report violations across 200 projects?
options: Policy Intelligence's IAM recommender and Security Command Center's misconfiguration findings
Manual review of IAM policies in the GCP console across all 200 projects
Cloud Asset Inventory export to BigQuery with a query finding primitive role bindings
Terraform plan comparing current IAM to desired state
questions.explanation: Cloud Asset Inventory can export IAM policy bindings for all resources across all projects in an organization to BigQuery in a single operation. A SQL query can then find all service accounts with roles/owner or roles/editor bindings efficiently across all 200 projects. IAM Recommender suggests removing unused permissions but doesn't specifically flag primitive role violations. Manual console review is not scalable across 200 projects. Terraform plan only works for Terraform-managed resources and requires Terraform state to be current.

questions.question: A company wants to ensure that data stored in Cloud SQL is encrypted with keys they manage, and that they can immediately prevent Google from accessing the data by disabling the key. Which encryption option achieves this?
options: Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) with a Cloud KMS key; disabling or destroying the key prevents data decryption
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) stored in the customer's on-premises HSM
Google default encryption with TDE (Transparent Data Encryption) enabled in Cloud SQL
Application-level encryption using Cloud KMS before writing to Cloud SQL
questions.explanation: CMEK for Cloud SQL uses a Cloud KMS key managed by the customer. Disabling the key version prevents Cloud SQL from decrypting data — Google cannot access plaintext data when the key is disabled. Destroying the key permanently prevents decryption. CSEK is not natively supported for Cloud SQL (it's available for Compute Engine disks and Cloud Storage). Cloud SQL doesn't have traditional TDE. Application-level encryption works but doesn't prevent the database engine from accessing plaintext in memory.

questions.question: A security team discovers that a GKE cluster's nodes have unnecessary permissions because the default Compute Engine service account (with Editor role) is used. What is the recommended remediation?
options: Create a minimal custom service account for GKE nodes with only the required GKE node pool permissions, and use Workload Identity for pod-level GCP API access
Remove the Editor role from the default Compute Engine service account for the project
Enable VPC Service Controls to limit what the GKE nodes can access with Editor permissions
Use Binary Authorization to prevent deployments to nodes with Editor-role service accounts
questions.explanation: The recommended practice is to create a minimal custom service account for GKE nodes with only the permissions required for node operation (container.nodeServiceAccount role), and use Workload Identity Federation for pods that need GCP API access. This provides least-privilege at both node and pod levels. Removing Editor from the default SA may break other services using that SA in the project. VPC Service Controls limits API access paths but doesn't reduce the SA's IAM permissions. Binary Authorization validates image provenance, not SA permissions.

questions.question: A company needs to implement a Zero Trust architecture for their internal web applications. Employees work remotely worldwide. The solution must verify user identity and device posture for every request without VPN. Which GCP service implements this?
options: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) with BeyondCorp Enterprise context-aware access policies
Cloud VPN with multi-factor authentication at the VPN gateway
Cloud Armor IP allowlist allowing only known employee home IP ranges
Cloud Endpoints with API key authentication for all internal services
questions.explanation: IAP with BeyondCorp Enterprise implements Zero Trust by verifying user identity (via Google Identity or SAML/OIDC IdP) and device posture (corporate certificate, OS version, etc.) for every request. There is no VPN — access is granted based on identity and context, not network location. Cloud VPN is network-based access control, not Zero Trust. Cloud Armor IP allowlists are location-based, not identity and device-based. Cloud Endpoints uses API keys (static credentials), not contextual Zero Trust.

questions.question: A company's compliance team requires that all GCP resource changes in production projects must go through an approval workflow before being executed. This includes changes made via gcloud CLI, Terraform, and the console. Which approach enforces this requirement?
options: Implement Change Management via Privileged Access Management (PAM) requiring justification and approval before granting elevated roles
Require all changes to go through Cloud Deploy pipelines with manual approval gates
Use Organization Policy to require approval for all compute.instances.create operations
Implement Breakglass procedures with VPC Service Controls requiring access level approval
questions.explanation: Privileged Access Management (PAM) on GCP allows you to require time-bound, just-in-time elevation of IAM roles with justification and manager approval. In production, users only have read roles by default and must request elevated access through PAM approval workflows before making changes. Cloud Deploy approval gates only work for deployment pipelines, not gcloud/console changes. Organization Policy doesn't have approval workflow capability. VPC Service Controls access levels control access paths, not change approval.

questions.question: A company wants Security Command Center to automatically notify their security team and create JIRA tickets when high-severity findings are detected. Which integration approach should they use?
options: Configure Security Command Center to export findings to Pub/Sub, then use a Cloud Function subscriber to filter high-severity findings and create JIRA tickets via the JIRA API
Configure Security Command Center email notifications for each finding type directly
Use Cloud Monitoring alert policies triggered by Security Command Center finding metrics
Enable Security Command Center's built-in JIRA integration plugin
questions.explanation: Security Command Center can export findings to Pub/Sub in real time. A Cloud Function subscribed to the topic can filter for HIGH/CRITICAL severity findings and call the JIRA REST API to create tickets. This is the flexible, programmatic integration pattern. SCC email notifications exist but are limited and not suitable for JIRA ticket creation. Cloud Monitoring doesn't natively receive SCC finding data as metrics. There is no built-in SCC-JIRA plugin — integration requires custom development.

questions.question: A company processes cardholder data and must comply with PCI DSS. They use GCP services. Which TWO controls are specifically required by PCI DSS that GCP helps implement? Select TWO.
options: Encrypt cardholder data at rest and in transit (satisfiable with Cloud KMS CMEK and TLS)
Maintain a firewall configuration to protect cardholder data (satisfiable with VPC firewall rules and Cloud Armor)
Store cardholder data in Archive class Cloud Storage for audit purposes
Use only preemptible VMs to reduce attack surface
questions.explanation: PCI DSS Requirement 3 mandates encryption of stored cardholder data (Cloud KMS CMEK satisfies this) and Requirement 4 mandates encryption in transit (TLS satisfies this). PCI DSS Requirement 1 mandates firewalls to protect cardholder data (VPC firewall rules and Cloud Armor provide this). Cloud Storage Archive class is a storage tier decision, not a PCI DSS requirement. Preemptible VMs have nothing to do with PCI DSS compliance requirements.

questions.question: A company wants to prevent Cloud Storage objects from being made publicly accessible across all projects in their organization. Which control prevents this at scale?
options: Organization Policy constraint constraints/storage.publicAccessPrevention set to enforced at the organization level
IAM deny policy preventing storage.buckets.setIamPolicy for all users
VPC Service Controls preventing public internet access to Cloud Storage
Bucket-level uniform access control enabled on each bucket individually
questions.explanation: The Organization Policy constraint constraints/storage.publicAccessPrevention, when set to enforced at the organization level, prevents any bucket in any project in the organization from being made publicly accessible (blocks allUsers and allAuthenticatedUsers ACLs). It's a single policy that cascades to all projects. An IAM deny on setIamPolicy would prevent all IAM changes, including legitimate access grants. VPC Service Controls restrict access paths from outside the perimeter but don't prevent public bucket configuration. Per-bucket configuration doesn't scale to all projects.

questions.question: A company's GKE cluster serves both internal microservices and external customer APIs. The security team wants to ensure that the customer-facing API pods cannot communicate directly with internal service pods, even within the cluster. Which Kubernetes feature enforces this isolation?
options: NetworkPolicy resources with default-deny-all ingress and explicit allow rules only between permitted service pairs
Separate node pools for internal and external services with firewall rules between node pool subnets
GKE private cluster with authorized networks restricting access to the cluster API server
Anthos Service Mesh mutual TLS (mTLS) between all pods
questions.explanation: Kubernetes NetworkPolicies with a default-deny-all ingress policy in each namespace, combined with explicit allow rules for permitted communication paths, enforce pod-level network isolation regardless of whether pods are on the same node or different nodes. This prevents customer-facing API pods from directly communicating with internal service pods. Separate node pools with firewall rules doesn't isolate pod-to-pod traffic within the same VPC. Private cluster configuration restricts cluster API server access, not pod-to-pod traffic. mTLS encrypts and authenticates traffic but doesn't block unauthorized connections without authorization policies.

questions.question: A company discovers that a service account key was accidentally committed to a public GitHub repository 2 hours ago. What should be the FIRST action?
options: Immediately disable or delete the compromised service account to revoke all access
Rotate the service account key to generate a new key
Review Cloud Audit Logs to determine if the key was used maliciously
Remove the key from the GitHub repository and repository history
questions.explanation: The immediate priority in a credential compromise is to revoke access. Disabling or deleting the service account immediately invalidates the leaked key, preventing any further unauthorized use. Rotating the key generates a new key but leaves the compromised key valid until deleted. Reviewing audit logs is important for incident response but should happen after revoking access. Removing from GitHub history is important but slower and doesn't revoke access already obtained by anyone who forked or cloned the repo.

questions.question: A company uses Private Service Connect to access Google APIs privately. They want to ensure that DNS resolution for googleapis.com resolves to the Private Service Connect endpoint IP, not the public Google IP. Which Cloud DNS configuration achieves this?
options: Create a Cloud DNS private zone for googleapis.com and *.googleapis.com with A records pointing to the Private Service Connect endpoint IP
Create a Cloud DNS forwarding zone pointing googleapis.com to the Private Service Connect endpoint
Enable restricted.googleapis.com on the subnet and configure DNS to resolve to 199.36.153.4
Use Private Google Access and configure no DNS changes — it resolves automatically
questions.explanation: For Private Service Connect endpoints for Google APIs, you create a Cloud DNS private zone for googleapis.com associated with the VPC, with a wildcard A record (*.googleapis.com) pointing to the PSC endpoint IP. This ensures all googleapis.com DNS queries within the VPC resolve to the private endpoint. DNS forwarding zones delegate resolution outward, not override it. restricted.googleapis.com uses fixed IP ranges (not PSC). Private Google Access requires DNS overrides for on-premises access.

questions.question: A company stores sensitive research data in BigQuery. They need row-level security so that each research team can only query rows belonging to their team, without creating separate tables per team. Which BigQuery feature implements this?
options: BigQuery row-level security (row access policies) filtering rows based on the querying user's group membership
BigQuery authorized views creating a separate view per research team
BigQuery column-level security restricting access to the team identifier column
BigQuery Data Transfer Service copying data to team-specific datasets
questions.explanation: BigQuery row-level security uses row access policies that filter rows based on the querying user's identity or group membership. A policy like FILTER USING (team_id IN (SELECT team FROM allowed_teams WHERE user = SESSION_USER())) dynamically filters visible rows. Authorized views require creating and maintaining N views for N teams and don't scale well. Column-level security restricts which columns are visible, not which rows. Data Transfer Service copying data to separate datasets creates data silos and N-fold storage costs.

questions.question: A company needs to implement a custom firewall rule that blocks a specific threat actor's IP range (203.0.113.0/24) from accessing any HTTP/HTTPS services in their GCP project. The services are behind a Global External Application Load Balancer. Where should this rule be applied?
options: Cloud Armor security policy attached to the load balancer's backend service with a deny rule for 203.0.113.0/24
VPC ingress firewall rule blocking 203.0.113.0/24 on ports 80 and 443
Route table entries dropping traffic from 203.0.113.0/24
Cloud DNS RPZ (Response Policy Zone) blocking DNS resolution for the threat actor's domain
questions.explanation: For Global External Application Load Balancers, VMs receive traffic from Google's load balancer frontend IPs, not the original client IPs. VPC firewall rules cannot block specific client IPs at the load balancer level. Cloud Armor security policies are the correct control — they inspect the original client IP before the request reaches the backend and can deny traffic from specific IP ranges. Route tables can't block traffic based on source IPs in GCP. Cloud DNS RPZ is not available in GCP and doesn't block IP ranges.

questions.question: A company uses Forseti Security (Policy Intelligence) to detect IAM policy drift. They want to receive alerts when any IAM policy changes deviate from their approved baseline. Which GCP capability supports this requirement natively?
options: Policy Intelligence's IAM Recommender combined with Cloud Asset Inventory change feeds and Pub/Sub alerts
Security Command Center's IAM anomaly detector sending findings to Cloud Monitoring
Cloud Audit Logs monitoring setIamPolicy calls with log-based alerts
Forseti Security running on a dedicated Compute Engine VM with scheduled policy scans
questions.explanation: The most practical native GCP approach for immediate IAM drift detection is Cloud Audit Logs monitoring of setIamPolicy API calls. A log-based metric filters for setIamPolicy events and a Cloud Monitoring alert sends notifications when IAM changes occur. Policy Intelligence's IAM Recommender identifies excess permissions, not drift from a baseline. Security Command Center has IAM-related findings but not baseline drift detection. Forseti is an open-source tool (now less maintained) that requires separate infrastructure.

questions.question: A company's compliance requirement states that all data in Cloud Storage must be encrypted with a key that was created in the same geographic region as the data. Which GCP feature enforces this?
options: Create Cloud KMS key rings in the same region as Cloud Storage buckets and use CMEK with regional keys
Enable Cloud Storage uniform bucket-level access with regional key enforcement
Use Organization Policy to require CMEK on all Cloud Storage buckets with a specific key location
Configure Cloud Storage bucket locations to match KMS key ring regions in Terraform
questions.explanation: Cloud KMS key rings are created in specific regions. By creating KMS key rings in the same region as the Cloud Storage bucket and using CMEK with those regional keys, data residency of the encryption key is enforced. You can also use Organization Policy constraints/gcp.resourceLocations to restrict where key rings can be created, and constraints/gcp.restrictCloudKeyManagementService to require CMEK. Uniform bucket-level access controls IAM, not encryption key location. Terraform is an enforcement mechanism, not a policy control.

questions.question: A company has a VPC Service Controls perimeter protecting BigQuery and Cloud Storage. A data science team uses Vertex AI Notebooks (outside the perimeter) to train models on data in BigQuery. Queries are failing with 403 errors. Which configuration change resolves this without disabling the perimeter?
options: Add Vertex AI as a protected service in the perimeter and add the Vertex AI service account to the perimeter's access policy, or create an ingress/egress rule allowing Vertex AI to access BigQuery
Move the Vertex AI Notebook project inside the VPC Service Controls perimeter
Grant the data science team's service account the roles/bigquery.dataViewer role
Enable Private Google Access for Vertex AI to bypass VPC Service Controls
questions.explanation: When Vertex AI (outside the perimeter) tries to access BigQuery (inside the perimeter), the VPC Service Controls perimeter blocks the request. The solution is to either: (1) add Vertex AI's service agent to the perimeter access policy, or (2) create an ingress rule allowing Vertex AI service accounts access to BigQuery from outside the perimeter. Moving the Notebook project inside changes topology significantly. IAM roles don't override VPC Service Controls blocks. Private Google Access routes VPC traffic, it doesn't bypass VPC Service Controls.

questions.question: A company needs to ensure that all Cloud SQL instances in production projects automatically enable backup, have deletion protection enabled, and use SSL connections only. Which approach enforces these settings at scale without manual configuration?
options: Terraform modules enforcing these settings for all Cloud SQL provisioning, combined with Organization Policy custom constraints validating these settings on create
Manual configuration checklist reviewed quarterly by the DBA team
Cloud SQL Admin API audit via Cloud Asset Inventory with automated remediation Cloud Functions
Cloud Monitoring alerts on Cloud SQL instances missing these configurations
questions.explanation: Terraform modules with built-in defaults for backup, deletion protection, and SSL requirements prevent non-compliant provisioning for Terraform-managed infrastructure. Organization Policy custom constraints (using CEL expressions) can enforce these settings at the API level, blocking non-compliant Cloud SQL creation regardless of how it's provisioned (console, gcloud, Terraform). Manual checklists are not scalable. Cloud Asset Inventory with remediation is detective and reactive, not preventive. Monitoring alerts are detective, not preventive.

questions.question: A company processes payment data and must ensure that data in transit between their on-premises payment processor and GCP Cloud SQL is encrypted. Cloud SQL is connected via Dedicated Interconnect. Is data encrypted on Dedicated Interconnect by default?
options: No, Dedicated Interconnect traffic is not encrypted by default; MACsec or application-level TLS must be implemented for encryption
Yes, Dedicated Interconnect automatically encrypts all traffic with AES-256
Yes, Google encrypts all inter-datacenter traffic including Interconnect automatically
No, but VPN tunnels over Interconnect are required for encryption
questions.explanation: Dedicated Interconnect traffic is NOT encrypted by default — it traverses physical fiber and Google's network without automatic encryption. For compliance requirements, you must implement either MACsec (Layer 2 encryption on the Interconnect link) or application/transport-layer encryption (TLS for Cloud SQL SSL connections). Google does encrypt inter-datacenter traffic within their internal network, but this is after the Interconnect handoff. VPN over Interconnect (Cloud VPN on top of Interconnect) is an alternative but adds overhead.

questions.question: A company wants to implement a custom role that allows users to create and list Compute Engine instances but prevents them from deleting instances. Which IAM configuration achieves this with minimum permissions?
options: Custom role with permissions: compute.instances.create, compute.instances.list, compute.instances.get, and required compute.* permissions for instance creation
Grant roles/compute.instanceAdmin and add an IAM deny policy for compute.instances.delete
Grant roles/compute.viewer and roles/compute.instanceAdmin.v1 together
Custom role inheriting from roles/compute.instanceAdmin with compute.instances.delete removed
questions.explanation: A custom role with precisely the required permissions (compute.instances.create, compute.instances.list, compute.instances.get, plus supporting permissions like compute.machineTypes.list, compute.networks.use, etc.) is the minimum-privilege approach. IAM deny policy + roles/compute.instanceAdmin works but grants all instanceAdmin permissions and relies on the deny to remove delete — not as clean as a purpose-built custom role. Viewer + instanceAdmin gives too many permissions. Custom roles cannot 'inherit' from predefined roles — you must specify each permission.

questions.question: An organization needs to demonstrate to auditors that no data in BigQuery is exfiltrated outside their organization's GCP projects. Which combination of controls provides the strongest evidence for this audit? Select TWO.
options: VPC Service Controls perimeter preventing data copy operations to projects outside the perimeter
Cloud Audit Logs Data Access logs capturing all BigQuery table reads and exports
BigQuery authorized datasets restricting which datasets can be queried cross-project
Cloud Armor blocking BigQuery API calls from external IP addresses
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls prevents data exfiltration by blocking BigQuery copy/export operations to destinations outside the perimeter — this is the preventive control that auditors want to see. Data Access audit logs provide the detective control and audit trail showing all data access events, enabling auditors to verify no unauthorized access or exfiltration occurred. Authorized datasets control cross-project query access within GCP but don't prevent exfiltration. Cloud Armor doesn't control BigQuery API access (BigQuery APIs are not behind a load balancer).

questions.question: A company uses Secret Manager to store API keys used by multiple Cloud Run services across different projects. They want to grant each Cloud Run service access to only its specific secrets, not all secrets. Which IAM configuration achieves this?
options: Grant roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor to each Cloud Run service account at the individual secret resource level (resource-level IAM binding)
Grant roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor at the Secret Manager project level to all Cloud Run service accounts
Grant roles/secretmanager.viewer at the project level and filter secrets by naming convention
Use a single shared service account for all Cloud Run services with access to all secrets
questions.explanation: Secret Manager supports resource-level IAM bindings on individual secrets. Granting roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor to each Cloud Run service account on only its specific secret implements least-privilege secret access. Project-level secretAccessor grants access to all secrets in the project — too broad. roles/secretmanager.viewer only allows listing/describing secrets, not accessing their values. A shared service account violates isolation and least-privilege principles.

questions.question: A company's SOC 2 audit requires evidence that production environment access is logged, access is granted through formal approval, and access is reviewed quarterly. Which combination of GCP features addresses all three requirements?
options: Privileged Access Management (PAM) for approval workflow + Cloud Audit Logs for access logging + Cloud Asset Inventory IAM snapshots for quarterly review
Cloud Audit Logs for all three requirements
IAP for production access + Cloud Monitoring for access metrics + Manual IAM reviews
Service account keys with rotation + Audit logs + Quarterly key inventory
questions.explanation: PAM provides the formal approval workflow for production access grants (addresses formal approval requirement). Cloud Audit Logs captures all access events (addresses logging requirement). Cloud Asset Inventory can export IAM policy snapshots for quarterly drift analysis and access reviews (addresses quarterly review requirement). Cloud Audit Logs alone doesn't provide the approval workflow or easy quarterly review capability. IAP + Monitoring is partial but lacks a formal approval workflow. Service account keys are an anti-pattern and don't provide the approval workflow.

questions.question: A company discovers that a GKE pod was compromised and exfiltrated data to an external IP. They want to prevent pods from making outbound connections to any destination not on an approved list. Which control implements this?
options: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy with a default-deny egress rule and explicit allow rules for approved destinations
Cloud Armor egress rules blocking unauthorized outbound connections from GKE pods
VPC firewall rules with deny-egress for the node subnet to all destinations
GKE Binary Authorization preventing deployment of unauthorized container images
questions.explanation: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy supports egress rules. A default-deny-all egress policy combined with explicit allow rules for approved internal services and external IPs controls outbound pod connections at the Kubernetes network level. Cloud Armor is an ingress WAF, it doesn't control egress traffic from pods. VPC firewall rules with deny-egress on the node subnet would block all GKE node traffic, including legitimate Kubernetes control plane communication. Binary Authorization validates image integrity, not runtime network connections.

questions.question: A company wants to use Security Command Center Premium to automatically detect when a Compute Engine VM has an open firewall rule allowing SSH (port 22) access from 0.0.0.0/0. Which SCC feature provides this detection?
options: Security Health Analytics finding for 'Open SSH port' (OPEN_SSH_PORT) detecting overly permissive firewall rules
Event Threat Detection monitoring VPC firewall change audit logs
Web Security Scanner scanning for open SSH ports
Container Threat Detection monitoring GKE nodes for SSH exposure
questions.explanation: Security Health Analytics in Security Command Center Premium continuously scans GCP resource configurations and generates findings for misconfigurations. The OPEN_SSH_PORT finding specifically detects firewall rules that allow SSH access from 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0. Event Threat Detection analyzes logs for suspicious runtime events (not configuration misconfigurations). Web Security Scanner tests web applications for vulnerabilities, not firewall configurations. Container Threat Detection monitors GKE runtime, not firewall rules.

questions.question: An organization's security team wants to ensure that GCP organization administrators cannot bypass audit logging by disabling Data Access logs for critical services. Which GCP feature prevents disabling of audit logs?
options: Cloud Audit Logs locks: grant _Required log bucket locking to prevent deletion or modification of Admin Activity and System Event logs
IAM deny policy preventing logging.logServices.disable for all principals
Organization Policy constraint requiring audit logging to be enabled
VPC Service Controls perimeter protecting Cloud Logging from configuration changes
questions.explanation: The _Required log bucket in Cloud Logging stores Admin Activity and System Event logs and cannot be disabled — these logs are always on. The _Required bucket can be locked (log bucket lock feature) to prevent any modifications or deletions. Additionally, Data Access logs configuration can be protected by restricting who can modify IAM audit config. An IAM deny on logging.logServices.disable would broadly block log management. Organization Policy doesn't have an audit logging enforcement constraint. VPC Service Controls protects data exfiltration, not audit log configuration.

questions.question: A company must ensure that personally identifiable information (PII) in Cloud Storage files is automatically detected and never written to a production BigQuery table. Which GCP service and architecture achieves this?
options: Sensitive Data Protection (DLP) inspection job scanning Cloud Storage files before Dataflow ingestion; Dataflow pipeline calls DLP and drops or masks PII-containing records before writing to BigQuery
BigQuery column-level security automatically detecting and blocking PII column writes
Cloud Armor WAF rules detecting PII patterns in HTTP request bodies
Firestore triggers using DLP API to scan documents before Cloud Storage writes
questions.explanation: Sensitive Data Protection (formerly DLP) provides inspection APIs that detect PII (names, SSNs, credit card numbers, etc.) in text, files, and structured data. In a Dataflow pipeline, each record is inspected via the DLP API before being written to BigQuery — PII-containing records are either dropped or de-identified. BigQuery has no built-in PII auto-detection for blocking writes. Cloud Armor inspects HTTP request metadata, not file contents for data governance. Firestore triggers and DLP don't apply to Cloud Storage writes.

questions.question: A company needs their Cloud Run services in one project to communicate with Cloud Run services in another project over private networking, without traffic leaving Google's network. Which approach enables this?
options: Configure Serverless VPC Access connectors in both projects, peer the VPCs, and use internal Cloud Run URLs
Use Cloud Run's built-in service-to-service authentication via OIDC tokens over the public internet
Implement Cloud Interconnect between the two projects for private service communication
Use Private Service Connect to expose Cloud Run services privately across projects
questions.explanation: Private Service Connect allows Cloud Run services (or any services) to be exposed as private endpoints accessible from other VPCs/projects without traffic leaving Google's network. The consuming project creates a PSC endpoint in its VPC that forwards to the producer's Cloud Run service. Serverless VPC Access connectors with VPC peering works but is more complex (peered VPCs have non-transitive routing limitations). OIDC authentication over the public internet doesn't keep traffic private. Cloud Interconnect connects on-premises networks to GCP, not GCP projects to each other.

questions.question: A company wants to implement a data classification system for Cloud Storage objects where objects tagged as CONFIDENTIAL are automatically encrypted with a stricter key and objects tagged as PUBLIC are served without encryption overhead. Which GCP feature helps automate classification-based encryption policies?
options: Sensitive Data Protection (DLP) inspection scans on Cloud Storage, tagging objects, and a Cloud Function triggering re-encryption with appropriate KMS keys based on DLP findings
Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management rules applying different encryption keys based on object age
Cloud KMS key policies automatically selecting keys based on Cloud Storage object metadata labels
BigQuery Data Policy applying CMEK based on column classification tags
questions.explanation: DLP inspection scans of Cloud Storage objects detect sensitive content and generate findings. A Cloud Function triggered by DLP findings can apply custom metadata tags and invoke the Cloud Storage rewrite API with a different CMEK key appropriate for the classification. This is the closest to automated classification-based encryption on GCP. Cloud Storage Lifecycle Management applies to storage class and deletion, not encryption keys. Cloud KMS doesn't have policies that auto-select keys based on storage metadata. BigQuery Data Policy applies to BigQuery columns, not Cloud Storage.

questions.question: A company's security audit finds that several GCP service accounts have not been used in 180 days but still have project-level Editor roles. What is the recommended remediation approach following Google's security best practices?
options: Disable the unused service accounts, then work with application owners to determine if they are needed; if not, delete them and remove their IAM bindings
Immediately delete all unused service accounts and their IAM bindings
Apply IAM conditions with expiry to limit the unused service accounts' permissions
