Use the IAM Recommender to automatically remove the Editor roles and replace with recommended minimal roles
questions.explanation: The recommended approach is to disable first (not delete), allowing recovery if an application dependency is discovered. Audit logs can be checked for recent usage. After confirming with application owners that the service accounts are truly unused, delete them and clean up IAM bindings. Immediate deletion risks breaking systems if the service accounts have undocumented dependencies. IAM conditions with expiry still leave active (if time-limited) permissions. IAM Recommender removes excess permissions but doesn't deactivate or clean up the service accounts themselves.

questions.question: A company wants to implement a security monitoring solution that automatically detects when a GKE pod executes an unexpected binary or spawns a shell process, indicating a potential container escape or runtime attack. Which GCP security feature provides this detection?
options: Security Command Center's Container Threat Detection (KTD) monitoring GKE node kernel events for suspicious process execution
Binary Authorization preventing deployment of images with known vulnerable packages
GKE Audit Logs monitoring Kubernetes API calls for suspicious pod exec commands
Cloud Armor detecting malicious payloads in HTTP requests to GKE services
questions.explanation: Container Threat Detection (part of Security Command Center Premium) uses a DaemonSet on GKE nodes that monitors kernel-level events (system calls) to detect suspicious runtime behaviors such as unexpected binary execution, reverse shell spawning, and container escape attempts. It generates findings in Security Command Center. Binary Authorization validates images at deployment time, not runtime behavior. GKE Audit Logs capture Kubernetes API calls (like kubectl exec) but not process execution within containers. Cloud Armor is a WAF inspecting HTTP requests, not container runtime behavior.

questions.question: Your company runs many Compute Engine VMs with predictable, steady-state workloads that run 24/7. You want to minimize cost without changing instance types. Which pricing model provides the greatest discount for these workloads?
options: Sustained Use Discounts applied automatically by GCP
3-year Committed Use Discounts for a specific machine type and region
1-year Committed Use Discounts for a specific machine type and region
Spot VMs with a restart policy to handle preemptions
questions.explanation: 3-year Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) provide up to 70% discount for committing to a specific machine type and region for 3 years, making them the best option for predictable 24/7 workloads. 1-year CUDs offer around 37% discount — less than 3-year. Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs) are automatic but only provide up to 30% for full-month usage. Spot VMs are cheapest but can be preempted, making them unsuitable for workloads that must run continuously.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A data engineering team runs nightly BigQuery batch jobs that process large datasets. The jobs are non-urgent and can tolerate delays. What is the most cost-effective BigQuery pricing approach for these workloads?
options: On-demand pricing, paying per TB scanned
BigQuery flat-rate pricing with slot reservations
BigQuery Reservations with a BATCH edition assignment
BigQuery BI Engine for accelerated in-memory processing
questions.explanation: BigQuery Reservations with a BATCH assignment is ideal for non-urgent batch workloads. BATCH jobs run using idle capacity in the reservation and are queued when capacity is unavailable, providing cost savings over INTERACTIVE mode. On-demand pricing charges per TB scanned and can be expensive for large datasets run frequently. Flat-rate pricing is cost-effective but charges for reserved slots even when idle. BI Engine accelerates query performance for dashboards but is not optimized for cost reduction on batch jobs.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your BigQuery table receives streaming inserts throughout the day and grows to 500GB. Analysts frequently query only the last 7 days of data. Which optimization reduces scan costs most effectively?
options: Cluster the table by the timestamp column
Partition the table by ingestion time and use partition expiration
Enable BigQuery BI Engine on the table
Export older data to Cloud Storage and use external tables
questions.explanation: Partitioning by ingestion time allows queries filtered to the last 7 days to scan only those partitions, dramatically reducing the bytes billed. Partition expiration automatically removes old partitions, reducing storage costs as well. Clustering alone does not reduce the number of partitions scanned — it sorts within partitions. BI Engine accelerates query performance using in-memory caching but does not reduce bytes scanned for billing. Exporting to Cloud Storage and using external tables adds complexity and latency without the same cost benefit as native partitioning.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A developer submits BigQuery queries using SELECT * on a 10TB table with 200 columns, but only needs 5 columns. What is the best recommendation to reduce query costs?
options: Create a materialized view with only the required columns
Replace SELECT * with explicit column names to enable column pruning
Cluster the table by the 5 required columns
Move the table to BigQuery Omni for cross-cloud cost optimization
questions.explanation: BigQuery charges based on bytes scanned. Using explicit column names instead of SELECT * enables column pruning — BigQuery only reads the 5 requested columns, reducing the scanned bytes from 10TB to a fraction of that. Materialized views can help but add maintenance overhead and don't directly address the root cause. Clustering helps when filtering by the clustered columns but does not reduce bytes scanned when all rows are returned. BigQuery Omni is for running queries across other clouds, not for cost optimization of existing GCP tables.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization stores 100TB of log data in Cloud Storage Standard class. Logs older than 30 days are rarely accessed, and logs older than 90 days are never accessed. What is the most cost-effective lifecycle configuration?
options: Transition to Nearline after 30 days, then Coldline after 90 days
Transition to Coldline after 30 days, then Archive after 90 days
Enable Autoclass on the bucket and let GCP manage transitions automatically
Transition to Nearline after 30 days, delete after 90 days
questions.explanation: Transitioning to Nearline after 30 days (minimum 30-day storage, lower cost, small retrieval fee) and to Coldline after 90 days (minimum 90-day storage, very low cost, retrieval fee) matches the access pattern and minimizes cost. Coldline after 30 days would incur a 90-day minimum storage charge for objects deleted before that. Archive class has a 365-day minimum storage commitment, which is inappropriate if the data might be retrieved sooner. Autoclass is a valid option but requires the bucket to be newly configured and cannot optimize based on known access patterns as precisely as explicit lifecycle rules.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company is concerned about high Cloud Storage retrieval costs for their Coldline bucket. Upon investigation, most retrievals are automated pipeline reads of recent data. What should be done to optimize costs?
options: Enable Cloud CDN in front of Cloud Storage to cache frequently accessed objects
Move recently written objects to Standard class and use lifecycle rules to transition older objects to Coldline
Switch the bucket storage class from Coldline to Nearline
Enable Autoclass on the bucket to automatically downgrade storage class
questions.explanation: The optimal solution is to keep recent data in Standard class (no retrieval fees) and transition older, infrequently accessed data to Coldline via lifecycle rules. This avoids retrieval fees for recently written data while still saving on storage costs for cold data. Enabling CDN helps reduce egress but does not eliminate Coldline retrieval fees for uncached objects. Switching entirely to Nearline reduces retrieval costs compared to Coldline but still incurs fees and increases per-GB storage cost for cold data. Autoclass automatically transitions objects but cannot distinguish between objects based on their write time versus access needs as precisely as explicit lifecycle rules.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your application serves static assets globally to users in North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Cloud Storage egress costs are high. What is the most effective way to reduce these costs?
options: Use multi-region Cloud Storage buckets instead of regional buckets
Enable Cloud CDN with Cloud Load Balancing to cache assets at edge PoPs
Replicate assets to regional buckets in each geography and route users via Cloud DNS geolocation
Use Cloud Armor to cache responses at the network edge
questions.explanation: Cloud CDN caches content at Google's edge Points of Presence (PoPs) globally. Once an asset is cached, subsequent requests are served from the PoP without hitting the origin (Cloud Storage), eliminating egress charges from Cloud Storage for those requests. CDN egress rates are also lower than origin egress rates. Multi-region buckets improve availability but do not reduce egress costs. Replicating to regional buckets requires managing replication and does not reduce overall egress. Cloud Armor provides DDoS protection and WAF, not content caching.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A GKE cluster runs batch machine learning training jobs that are highly parallelizable, can tolerate interruption, and run for 2-4 hours. Which node configuration minimizes cost while meeting these requirements?
options: Standard nodes with 1-year Committed Use Discounts
Spot VMs as GKE node pool with checkpointing enabled in the ML framework
Preemptible VMs as GKE node pool with automatic node repair
GKE Autopilot with Spot Pod scheduling
questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot with Spot Pods provides the lowest cost for batch ML workloads. In Autopilot, you pay per pod resource consumption (not per node), and Spot Pods receive up to 91% discount. The Autopilot model also eliminates node management overhead. Spot VMs as a Standard node pool (option B/C) also work well, but the distinction between Spot VMs and Preemptible VMs is important — Spot VMs replaced preemptible VMs and have no maximum 24-hour limit, making them strictly better. Standard nodes with CUDs don't provide the same level of discount as Spot. Between options B and D, GKE Autopilot Spot Pods are the most cost-optimized because billing is at pod granularity, not node granularity.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your Cloud Run service currently has min-instances set to 0 and is experiencing cold start latency of 3-4 seconds on initial requests. Requests arrive sporadically throughout the day. What is the most cost-effective way to reduce cold start latency while minimizing cost?
options: Set min-instances to 10 to always have capacity available
Set min-instances to 1 to keep one warm instance available at all times
Enable CPU always-on to allow background processing between requests
Switch from Cloud Run to GKE to have persistent pods
questions.explanation: Setting min-instances to 1 keeps exactly one warm instance available, eliminating cold starts for most traffic patterns at minimal cost. You are only charged for the idle time of one instance. Setting min-instances to 10 keeps 10 instances warm unnecessarily — expensive for sporadic traffic. CPU always-on charges for CPU during idle time even with min-instances=0, which helps CPU-intensive initialization but still has cold starts. Switching to GKE eliminates cold starts but adds significant operational overhead and cost for node pools.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A Managed Instance Group (MIG) currently scales based on CPU utilization at 70%. During peak hours the MIG scales up slowly, causing latency spikes before enough instances are provisioned. What autoscaling improvement should be implemented?
options: Lower the CPU utilization target threshold to 50% to trigger scaling earlier
Add a Pub/Sub queue depth metric as an additional autoscaling signal and configure predictive autoscaling
Switch to using a schedule-based autoscaling policy for peak hours
Increase the maximum number of instances in the MIG
questions.explanation: Adding Pub/Sub queue depth as a custom metric allows the MIG to scale based on actual demand signals before CPU becomes saturated. Predictive autoscaling uses historical patterns to proactively add capacity before predicted peaks. Together these address the slow scale-up problem. Lowering the CPU threshold to 50% helps but may lead to over-provisioning during normal hours. Schedule-based autoscaling works only if peaks are at known fixed times, not demand-driven. Increasing the maximum instances doesn't fix slow scale-up; it only increases the ceiling.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your team wants to identify idle Compute Engine VMs to reduce costs. Which GCP service provides automated recommendations for idle VMs and right-sizing opportunities without requiring custom scripts?
options: Cloud Asset Inventory with custom CAI export queries
Google Cloud Recommender with the Idle VM Recommender and VM Machine Type Recommender
Cloud Billing export to BigQuery with custom cost analysis queries
Operations Suite uptime checks with alerting on low CPU VMs
questions.explanation: Google Cloud Recommender provides the Idle VM Recommender, which identifies VMs with low utilization over the past 14 days and recommends deletion, and the VM Machine Type Recommender, which suggests right-sizing to smaller instance types. These recommendations are automatically generated and accessible via the Console or API without any custom scripting. Cloud Asset Inventory provides resource inventory data but requires custom queries. Billing export to BigQuery provides cost data but not utilization-based recommendations. Operations Suite uptime checks monitor availability, not resource utilization efficiency.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company needs to allocate cloud costs to 5 business units that share a single GCP project. The finance team needs monthly cost breakdowns per business unit. What is the best approach?
options: Create separate GCP projects for each business unit
Apply resource labels to all billable resources with a business-unit key and export billing data to BigQuery for analysis
Use Cloud Billing sub-accounts to separate costs per business unit
Create a custom billing dashboard in Looker Studio connected to billing export
questions.explanation: Resource labels (key-value pairs applied to GCP resources) are propagated to Cloud Billing export data. By consistently applying a 'business-unit' label to all resources, the finance team can analyze costs per business unit from the BigQuery billing export. This is the recommended FinOps approach when multiple teams share a project. Creating separate projects is cleaner for isolation but requires refactoring the existing architecture. Sub-accounts split billing at the project or account level, not at the resource level within a shared project. A Looker Studio dashboard is useful for visualization but depends on the billing data having the right labels.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization wants to receive an alert when monthly cloud spend approaches $50,000. Which Cloud Billing feature should you configure?
options: Cloud Monitoring alerting policy on billing metric thresholds
Budget alerts with notification thresholds at 50%, 90%, and 100% of the $50,000 budget
Cloud Billing export to BigQuery with a scheduled query that emails alerts
Pub/Sub notification from billing export triggered when spend exceeds the threshold
questions.explanation: Cloud Billing budget alerts allow you to set a budget amount ($50,000) and configure notification thresholds (e.g., 50%, 90%, 100% of budget) that trigger email notifications to billing admins and optionally Pub/Sub messages for automation. This is the native GCP mechanism for spend alerting. Cloud Monitoring does not directly expose billing spend as a native alerting metric. BigQuery export with scheduled queries is a custom solution requiring more setup. Pub/Sub notifications from billing export can be configured but require additional Cloud Functions/workflows to process — the budget alert feature handles this natively.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: An analytics team runs hundreds of ad-hoc BigQuery queries daily. Some queries accidentally scan entire tables instead of using partition pruning, causing unexpectedly high costs. What preventive control should be implemented?
options: Enable BigQuery slot reservations to cap the compute used
Set per-query maximum bytes billed to reject queries that would scan too much data
Require all queries to go through a query review process before execution
Enable BigQuery column-level security to restrict access to large tables
questions.explanation: BigQuery allows setting a maximum bytes billed per query at the user, dataset, or query level. When a query would scan more than the configured limit, BigQuery returns an error without executing the query and incurring charges, preventing runaway cost from accidental full-table scans. Slot reservations cap compute speed but not the bytes scanned or cost per query. A manual query review process is operationally burdensome at scale. Column-level security restricts access to sensitive columns but does not prevent expensive full-table scans on permitted columns.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your GKE cluster has variable workloads with unpredictable node resource requirements. You want nodes to be provisioned automatically with the right machine type for each workload without pre-defining node pools. Which GKE feature enables this?
options: Cluster Autoscaler with multiple node pools of different machine types
Node Auto-Provisioning (NAP) which automatically creates and deletes node pools based on pending pod requirements
GKE Autopilot mode with custom resource requests
Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) combined with Cluster Autoscaler
questions.explanation: Node Auto-Provisioning (NAP) automatically creates new node pools with the optimal machine type to satisfy pending pod resource requests, and deletes node pools when they are no longer needed. This eliminates the need to pre-define node pools for every possible workload shape. Cluster Autoscaler scales existing node pools but does not create new pools with different machine types. GKE Autopilot is a fully managed mode where GCP manages the node infrastructure entirely, but you have less control over machine types. VPA adjusts pod resource requests based on usage but does not provision nodes.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company uses Looker Studio to create dashboards for business stakeholders. The dashboards query BigQuery directly and load slowly. Which approach best improves dashboard performance without changing the underlying data model?
options: Enable BigQuery BI Engine reservations to accelerate Looker Studio queries in-memory
Export BigQuery data to Cloud Spanner for faster OLTP query performance
Create BigQuery materialized views for dashboard aggregations
Increase BigQuery slot reservations dedicated to the project
questions.explanation: BigQuery BI Engine is an in-memory analysis service that accelerates Looker Studio and Data Studio queries by caching frequently accessed data in memory. It integrates natively with Looker Studio and can significantly reduce query latency for interactive dashboards. Cloud Spanner is an OLTP database, not optimized for analytical aggregations. Materialized views help but require defining them for each specific dashboard query pattern. Increasing slot reservations speeds up complex queries but doesn't cache data in memory — BI Engine specifically targets sub-second dashboard performance.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your application uses Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL and experiences high read latency during peak hours. The database is write-heavy with a 90% read / 10% write ratio. What is the most effective performance optimization?
options: Upgrade the Cloud SQL instance to a larger machine type with more vCPUs
Add read replicas and distribute read traffic across them using connection pooling
Enable Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL high availability (HA) configuration
Migrate to Cloud Spanner for horizontally scalable reads
questions.explanation: Adding read replicas to Cloud SQL allows read queries to be distributed across multiple replica instances, effectively scaling read throughput horizontally. Using connection pooling (e.g., PgBouncer or Cloud SQL proxy) ensures efficient connection reuse. This directly addresses the 90% read workload. Upgrading to a larger instance scales vertically and helps but has limits and higher cost. High Availability (HA) adds a failover replica for availability, not additional read capacity — HA replicas don't serve reads. Migrating to Cloud Spanner is architecturally complex and expensive, appropriate only when Cloud SQL limits are fully exhausted.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A web application experiences repeated slow database queries for the same user profile data on every request. The data changes only once per day. What caching solution integrates best with GCP and reduces database load?
options: Cloud Bigtable as a caching layer for user profile data
Memorystore for Redis as an in-memory cache with a 24-hour TTL for user profiles
Cloud CDN to cache API responses serving user profile data
BigQuery BI Engine to cache the user profile lookup results
questions.explanation: Memorystore for Redis is a fully managed in-memory cache on GCP ideal for caching application data like user profiles. Setting a TTL of 24 hours ensures cached data is refreshed daily, matching the update frequency. Redis provides sub-millisecond read latency and significantly reduces database load. Cloud Bigtable is a NoSQL wide-column database optimized for high-throughput analytics, not as an application-layer cache. Cloud CDN caches HTTP responses at edge nodes, appropriate for static assets or public API responses, not for per-user authenticated data. BigQuery BI Engine accelerates SQL queries and is not applicable to application-layer caching.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your team needs to understand which BigQuery queries are consuming the most slot-hours to optimize cost allocation. Which approach provides this visibility without custom instrumentation?
options: Enable Cloud Monitoring BigQuery metrics and create a custom dashboard
Query the INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT view to analyze slot usage, bytes scanned, and cost by user and query
Export BigQuery billing data to Cloud Storage and analyze with Dataproc
Use Cloud Trace to instrument BigQuery API calls and measure duration
questions.explanation: BigQuery's INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT (and related views like JOBS_BY_USER, JOBS_BY_FOLDER) provides detailed job-level metadata including slot milliseconds consumed, bytes billed, query text, user email, and job duration without any custom instrumentation. This is the standard GCP-native way to analyze query costs. Cloud Monitoring provides aggregate BigQuery metrics but not per-query breakdown. Exporting billing data to Cloud Storage adds latency and requires Dataproc setup. Cloud Trace instruments application-level calls, not BigQuery's internal resource consumption.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization wants to use the Cloud Billing export for FinOps analysis. Which export type provides the most granular data including SKU-level details, labels, and resource-level cost attribution?
options: Standard usage cost export to BigQuery
Detailed usage cost export to BigQuery
Summary cost export to Cloud Storage as CSV
Pricing export to BigQuery
questions.explanation: The Detailed usage cost export to BigQuery includes all data from the Standard export plus resource-level labels, system labels, and additional resource details at the SKU level, providing the most granular data for FinOps analysis and cost attribution. Standard usage cost export includes project, service, SKU, and label data but lacks some resource-level details. The summary export to Cloud Storage provides aggregated cost data without label or resource detail. The Pricing export shows the pricing catalog (rates) for GCP services, not actual usage costs.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company has a Cloud Storage bucket with Autoclass enabled. Which statement accurately describes how Autoclass manages object storage classes?
options: Autoclass transitions objects to cheaper storage classes based on a schedule you define
Autoclass automatically moves objects to Nearline, Coldline, or Archive based on their access patterns, and transitions them back to Standard when accessed
Autoclass converts objects to a compressed format and stores them in Coldline by default
Autoclass only transitions objects that have not been accessed for more than 90 days to Coldline
questions.explanation: Autoclass is a Cloud Storage feature that automatically transitions objects to less expensive storage classes (Nearline, Coldline, Archive) when they are not accessed, and moves them back to Standard class when they are accessed again. This eliminates the need to manually configure lifecycle rules and handles unpredictable access patterns efficiently. It does not use a user-defined schedule, does not compress objects, and does not have a fixed 90-day threshold — the transitions are based on actual access patterns tracked by GCP.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Select TWO practices that reduce network egress costs in GCP architectures.
options: Deploy application and database VMs in the same GCP region to avoid inter-region egress charges
Use Cloud CDN to serve static content to end users from edge PoPs instead of the origin
Use Cloud Interconnect instead of Cloud VPN for on-premises connectivity to reduce bandwidth costs
Enable VPC Flow Logs on all subnets for real-time egress monitoring
questions.explanation: Deploying application and database in the same region avoids inter-region data transfer charges (traffic within the same region over internal IP is free or minimal cost). Cloud CDN caches content at edge nodes, reducing origin egress to end users and at lower CDN egress rates. Cloud Interconnect provides dedicated bandwidth but does not reduce egress costs compared to internet — it provides predictable pricing and potentially higher bandwidth, not lower egress rates. VPC Flow Logs are for network visibility and troubleshooting, not cost reduction.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization's SRE team wants to define an SLO for an API service. The team measures the percentage of requests that return a successful response within 500ms. What does this measurement represent?
options: Service Level Agreement (SLA)
Service Level Indicator (SLI)
Error Budget
Service Level Objective (SLO)
questions.explanation: A Service Level Indicator (SLI) is a quantitative measure of a service's behavior — in this case, the percentage of requests returning a successful response within 500ms. This is the raw metric being measured. An SLO is the target value for the SLI (e.g., 99.9% of requests must complete successfully within 500ms). An SLA is a contractual agreement with consequences for missing SLOs. The Error Budget is the allowable amount of SLO violations within a window (e.g., 0.1% of requests can fail).
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company wants to use BigQuery partitioned tables for cost optimization. Which partition type is best when the dataset has a natural timestamp column representing event time and queries always filter by event date?
options: Ingestion-time partitioning using _PARTITIONTIME pseudo-column
Column partitioning on the timestamp column representing event time
Integer range partitioning on a numeric event ID
No partitioning; rely on clustering on the timestamp column instead
questions.explanation: Column partitioning on the event timestamp column is the best choice when queries consistently filter by event date and the data has a natural timestamp. It partitions the data based on the actual event time, ensuring partition pruning works correctly even when data arrives late (out of order). Ingestion-time partitioning uses the time the data was loaded into BigQuery, which may differ from event time, causing incorrect partition pruning for historical loads. Integer range partitioning requires a numeric column. Clustering alone does not enable partition pruning — queries still scan all rows even with clustering.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your Compute Engine workloads run predictably from 8am to 8pm on weekdays and are completely idle on weekends. You want to minimize costs. Which approach is most cost-effective?
options: Purchase 1-year Committed Use Discounts for these instances
Use instance scheduling to automatically start and stop instances according to the work schedule
Switch to Spot VMs which are automatically reclaimed when not needed
Enable Sustained Use Discounts by ensuring instances run for the full month
questions.explanation: Instance scheduling (using Cloud Scheduler or the Instance Schedule resource) automatically starts and stops Compute Engine VMs on a defined schedule. Running only 12 hours per weekday (60 hours/week) instead of 168 hours/week saves approximately 64% of compute cost. CUDs are for predictable steady-state workloads — committing to instances that are idle 64% of the time wastes the commitment. Spot VMs can be preempted at any time and are designed for fault-tolerant batch workloads, not scheduled business applications. SUDs require instances to run the full month to maximize the discount.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A team manages a large GKE cluster with multiple node pools. They want to reduce cost by automatically removing underutilized nodes. Which GKE feature should they enable?
options: Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) in Auto mode
Cluster Autoscaler with scale-down enabled and configuring the minimum node count per pool
Node Auto-Provisioning with resource limits defined at the cluster level
GKE usage metering to track and report idle node costs
questions.explanation: The GKE Cluster Autoscaler automatically removes nodes when they are underutilized and their pods can be scheduled on other nodes, reducing the node count down to the configured minimum. Scale-down is enabled by default when Cluster Autoscaler is enabled. VPA adjusts pod resource requests and limits but does not remove nodes. Node Auto-Provisioning is for automatically creating new node pools for pending pods, not specifically for removing underutilized nodes. GKE usage metering reports costs but does not take automated action to reduce them.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your data warehouse team uses BigQuery with on-demand pricing. They observe that monthly costs are unpredictable due to varying query volumes. What change provides predictable monthly BigQuery costs while potentially saving money at scale?
options: Switch to BigQuery flat-rate pricing (slot reservations) and purchase a reservation appropriate for average slot usage
Enable per-user cost controls and quotas to cap individual spending
Migrate to BigQuery Omni to distribute query costs across multiple clouds
Enable BigQuery BI Engine to reduce the number of queries needed
questions.explanation: BigQuery slot reservations (flat-rate pricing) provide a fixed monthly cost regardless of query volume, making costs predictable. If the team's average slot usage justifies the commitment, it can also be more economical than on-demand pricing. Per-user quotas limit individual spending but don't make total costs predictable. BigQuery Omni runs queries on other clouds' data and doesn't address GCP cost predictability. BI Engine accelerates certain queries but doesn't change the billing model or make costs predictable.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Select TWO BigQuery optimization techniques that reduce both cost and query latency when applied to a 5TB table queried by date range and user ID.
options: Partition the table by date column to enable partition pruning on date range queries
Cluster the table by user_id to optimize queries that filter by user
Enable BigQuery reservations with INTERACTIVE priority
Use table snapshots to reduce data processed by queries
questions.explanation: Partitioning by date allows BigQuery to skip partitions outside the query's date range, reducing bytes scanned and cost. Clustering by user_id sorts data within each partition by user, so queries filtering on user_id read fewer blocks. Together, partitioning and clustering are complementary and provide the best cost and performance optimization for this access pattern. BigQuery reservations with INTERACTIVE priority ensures query slots but doesn't reduce bytes scanned or cost. Table snapshots capture a point-in-time copy for backup purposes, not for query optimization.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company runs a Cloud Run service that processes image uploads. Processing takes 45 seconds per image. Users report timeouts. The default Cloud Run request timeout is 60 seconds. What configuration change resolves the timeout issue most efficiently?
options: Increase the Cloud Run service request timeout up to 3600 seconds
Implement an async pattern: have Cloud Run push images to a Pub/Sub topic and process asynchronously with a separate Cloud Run job
Migrate the service to a Compute Engine VM to remove the timeout constraint
Increase Cloud Run concurrency to process multiple images in parallel and reduce per-image time
questions.explanation: The async pattern is the architecturally correct solution: the upload endpoint publishes to Pub/Sub immediately (fast response to the user), and a separate Cloud Run service or job processes images asynchronously. This decouples the slow processing from the HTTP request lifecycle. While increasing the timeout to 3600s (option A) technically resolves the timeout, it keeps the user waiting synchronously for 45 seconds — a poor UX. Migrating to Compute Engine adds operational overhead. Increasing concurrency allows more simultaneous requests but doesn't reduce per-image processing time.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your company needs a business intelligence platform that allows analysts to explore and share data insights from BigQuery without writing SQL. Which GCP-native or GCP-integrated tool is most appropriate?
options: Looker (now part of Google Cloud) for governed self-service analytics with LookML modeling
Cloud Dataprep by Trifacta for visual data cleaning and transformation
Vertex AI Workbench for Jupyter-based exploratory analysis
Cloud Composer for orchestrating analytical workflows
questions.explanation: Looker (part of Google Cloud) is a modern BI platform that connects directly to BigQuery and allows analysts to explore data, build dashboards, and share insights without writing SQL. LookML, Looker's modeling layer, provides governed semantic definitions. Looker Studio (formerly Data Studio) is the free, lighter-weight option. Cloud Dataprep is for data preparation and wrangling, not BI dashboards. Vertex AI Workbench is for data scientists writing code. Cloud Composer is a managed Apache Airflow service for workflow orchestration, not BI.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A GKE workload has a memory leak and pods crash after a few hours. You want to automatically restart pods when memory exceeds a threshold before the crash, without manual intervention. What is the best approach?
options: Configure Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) to automatically increase memory limits as usage grows
Set resource limits on the pod; when memory exceeds the limit, Kubernetes OOMKills and restarts the pod automatically
Use Cloud Monitoring to create an alerting policy that pages an operator when memory is high
Enable GKE node auto-repair to replace nodes when pods crash due to memory issues
questions.explanation: Setting a memory limit on the pod container causes Kubernetes to send an OOMKill signal when the container exceeds its limit, terminating and restarting it automatically. This prevents the pod from consuming all node memory and crashing other pods. Combined with the deployment's restart policy, the pod restarts without manual intervention. VPA increases limits but won't help a memory leak — it just delays the crash. A monitoring alert notifies humans but requires manual action. Node auto-repair replaces unhealthy nodes, not individual crashing pods.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization wants to identify which GCP projects are generating the most cost and attribute them to teams. Without restructuring the resource hierarchy, what mechanism provides the best cost attribution?
options: Use Cloud Billing sub-accounts, one per team, with projects assigned to each sub-account
Apply consistent resource labels (team: <team-name>) to all resources and analyze labeled costs in BigQuery billing export
Use IAM policies to restrict each team to only their project resources
Enable Cloud Asset Inventory to track all resources per project
questions.explanation: Resource labels propagate to billing export data in BigQuery. Consistently applying a 'team' label to all GCP resources allows filtering and grouping by team in the detailed billing export, providing cost attribution without changing the resource hierarchy. Cloud Billing sub-accounts require projects to be moved under different billing accounts — a structural change. IAM policies control access, not cost attribution. Cloud Asset Inventory tracks resource existence and configuration, not cost.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A development team frequently creates GCE instances and GKE clusters for testing but forgets to delete them, accumulating significant monthly costs. What automated mechanism reduces this waste with minimal developer friction?
options: Mandate that all resources have a 'delete-on' label and run a daily Cloud Function to clean up expired resources
Use Cloud Billing budget alerts to notify the team when costs exceed a threshold
Implement an Organization Policy that prevents resource creation without an expiry label, combined with a cleanup Cloud Function
Require all test resources to be created via Terraform so they can be tracked
questions.explanation: Combining an Organization Policy (custom constraint requiring an expiry label) with an automated Cloud Function that scans for resources past their expiry date provides enforcement at creation time and automated cleanup. This removes the dependency on developer discipline. Simply mandating labels (option A) doesn't enforce the requirement at creation time — developers can skip it. Budget alerts notify about cost overruns but don't clean up resources. Requiring Terraform improves tracking but doesn't force deletion of forgotten test resources.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your application's Pub/Sub subscription has a growing backlog because the consumer service can't process messages fast enough. What autoscaling approach allows the MIG consumer to scale based on Pub/Sub backlog?
options: Configure MIG autoscaling using CPU utilization as the scaling metric
Configure MIG autoscaling with a custom Cloud Monitoring metric based on Pub/Sub subscription/num_undelivered_messages
Manually adjust the MIG target size based on daily backlog reports
Switch to Cloud Run with Pub/Sub push subscription so Cloud Run scales automatically
questions.explanation: MIG autoscaler supports Cloud Monitoring metrics as custom autoscaling signals. The Pub/Sub metric pubsub.googleapis.com/subscription/num_undelivered_messages represents the backlog depth and is natively available in Cloud Monitoring. Configuring the MIG autoscaler to target a specific per-instance backlog (e.g., scale to keep backlog per instance below 1000 messages) is the standard GCP pattern for queue-based autoscaling. CPU utilization is reactive and may not correlate well with processing backlog. Manual adjustment is not automated. Cloud Run with push subscriptions is a valid alternative but changes the architecture significantly.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company wants to reduce toil in their operations team. According to SRE principles, which activity is classified as toil that should be reduced?
options: Designing and implementing a new feature for the platform
Manually restarting a Cloud SQL instance every night because a known bug causes it to stop accepting connections
Writing a blameless postmortem after a production incident
Conducting quarterly disaster recovery drills
questions.explanation: Toil is repetitive, manual, automatable work that scales linearly with service growth and has no lasting value. Manually restarting a Cloud SQL instance every night is toil: it's repetitive, manual, triggered by a known failure mode, and provides no lasting improvement. The correct action is to automate the restart (or fix the bug). Designing new features is engineering work. Writing postmortems is valuable engineering/process work with lasting value. DR drills are planned reliability exercises with lasting value.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your team runs a GKE Autopilot cluster with workloads that have highly variable resource needs. How does GKE Autopilot billing differ from GKE Standard billing?
options: Autopilot bills per node-hour based on the largest node in the cluster
Autopilot bills per pod resource requests (vCPU and memory) consumed, not per node
Autopilot bills per API call made to the Kubernetes control plane
Autopilot billing is identical to Standard GKE; costs are per node-hour
questions.explanation: GKE Autopilot charges based on the vCPU, memory, and storage requested by pods, not for the underlying nodes. You pay for the resources your workloads actually request, and Google manages the node infrastructure. This eliminates cost waste from unused node capacity. In GKE Standard, you pay for node instances (whether pods use all their capacity or not) plus the cluster management fee. Autopilot does not bill per API call or per node-hour.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Select TWO characteristics that make a workload suitable for Spot VMs in GCP.
options: The workload can be interrupted and restarted without data loss (fault-tolerant)
The workload requires strict SLA uptime guarantees of 99.9%
The workload is a batch processing job that can be checkpointed
The workload is a primary database with active client connections
questions.explanation: Spot VMs can be preempted by GCP with a 30-second warning when capacity is needed. They are suitable for fault-tolerant workloads (option A) that can handle interruption gracefully, and batch jobs with checkpointing (option C) that can resume from the last checkpoint after restart. Workloads requiring strict SLA uptime guarantees (option B) cannot use Spot VMs because preemptions would violate those guarantees. Primary databases with active connections (option D) would cause client errors and data consistency issues upon preemption.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company's cloud costs increased 40% last quarter without a corresponding increase in business value. The architect is asked to identify optimization opportunities. Which combination of GCP tools provides the most comprehensive cost visibility?
options: Cloud Billing Console cost breakdown charts and budget alerts
Detailed billing export to BigQuery analyzed with Looker Studio dashboards, combined with Google Cloud Recommender for actionable recommendations
Cloud Asset Inventory export to Cloud Storage analyzed with Dataproc jobs
Cloud Monitoring cost metrics aggregated per project
questions.explanation: The combination of detailed billing export to BigQuery (granular cost data with labels, SKUs, and resources) analyzed via Looker Studio (visual dashboards for trend analysis and attribution) and Google Cloud Recommender (actionable idle VM, right-sizing, and CUD recommendations) provides comprehensive cost visibility and actionable insights. The Billing Console has basic charts but limited analytical depth. Cloud Asset Inventory tracks resource configuration, not costs. Cloud Monitoring has some cost-related metrics but is not designed for comprehensive FinOps analysis.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your company needs to run analytics on streaming data from IoT sensors in BigQuery as soon as it arrives. Which ingestion method provides the lowest latency path from sensor data to BigQuery queryability?
options: Cloud Storage HMAC key-authenticated batch upload every 5 minutes, then BigQuery Data Transfer Service
Pub/Sub → Dataflow streaming pipeline → BigQuery Storage Write API (default stream)
Pub/Sub → Cloud Function → BigQuery insertAll streaming API
Pub/Sub → Dataflow streaming pipeline → BigQuery Storage Write API (committed stream)
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery Storage Write API (default stream) provides data availability in seconds. The default stream writes data immediately and makes it queryable without buffering. Cloud Function + insertAll streaming API (option C) has higher cost and less control over exactly-once delivery. The committed stream (option D) provides exactly-once semantics with slightly higher latency due to commit overhead. Batch upload every 5 minutes (option A) has 5+ minute latency, violating the low-latency requirement.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A team wants to optimize their Cloud Spanner database costs. The database has predictable usage patterns with high read volume and low write volume. What pricing model reduces Spanner costs for this workload?
options: Reduce the number of Spanner nodes to the minimum required and rely on autoscaling
Use Spanner autoscaling to automatically adjust processing units based on CPU and storage utilization
Switch to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL which has lower per-unit cost
Use Spanner multi-region configuration to enable global reads at no extra cost
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner supports autoscaling where processing units scale automatically between configured minimum and maximum values based on CPU utilization and storage usage. For workloads with predictable but varying patterns, autoscaling ensures you pay for what you need rather than over-provisioning. This is directly billed by processing unit-hours consumed. Manually minimizing nodes risks under-provisioning. Switching to Cloud SQL is a significant architectural change. Multi-region Spanner is more expensive, not cheaper — it adds replica costs.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization has a mix of development, staging, and production environments all in the same GCP project. Finance needs to separately track spend per environment. What is the correct approach?
options: Create separate billing accounts for each environment
Apply an 'environment' resource label to all resources and use labeled billing export analysis in BigQuery
Use IAM conditions to restrict resource creation per environment
Move each environment to a separate folder with folder-level billing reports
questions.explanation: Resource labels with an 'environment' key (values: dev, staging, prod) applied to all resources enable cost attribution per environment in the detailed billing export. This is the most practical solution when environments share a project. Creating separate billing accounts requires separate project assignments and is typically for organizational, not just attribution, purposes. IAM conditions restrict access, not cost tracking. Moving to separate folders (and projects) would be architecturally cleaner for isolation but is a major refactoring effort not required just for cost attribution.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A Dataflow streaming job processes clickstream events but the per-worker CPU utilization is consistently below 30%. The team wants to reduce costs. What is the most appropriate optimization?
options: Switch from streaming to batch processing to use cheaper standard workers
Reduce the maximum number of workers in the Dataflow autoscaling configuration
Enable Dataflow Streaming Engine and optimize pipeline fusion to reduce worker count
Move the pipeline to Dataproc which has lower per-worker cost
questions.explanation: Dataflow Streaming Engine offloads streaming state and shuffle from workers to a managed backend, significantly reducing the number of workers needed. Pipeline fusion optimization reduces the number of distributed steps, consolidating work per worker. Together these can dramatically lower worker count and cost. Reducing the max workers cap (option B) alone may cause processing lag. Switching to batch changes the architecture and loses real-time capability. Dataproc on Spark requires significant code rewrite and may not be cheaper for streaming workloads.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company processes financial transactions and needs to ensure database queries complete within 100ms at the 99th percentile. Which GCP database service is best suited to guarantee this latency requirement at scale?
options: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with read replicas
Cloud Spanner with multi-region configuration
Cloud Bigtable for low-latency reads and writes
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL with columnar engine
questions.explanation: Cloud Bigtable is designed for consistent single-digit millisecond latency at petabyte scale for high-throughput read/write workloads. It guarantees sub-10ms p99 latency for typical workloads, far exceeding the 100ms requirement. This makes it ideal for financial transaction processing at scale. Cloud SQL with read replicas can achieve low latency but has scaling limits and doesn't guarantee p99 latency at very high throughput. Cloud Spanner provides strong consistency and scales globally but adds latency for cross-region operations. AlloyDB is excellent for OLTP but has higher latency than Bigtable for simple key-value lookups.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your team wants to reduce operational overhead of managing BigQuery table lifecycle. Data older than 365 days is no longer needed for analysis but must be retained for compliance for 7 years. What is the most operationally efficient solution?
options: Write a Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Function job to delete rows older than 365 days and export them to Coldline Cloud Storage
Set a 365-day partition expiration on the BigQuery table; configure a Dataflow job to export data older than 365 days to a Cloud Storage Archive bucket with a 7-year retention policy
Use BigQuery table snapshots to preserve older data and set expiry on snapshots
Configure BigQuery long-term storage pricing which automatically applies to data not queried for 90 days
questions.explanation: Setting partition expiration automatically deletes BigQuery partitions after 365 days, reducing storage costs without manual intervention. The Dataflow export pipeline moves data to Archive class Cloud Storage (lowest cost, 7-year retention lock with Object Retention Lock or Bucket Lock), satisfying the compliance requirement. This is fully automated and managed. Option A requires maintaining a custom Cloud Function to delete rows and manage exports. Table snapshots preserve BigQuery-formatted data at higher cost than Cloud Storage Archive. Long-term storage pricing is a discount for cold BigQuery data, not a lifecycle management solution.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company wants to implement SLO-based alerting using Cloud Monitoring. They have defined a 99.9% availability SLO over a 30-day rolling window. Which alerting approach is recommended by Google SRE for detecting SLO burn rate issues early?
options: Alert when error rate exceeds 0.1% in any 1-minute window
Alert using multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting: high burn rate over short windows and medium burn rate over longer windows
Alert when the 30-day error budget is 50% consumed
Alert when uptime checks fail for more than 5 consecutive minutes
questions.explanation: Multi-window multi-burn-rate alerting, as recommended in Google's SRE Workbook, combines fast detection (short window, high burn rate for critical incidents) with precision (long window, lower burn rate to reduce false positives). For example: page immediately if burning budget at 14x rate over 1 hour AND 1 hour; notify if burning at 6x rate over 6 hours AND 30 minutes. Alerting on any 1-minute spike has too many false positives. Alerting only when 50% budget is consumed is too slow for a 30-day window. Uptime check alerts detect availability issues but don't relate to SLO burn rate.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Select TWO strategies that reduce Cloud Run costs for a service with sporadic, infrequent traffic that receives approximately 100 requests per day.
options: Set min-instances to 0 to allow the service to scale to zero during idle periods
Set concurrency to 1 so each instance handles only one request at a time
Set CPU throttling to enabled (default) so the CPU is throttled when not processing requests
Set min-instances to 5 to pre-warm instances and avoid any cold starts
questions.explanation: Setting min-instances to 0 allows Cloud Run to scale to zero when idle, eliminating costs during the long periods between requests. CPU throttling (enabled by default) means CPU is throttled when not processing requests — you only pay for CPU time during actual request processing. Together these minimize cost for low-traffic services. Setting concurrency to 1 is a configuration option but doesn't reduce cost — it causes more instances to spin up under concurrent load. Setting min-instances to 5 for 100 requests/day is grossly over-provisioned and wasteful.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization is evaluating whether to use Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) or Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs) for a fleet of Compute Engine instances. Which statement is accurate regarding these two discount types?
options: SUDs and CUDs can both be applied simultaneously to the same instance for combined savings
CUDs provide higher discounts but require upfront commitment; SUDs are automatic and apply to the portion of the month instances run
SUDs provide up to 70% discount for instances running the full month, while CUDs provide only 30%
CUDs apply only to N2 machine types; SUDs apply to all machine types including E2
questions.explanation: CUDs require a 1-year or 3-year commitment to specific resources and provide up to 37% (1-year) or 70% (3-year) discount. SUDs are automatically applied based on how long an instance runs within a month — no commitment required — providing up to 30% for instances running the full month. CUDs and SUDs cannot both apply to the same resource; CUD-committed resources are not eligible for SUDs. SUDs provide up to 30%, not 70%. CUDs apply to multiple machine families including N1, N2, C2, and others — not just N2.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A company runs a global application with users in North America, Europe, and Asia. They want to optimize both performance and egress costs. Which architecture best achieves this?
options: Deploy the application in a single us-central1 region and use Cloud CDN for static assets globally
Deploy the application in three regional clusters, use Cloud Load Balancing with geo-routing to route users to the nearest region, and use Cloud CDN for static assets
Use a single Cloud Spanner multi-region instance to serve all data globally
Deploy to Cloud Run with concurrency=1 in each region for maximum isolation
questions.explanation: Deploying to three regional clusters with geo-routing sends users to the nearest region, minimizing latency and reducing cross-region data transfer. Cloud CDN caches static assets at edge PoPs, further reducing origin egress. This balances performance and cost. A single us-central1 region with CDN only helps for static content — dynamic API calls still cross the globe, causing latency. Cloud Spanner multi-region provides global data replication but at high cost and doesn't address compute latency. Cloud Run with concurrency=1 per region is fine for isolation but setting concurrency=1 wastes instances for concurrent requests.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: Your organization wants to enforce cost governance so that no single team can spend more than $10,000/month without approval. Which GCP mechanism enforces this at the infrastructure level?
options: Cloud Billing budget alerts set to $10,000 with email notification to finance
Organization Policy constraints to cap resource creation quotas per project
Cloud Billing programmatic budget notifications that trigger a Cloud Function to disable billing when threshold is exceeded
IAM roles restricted so only senior architects can create expensive resources like TPUs
questions.explanation: Cloud Billing programmatic budget notifications send a Pub/Sub message when budget thresholds are exceeded. A Cloud Function subscribed to this Pub/Sub topic can automatically disable billing for a project when the $10,000 threshold is hit, stopping further resource creation. This is the GCP-native enforcement mechanism for spend caps. Budget alerts only send notifications — they don't stop spending. Organization Policy quota constraints limit resource counts but don't track monetary spend. IAM role restrictions limit who can create resources but don't enforce budget limits.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A BigQuery table is used for interactive dashboards. Queries on the table always filter by `region` and `product_category`. The table has 2TB of data. What optimization provides the best query performance improvement for these access patterns?
options: Partition by region and cluster by product_category
Cluster by region and product_category (without partitioning)
Partition by ingestion time and add a WHERE clause filter on _PARTITIONTIME
Create a BigQuery materialized view pre-aggregating by region and product_category
questions.explanation: Partitioning by region (assuming a manageable number of distinct values) combined with clustering by product_category provides the best optimization: partition pruning eliminates entire partitions not matching the region filter, and clustering sorts rows within partitions by product_category so only relevant blocks are scanned. This reduces both bytes scanned and cost. Clustering alone (option B) helps but cannot prune partitions — all partitions are still scanned. Ingestion-time partitioning doesn't align with the query filter on region. Materialized views are useful but require maintenance and add storage cost.
questions.category: Analyzing and optimizing technical and business processes

questions.question: A team wants to implement CI/CD for a containerized application on GKE using GCP-native services. Which combination of services provides build, image storage, and progressive deployment capabilities?
options: Cloud Build for building images, Container Registry for storage, Cloud Deploy for progressive delivery to GKE
Cloud Build for building images, Artifact Registry for storage, Cloud Deploy for progressive delivery to GKE
Cloud Build for building images, Artifact Registry for storage, Cloud Run for deployment
Jenkins on GKE for building, Artifact Registry for storage, Helm for deployment
questions.explanation: Cloud Build compiles and packages container images. Artifact Registry (the successor to Container Registry) is the recommended service for storing Docker images, supporting vulnerability scanning and cleanup policies. Cloud Deploy provides managed progressive delivery to GKE with canary and blue-green deployment strategies. Container Registry (option A) is the legacy service and Google recommends migrating to Artifact Registry. Cloud Run (option C) is for serverless containers, not GKE. Jenkins (option D) adds operational overhead and is not GCP-native.

questions.question: A company is migrating a legacy monolith to GCP. The application requires minimal code changes, must run on VMs, and the team wants the fastest possible migration. Which of the 6 R migration strategies applies?
options: Refactor/Re-architect — rewrite the application as microservices on GKE
Replatform (lift-and-tinker) — move to Compute Engine with minor optimizations like managed database
Rehost (lift-and-shift) — move the application to Compute Engine VMs without code changes
Repurchase — replace the application with a SaaS equivalent
questions.explanation: Rehosting (lift-and-shift) involves moving the existing application to GCP VMs without code changes, providing the fastest migration path. This directly matches the requirement for minimal code changes and fastest migration. Replatforming involves some optimization (e.g., switching to managed database) but still requires some changes. Refactoring requires significant rearchitecting as microservices — time-consuming. Repurchasing replaces the application with a commercial SaaS product, which may not be feasible for custom applications.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate their on-premises MySQL 8.0 database to Cloud SQL for MySQL with minimal downtime. The database is 500GB. Which migration service enables continuous replication for near-zero downtime cutover?
options: Storage Transfer Service to copy database dump files to Cloud Storage, then restore to Cloud SQL
Database Migration Service (DMS) using continuous data replication (CDC) with a Cloud SQL destination
Datastream to replicate MySQL binlog changes to a Cloud Storage bucket, then load to Cloud SQL
BigQuery Data Transfer Service to migrate the MySQL tables to Cloud SQL
questions.explanation: Database Migration Service (DMS) supports continuous data migration from MySQL to Cloud SQL using Change Data Capture (CDC) via MySQL binlog replication. This keeps the Cloud SQL destination in sync with the source during migration, enabling a near-zero downtime cutover window (stop writes, wait for replication to catch up, switch over). Storage Transfer Service handles file/object storage migration, not database migration. Datastream replicates to BigQuery or Cloud Storage for analytics use cases, not to Cloud SQL. BigQuery Data Transfer Service migrates data into BigQuery, not Cloud SQL.

questions.question: A company has 50TB of on-premises data to migrate to Cloud Storage. Their internet connection is 100Mbps shared with production traffic. How long would a full upload take at 50% of available bandwidth, and what is the recommended alternative?
options: Approximately 23 days; use Storage Transfer Service online transfer
Approximately 23 days; use Transfer Appliance to ship data physically to Google
Approximately 46 days; use Cloud Interconnect to increase bandwidth for the transfer
Approximately 92 days; use a parallel upload with gsutil -m
questions.explanation: At 50Mbps effective bandwidth: 50TB = 400Tb; 400Tb / 50Mbps = ~8,000,000 seconds ≈ 92 days. However, the question option framing reflects the scenario. For datasets larger than roughly 10-20TB where network transfer would take more than a week, Google recommends Transfer Appliance — a physical device shipped to your location, loaded with data, and shipped back to Google for ingest into Cloud Storage. Storage Transfer Service is for online transfers and would still be limited by the same bandwidth. Cloud Interconnect increases bandwidth but adding dedicated circuit takes time and is expensive for a one-time migration.

questions.question: A team manages a GKE cluster and wants to enforce consistent Kubernetes resource configurations (namespaces, RBAC, network policies) across multiple clusters in different environments. Which GCP service enables this GitOps-based policy enforcement?
options: Cloud Build with a trigger that runs kubectl apply on every git push
Anthos Config Management (ACM) which synchronizes configurations from a Git repository to all registered clusters
GKE Config Connector which manages GCP resources as Kubernetes CRDs
Cloud Deployment Manager with Kubernetes resource templates
questions.explanation: Anthos Config Management (ACM) implements GitOps for Kubernetes: it continuously synchronizes the desired state from a Git repository to all registered GKE clusters, enforcing namespace configurations, RBAC policies, network policies, and custom resource definitions. It can also enforce policy constraints using Policy Controller. Cloud Build with kubectl apply works but is not continuous — it only runs on push. Config Connector manages GCP infrastructure (projects, buckets, etc.) as Kubernetes resources, not Kubernetes cluster configurations. Cloud Deployment Manager is an infrastructure provisioning tool, not a continuous config sync solution.

questions.question: A company is performing a blue-green deployment of a new GKE application version. Traffic is currently 100% on the blue deployment. What GKE mechanism allows gradually shifting traffic from blue to green while monitoring error rates?
options: Update the Kubernetes Deployment's image tag and perform a rolling update
Use Cloud Deploy with a canary deployment target that splits traffic using a GKE Gateway or Istio VirtualService
Manually scale down blue pods and scale up green pods simultaneously
Use a Kubernetes CronJob to periodically shift traffic percentages
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy supports canary deployments to GKE by integrating with GKE Gateway API or Istio/Anthos Service Mesh to split traffic by percentage between the current and new version. This allows gradual traffic shifting (e.g., 10% → 50% → 100%) with automated rollback if metrics degrade. Rolling updates change all pods progressively but don't allow traffic splitting — all pods run the new version once updated. Manual scaling is error-prone and doesn't provide controlled traffic splitting. A CronJob is not a traffic management mechanism.

questions.question: Your Cloud Build pipeline builds Docker images and pushes them to Artifact Registry. The pipeline needs database credentials to run integration tests. What is the most secure way to provide these credentials to Cloud Build?
options: Hard-code the credentials in the cloudbuild.yaml file and restrict repository access
Store credentials in a Cloud Build substitution variable defined in the trigger configuration
Store credentials in Secret Manager and reference them in cloudbuild.yaml using the secretEnv or availableSecrets syntax
Encrypt credentials with a KMS key and store them in a Cloud Storage bucket accessible only to the Cloud Build service account
questions.explanation: Cloud Build natively integrates with Secret Manager via the availableSecrets field in cloudbuild.yaml. The Cloud Build service account is granted Secret Manager access, and secrets are injected as environment variables into build steps without appearing in logs or build configuration. Hard-coding credentials in cloudbuild.yaml exposes them in the repository. Substitution variables in trigger configuration store values in the Cloud Build trigger settings — less secure than Secret Manager and not recommended for secrets. KMS encryption in Cloud Storage is a viable but more complex alternative to the native Secret Manager integration.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate their PostgreSQL database from on-premises to AlloyDB for PostgreSQL using continuous replication. Which GCP service supports this migration path?
options: Database Migration Service (DMS) with AlloyDB as the destination
Datastream with AlloyDB destination connector
Storage Transfer Service with PostgreSQL dump format
BigQuery Data Transfer Service PostgreSQL connector
questions.explanation: Database Migration Service (DMS) supports migration from PostgreSQL (on-premises, Cloud SQL, or other clouds) to AlloyDB for PostgreSQL using continuous replication via PostgreSQL logical replication. This enables near-zero downtime migrations. Datastream supports PostgreSQL as a source but writes to Cloud Storage or BigQuery, not to AlloyDB (it can be used as a component in a migration pattern but DMS is the direct tool). Storage Transfer Service handles file/object storage. BigQuery Data Transfer Service is for ingesting data into BigQuery.

questions.question: Select TWO benefits of using Workload Identity for GKE pods instead of service account key files.
options: Pods can authenticate to GCP services using the Kubernetes service account identity mapped to a GCP IAM service account
Service account key files are automatically rotated every 24 hours with Workload Identity
Workload Identity eliminates the need to create, manage, distribute, and rotate service account key JSON files
Workload Identity provides faster API call performance than key-based authentication
questions.explanation: Workload Identity maps a Kubernetes Service Account (KSA) to a GCP IAM Service Account (GSA), allowing pods to automatically obtain short-lived tokens for GCP API authentication (option A). This eliminates the need to create long-lived service account key JSON files, distribute them to pods as secrets, and manually rotate them (option C) — eliminating a significant security risk. Key files are not automatically rotated with Workload Identity; Workload Identity replaces them with short-lived tokens obtained via the metadata server. Workload Identity does not inherently improve API call speed.

questions.question: A team uses Terraform to manage GCP infrastructure. They want multiple developers to work on different features simultaneously without interfering with each other's state. Which Terraform feature should they use?
options: Terraform modules to separate infrastructure into reusable components
Terraform workspaces to maintain separate state files for each developer or feature branch
Terraform remote state in Cloud Storage with state locking via Cloud Firestore
Multiple Terraform Cloud accounts, one per developer
questions.explanation: Terraform workspaces allow multiple state files within the same configuration directory, enabling separate infrastructure stacks per developer or environment. Each workspace has its own state file, so changes in one workspace don't affect others. Modules provide reusable configuration components but share state within a workspace. Remote state in Cloud Storage (option C) is important for team collaboration and state locking, but without workspaces all developers share the same state. Separate Terraform Cloud accounts is not a standard pattern for this use case.

questions.question: A company wants to implement progressive delivery for a Cloud Run service, shifting 10% of traffic to a new revision initially, then 100% after validation. How is this configured in Cloud Run?
options: Deploy the new revision and configure Cloud Load Balancing weights to split traffic
Use Cloud Run traffic splitting to assign traffic percentages to named revisions
Configure a Cloud Endpoints canary routing rule for the service
Use Istio VirtualService traffic weights in the Cloud Run networking configuration
questions.explanation: Cloud Run has native traffic splitting: you can deploy a new revision (without immediately routing traffic to it using --no-traffic) and then configure traffic percentages across named revisions (e.g., 90% to current, 10% to new). After validation, you can shift 100% to the new revision. This is built into Cloud Run and requires no additional services. Cloud Load Balancing integrates with Cloud Run but traffic splitting is configured at the Cloud Run service level, not the load balancer. Cloud Endpoints is an API management layer. Istio is not applicable to managed Cloud Run.

questions.question: A GKE cluster must be upgraded from Kubernetes 1.27 to 1.29. The cluster runs stateful workloads with strict availability requirements. Which upgrade strategy minimizes application downtime?
options: In-place rolling upgrade of all nodes simultaneously
Blue-green node pool upgrade: create new 1.29 node pool, cordon and drain old nodes, delete old pool
Surge upgrade with maxSurge=1 and maxUnavailable=0 on each node pool
Enable GKE auto-upgrade and let GKE handle the upgrade automatically
questions.explanation: Blue-green node pool upgrade creates a new node pool running the target Kubernetes version, migrates workloads by cordoning and draining old nodes (so pods reschedule onto new nodes gracefully), and then deletes the old pool. This provides the most control and minimizes disruption for stateful workloads. Surge upgrade (option C) with maxUnavailable=0 is safer than default but upgrades nodes in-place one at a time — acceptable but riskier for stateful workloads. Simultaneous upgrade of all nodes causes massive disruption. Auto-upgrade is convenient but gives less control over timing and strategy for critical stateful workloads.

questions.question: A company is migrating a large data warehouse from on-premises Hadoop/HDFS to GCP. The data is 200TB. Which migration strategy and tool combination is most appropriate?
options: Use gsutil rsync to copy all HDFS files to Cloud Storage, then run BigQuery Data Transfer
Use Storage Transfer Service with a self-hosted transfer agent to copy from HDFS to Cloud Storage, then load into BigQuery
Use Transfer Appliance to physically ship HDFS data to Google, then ingest into BigQuery
Use Datastream to replicate HDFS data continuously to BigQuery
questions.explanation: Storage Transfer Service supports HDFS as a source via a self-hosted transfer agent installed in the Hadoop cluster. This enables managed, resumable transfer of 200TB from HDFS to Cloud Storage without requiring physical shipment. Once in Cloud Storage, data can be loaded into BigQuery. gsutil rsync directly from HDFS is possible but not managed or resumable at scale. Transfer Appliance is appropriate for offline migration when network transfer is too slow, but 200TB over a decent corporate network connection is feasible online. Datastream is for database CDC replication, not HDFS file transfer.

questions.question: A company uses Artifact Registry for Docker images and wants to automatically delete images that are older than 30 days and untagged. What feature enables this without custom scripts?
options: Cloud Storage lifecycle rules applied to the Artifact Registry bucket
Artifact Registry cleanup policies configured to delete untagged images older than 30 days
Cloud Scheduler job that calls the Artifact Registry API to delete old images
Container Analysis vulnerability scanning with automatic deletion of vulnerable images
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry supports cleanup policies that can automatically delete artifacts based on conditions such as tag status (untagged), age, keep the most recent N versions, or a combination. Configuring a policy to delete untagged images older than 30 days handles this without custom scripts. Cloud Storage lifecycle rules apply to Cloud Storage buckets, not to Artifact Registry repositories. Cloud Scheduler with API calls is a custom solution requiring maintenance. Container Analysis vulnerability scanning identifies vulnerabilities but does not automatically delete images based on age or tag status.

questions.question: A company operates Kubernetes clusters both on-premises and in GKE. They want a single control plane to manage policies, service mesh, and compliance across all clusters. Which GCP product provides this capability?
options: GKE Hub for registering clusters with a unified management interface
Anthos, which provides a unified platform for hybrid and multicloud Kubernetes management including Config Management and Service Mesh
Cloud Operations Suite with GKE monitoring dashboards for all clusters
Cloud Deployment Manager with Kubernetes resource templates applied to all clusters
questions.explanation: Anthos is Google's hybrid and multicloud platform that enables unified management of Kubernetes clusters anywhere (on-premises, GKE, other clouds). It includes Anthos Config Management for GitOps-based policy enforcement, Anthos Service Mesh for traffic management and observability, and Anthos clusters for running GKE on-premises or on other clouds. GKE Hub is the registration mechanism that underpins Anthos. Cloud Operations Suite provides monitoring but not policy enforcement or service mesh. Cloud Deployment Manager is a GCP-specific IaC tool, not a multi-cluster management platform.
