These logs are retained for 400 days by default and can be exported for longer retention. Cloud Monitoring tracks metrics, not configuration change history. Cloud Asset Inventory provides point-in-time resource state snapshots and change history, but Admin Audit Logs are the primary compliance audit trail. VPC Flow Logs record network traffic, not resource configuration changes.

questions.question: Your GKE cluster has a horizontal pod autoscaler (HPA) configured to scale between 2 and 20 replicas based on CPU utilization. During a load test, the HPA doesn't scale up fast enough. What HPA configuration change improves scale-up responsiveness?
options: Increase the HPA's maxReplicas to 50 to allow faster scaling
Reduce the HPA stabilization window for scale-up and increase the scale-up rate in the behavior configuration
Switch from CPU-based to custom metric-based HPA using Pub/Sub queue depth
Enable the Cluster Autoscaler to provision new nodes more quickly
questions.explanation: HPA scaling behavior can be configured with the spec.behavior field, which controls stabilization windows and scaling policies. Reducing the scale-up stabilization window (default 0 seconds for scale-up but can affect responsiveness) and increasing the periodSeconds and value for scale-up policies allows faster pod addition. The default HPA scale-up rate may be conservative. Increasing maxReplicas sets the ceiling but doesn't change how fast scaling occurs. Switching metrics changes what the HPA responds to but not the scale-up speed. Cluster Autoscaler provisions nodes (required for pod scheduling) but HPA must first request more pods.

questions.question: Select TWO characteristics that distinguish Cloud Spanner backups from Cloud SQL backups.
options: Cloud Spanner backups are strongly consistent across all nodes at the time of backup creation
Cloud Spanner backups can be restored to a different Spanner instance in any region globally
Cloud Spanner supports cross-region backup replication automatically like Cloud SQL HA
Cloud Spanner backups are incremental and stored in the same region as the source instance by default
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner backups are strongly consistent, capturing a consistent state across all nodes at a specific version timestamp (option A) — this is a distinguishing feature given Spanner's distributed nature. Spanner backups are full backups stored in the same region as the source instance by default, not automatically replicated cross-region (option D). Spanner backup restores can be to a different instance but must be in the same project and the restored instance's region must be compatible with the backup configuration — they cannot be freely restored to any global region (option B is incorrect). Spanner does not have cross-region backup replication like Cloud SQL's HA read replica.

questions.question: A company wants to track SLO compliance over time and generate monthly reports showing what percentage of time each service met its SLO. Which Cloud Operations feature supports SLO tracking natively?
options: Cloud Monitoring Service Monitoring with SLO creation and error budget burn tracking
BigQuery with a custom query on Cloud Monitoring metric export data
Cloud Logging with log-based metrics tracking SLO compliance events
Cloud Trace with latency percentile tracking per service
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring Service Monitoring allows defining services, creating SLOs based on availability or latency metrics, and tracking error budget burn rates. It provides built-in SLO compliance views showing good minutes, bad minutes, and error budget consumption over the defined rolling window. Monthly compliance reports can be generated from this data. BigQuery with metric export can provide historical analysis but requires custom setup. Log-based metrics with manual SLO compliance tracking is a custom solution. Cloud Trace provides latency data but doesn't provide SLO tracking dashboards.

questions.question: A company's operations team receives incident alerts but struggles to respond efficiently because runbooks are outdated and scattered across wikis. What SRE practice addresses this?
options: Implement automated rollback for all deployments to eliminate the need for runbooks
Maintain up-to-date runbooks as code in a version-controlled repository, with clear steps for each alert type and links from alert notifications to the relevant runbook
Reduce the number of alerts by increasing all alerting thresholds to eliminate runbook-requiring incidents
Hire more SREs to handle the volume of incidents requiring runbook execution
questions.explanation: Effective runbooks are version-controlled, regularly updated, and directly linked from alert notifications so responders can immediately access the correct procedure during an incident. Storing runbooks as code (e.g., in a Git repository as Markdown) ensures they are reviewed, updated, and have change history. Alert descriptions in Cloud Monitoring can include documentation links. Automated rollback helps for deployment issues but doesn't address all incident types. Raising thresholds reduces alert volume but also reduces detection sensitivity — it doesn't improve response quality. More SREs can help capacity but not runbook quality.

questions.question: A company's GKE application uses persistent volumes backed by Persistent Disks. They want to back up these volumes to protect against data loss. What GCP-native mechanism provides backup for GKE persistent volumes?
options: Enable Cloud Storage FUSE to replicate persistent disk data to a GCS bucket
Use Backup for GKE which creates application-consistent backups of GKE workloads including persistent volume data
Create Persistent Disk snapshots via Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Functions on a schedule
Use Velero with Cloud Storage backend for GKE volume backup and restore
questions.explanation: Backup for GKE is the GCP-native managed backup solution for GKE workloads. It creates backups of Kubernetes resources (workload manifests, config maps, secrets) alongside the associated Persistent Disk data using disk snapshots, providing application-consistent backups. It supports scheduled backup plans, retention policies, and one-click restore. Persistent Disk snapshot scheduling (option C) via Cloud Scheduler works but is not GKE-aware — it doesn't capture Kubernetes resource state. Cloud Storage FUSE provides filesystem access, not backup. Velero is a valid open-source option but requires additional configuration versus the managed Backup for GKE service.

questions.question: An organization wants to measure Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) for production incidents. Which definition of MTTR aligns with DORA metrics?
options: The average time between the start of an incident and the deployment of the fix to production
The average time between a service disruption beginning and the service being restored to full operation
The average time between detecting an alert and acknowledging it in the on-call system
The average time between consecutive production incidents
questions.explanation: DORA's MTTR (Mean Time to Restore) measures the time from when a service disruption begins (or is detected) to when it is fully restored to normal operation. This captures the full incident response cycle. Option A describes time to fix deployment, which is part of MTTR but not the complete measure. Option C is mean time to acknowledge (MTTA), a different metric. Option D describes Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), not MTTR.

questions.question: Select TWO capabilities provided by Google Cloud's Operations Suite (formerly Stackdriver) that help with GKE application observability.
options: GKE system metrics and workload metrics automatically collected by the Cloud Ops Agent on nodes
Pre-built Kubernetes monitoring dashboards in Cloud Monitoring showing cluster, node, namespace, and workload metrics
Automatic code-level performance fixes applied to GKE workloads when profiling identifies bottlenecks
Automatic security patch application to GKE node OS when vulnerabilities are detected
questions.explanation: Cloud Operations Suite provides Google Kubernetes Engine metrics integration: GKE system metrics (CPU, memory, network per pod/container/node) are automatically collected and sent to Cloud Monitoring when GKE metrics are enabled (option A). Cloud Monitoring includes pre-built GKE dashboards showing cluster health, node utilization, namespace resource usage, and workload performance (option B). Neither automatic code fixes from profiling nor automatic OS patching are provided by Cloud Operations — those are separate concerns handled by Cloud Profiler (profiling only, no fixes) and GKE node auto-upgrade/auto-repair.

questions.question: A company wants to implement capacity planning for their GKE cluster. They need to forecast resource requirements 3 months ahead based on historical usage trends. Which approach best supports this?
options: Review GKE cluster autoscaler logs to predict future node requirements
Export GKE metrics to BigQuery via Cloud Monitoring metric export, then use BQML or Looker to forecast future CPU and memory demand based on historical trends
Use Cloud Spanner to store cluster metrics and run SQL forecasting queries
Enable GKE Node Auto-Provisioning which automatically predicts and provisions capacity
questions.explanation: Exporting Cloud Monitoring GKE metrics to BigQuery (via metric export or Monitoring API) enables historical analysis. BigQuery ML (BQML) provides time-series forecasting capabilities (ARIMA_PLUS model) that can project future CPU and memory demand based on historical trends, enabling data-driven capacity planning. Looker can visualize trends and projections. Autoscaler logs show past scaling events but don't provide forecasting. Cloud Spanner storing metrics is a custom data warehouse solution requiring significant setup. Node Auto-Provisioning reacts to current demand — it doesn't forecast future requirements.

questions.question: A company's error budget for a critical service is being consumed faster than expected. During the on-call engineer's investigation, they discover the errors began after a recent schema migration. What SRE principle should guide the next action?
options: Continue collecting data for 24 hours before taking action to avoid unnecessary rollbacks
Immediately roll back the schema migration to stop error budget consumption and restore service, then investigate the root cause safely
Increase the SLO error budget threshold to give more time for the migration to stabilize
Alert the database team and wait for their approval before taking any action
questions.explanation: When a clear causal relationship exists between a recent change and active error budget burn, the priority is to restore service by reverting the change (rollback). SRE principles prioritize reducing MTTR — every minute of active degradation consumes error budget and impacts users. Rollback first, investigate second is the correct sequence. Waiting 24 hours extends the incident and user impact. Expanding the error budget doesn't fix the underlying issue. Waiting for database team approval while errors burn introduces unnecessary delay — the on-call engineer should have the authority to act on rollback for availability restoration.

questions.question: Your organization wants to implement a third-party SIEM solution (like Splunk or Datadog) that processes GCP audit logs for security monitoring. What is the recommended GCP integration pattern?
options: Install the SIEM agent on each Compute Engine VM to collect logs locally
Configure a Cloud Logging log sink to export audit logs to a Pub/Sub topic; configure the SIEM to subscribe to the Pub/Sub topic for real-time log ingestion
Export audit logs to BigQuery and configure the SIEM to query BigQuery hourly
Use the Cloud Logging API from the SIEM to poll for new log entries every minute
questions.explanation: The recommended GCP-native pattern for SIEM integration is: Cloud Logging log sink → Pub/Sub topic → SIEM subscription. Log sinks in Cloud Logging stream matching log entries to Pub/Sub in near-real-time. The SIEM subscribes to the Pub/Sub topic and ingests logs for analysis. This is push-based (real-time), scalable, and supported by all major SIEMs (Splunk, Datadog, QRadar, Sentinel). Installing agents on each VM is not scalable and misses non-VM resources. BigQuery export is for analytics, not real-time SIEM ingestion. Polling the Logging API adds latency and is less efficient than the Pub/Sub push model.

questions.question: A company uses a Managed Instance Group for a stateless web application. They want to perform a rolling update to a new image with zero downtime, allowing only 1 instance to be updated at a time. How is this configured?
options: Set maxSurge=0 and maxUnavailable=1 in the MIG update policy
Set maxSurge=1 and maxUnavailable=0 in the MIG update policy
Set maxSurge=0 and maxUnavailable=0 to pause all updates
Delete the old MIG and create a new MIG with the updated image template
questions.explanation: maxSurge=1 allows one additional instance to be created above the target size (the new version), maxUnavailable=0 ensures no currently healthy instances are taken down until a new one is ready. This means: create new instance (now target+1), wait for it to be healthy, remove an old instance (back to target). This ensures zero downtime because at no point is the healthy capacity reduced below the target. maxUnavailable=1 with maxSurge=0 takes down one old instance before creating a new one — causes brief capacity reduction. maxSurge=0, maxUnavailable=0 effectively pauses the update. Deleting and recreating causes significant downtime.

questions.question: Select TWO best practices for implementing on-call rotation for a 24/7 production service using GCP's operations tools.
options: Configure Cloud Monitoring alerting policies with PagerDuty or Opsgenie notification channels for automated on-call routing and escalation
Create separate alert notification channels for different severity levels, routing critical alerts to pager and warning alerts to email/Slack
Disable all non-critical alerts during business hours to reduce daytime interruptions
Configure a single alert notification channel for all alerts regardless of severity
questions.explanation: Integrating Cloud Monitoring with PagerDuty or Opsgenie (option A) provides managed on-call scheduling, rotation, and escalation policies — ensuring the right person is paged at the right time. Tiered notification channels by severity (option B) ensures critical alerts page the on-call engineer while warnings go to lower-priority channels (Slack, email) that can be reviewed asynchronously. Disabling alerts during business hours increases the risk of missing daytime incidents. A single notification channel for all alerts regardless of severity creates alert fatigue and makes all alerts feel equally urgent.

questions.question: A company wants to enable load testing for their production-like environment on GCP to validate capacity and performance before major traffic events. Which GCP-integrated tool provides managed distributed load testing?
options: Cloud Spanner load testing using the benchmark workload generator
Cloud Load Testing (formerly Distributed Cloud Load Testing on GCP) using Locust or JMeter on Compute Engine or GKE
Cloud Monitoring load simulation using synthetic monitor tests
Cloud Armor rate limiting tests using the WAF policy simulator
questions.explanation: GCP supports distributed load testing by running load testing frameworks like Locust, JMeter, or k6 on Compute Engine VMs or GKE clusters, scaling the number of load generators as needed. GCP provides reference architectures and tutorials for distributed load testing. Cloud Spanner has its own benchmark but this is for database benchmarking, not application load testing. Cloud Monitoring synthetic monitors test individual endpoints for availability, not high-throughput load. Cloud Armor rate limiting controls traffic, it's not a load testing tool.

questions.question: A company's Cloud Run service must comply with regulations requiring all logs containing PII to be automatically detected and redacted before storage in Cloud Logging. Which solution achieves this?
options: Configure a Cloud Logging exclusion filter to delete all logs from the Cloud Run service
Implement application-level PII removal in the logging code before log entries are written
Use Cloud DLP (Data Loss Prevention) API integrated into a Cloud Logging log sink processing pipeline via Pub/Sub and Cloud Functions to inspect and de-identify logs before storing them
Enable Cloud Logging data access controls to restrict who can view PII in logs
questions.explanation: The recommended pattern for PII redaction in logs is: Cloud Logging log sink → Pub/Sub → Cloud Function that calls Cloud DLP API to inspect and de-identify log entries → writes sanitized logs to a separate Cloud Logging bucket or Cloud Storage. This intercepts logs before they are stored in their final form, applying automated DLP de-identification. Exclusion filters delete logs entirely — inappropriate if logs are needed for debugging. Application-level removal is effective but requires code changes in every service. Data access controls restrict who sees logs but don't redact the PII content.

questions.question: An operations team wants to automate remediation when Cloud Monitoring detects a known, well-understood issue (e.g., a specific Cloud SQL metric indicating connection pool exhaustion that requires bouncing a connection pooler). Which GCP pattern implements this automated remediation?
options: Configure the Cloud Monitoring alert to automatically restart Cloud SQL instances when the metric fires
Configure the alerting policy notification channel to send to a Pub/Sub topic; a Cloud Function subscribed to that topic executes the remediation action
Use Cloud Scheduler to periodically restart the connection pooler regardless of whether the issue is detected
Create a Cloud Monitoring policy that directly restarts the connection pooler via the GCE API
questions.explanation: The standard GCP pattern for automated remediation is: Cloud Monitoring alerting policy → Pub/Sub notification channel → Cloud Function (or Cloud Run) that executes the remediation. The alerting policy publishes a notification to Pub/Sub when the condition fires; the Cloud Function receives the notification and calls the appropriate GCP API (restart an instance, scale a service, update a configuration). Cloud Monitoring alerting policies cannot directly restart instances or call arbitrary APIs. Cloud Scheduler-based periodic restarts waste resources and add unnecessary disruption when the issue isn't occurring.

questions.question: Select TWO practices that reduce MTTR (Mean Time to Recover) for production incidents.
options: Maintain pre-approved rollback procedures that engineers can execute without additional approvals during an active incident
Require a full root cause analysis to be completed before any remediation action is taken
Implement feature flags that allow disabling problematic features without a new deployment
Always deploy hotfix patches through the full CI/CD pipeline including all test stages before applying to production
questions.explanation: Pre-approved rollback procedures (option A) reduce MTTR by eliminating approval delays during incidents — engineers can act immediately using established protocols. Feature flags (option C) allow instant disabling of problematic features by flipping a configuration flag without a deployment cycle, dramatically reducing time from detection to mitigation. Requiring complete RCA before remediation (option B) extends the incident duration — the correct SRE approach is to mitigate first, investigate after. Requiring full CI/CD pipeline execution for hotfixes (option D) increases MTTR — hotfixes should have a fast track while balancing quality.

questions.question: A company wants to monitor the performance of their BigQuery queries and receive alerts when a query job takes longer than 5 minutes. What is the correct approach using Cloud Monitoring?
options: Create a Cloud Monitoring uptime check on the BigQuery API endpoint with a 5-minute timeout
Create a log-based metric in Cloud Logging counting BigQuery job completion log entries filtered by duration > 5 minutes, then create an alerting policy on that metric
Query INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT in BigQuery and set up a scheduled query that emails when any job exceeds 5 minutes
Use Cloud Trace to track BigQuery query spans and alert when trace duration exceeds 5 minutes
questions.explanation: BigQuery logs job completion events to Cloud Logging including the job duration. Creating a log-based metric with a filter for completed BigQuery jobs where duration exceeds 300 seconds provides a count of slow jobs. An alerting policy on this metric fires when slow queries are detected. Uptime checks test endpoint availability, not query duration. A scheduled INFORMATION_SCHEMA query sends periodic reports but not real-time alerts. Cloud Trace would require instrumenting BigQuery API calls from the application — BigQuery doesn't automatically generate Cloud Trace spans for internal query execution.

questions.question: A company is designing a multi-region active-active deployment for a web application with a global user base. They want all regions to serve traffic simultaneously, with automatic failover if a region becomes unhealthy. Which GCP load balancing configuration achieves this?
options: Cloud DNS with geolocation routing policies directing users to the nearest region
Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer with backend services in multiple regions, health checks on each regional backend, and traffic automatically distributed to healthy backends
Cloud CDN with origin failover configured between two regional origins
Cloud Armor with a backend security policy routing traffic to the nearest healthy region
questions.explanation: The Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer (now Cloud Load Balancing with the global external application load balancer) uses Google's global network to route user requests to the nearest healthy backend. Backend services span multiple regions; health checks continuously verify regional backend health. If a region fails health checks, the load balancer automatically stops sending traffic to that region and distributes to remaining healthy regions — achieving active-active with automatic failover. Cloud DNS geolocation routing directs users to regions but lacks health-check-based failover and requires DNS TTL expiry for failover (slow). CDN origin failover helps cache misses but isn't a full active-active load balancing solution. Cloud Armor is a WAF/DDoS service, not a load balancer.
